topics.title: Basics of Computer Graphics and Its Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Computer Graphics and Its Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer Graphics (CG)** is a branch of computer science that deals with the **generation, storage, manipulation, and display of pictorial (visual) content** using digital computers. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, it is formally defined as a discipline that encompasses the methodologies, algorithms, and hardware/software systems used to create, render, and interact with images on a display device.

In KTU terminology, Computer Graphics is classified as the science of converting **data → image**, where data may originate from mathematical models, user input, captured photographs, or simulation engines. The field broadly covers **2D Graphics, 3D Graphics, Animation, Visualization, Image Processing, and Multimedia Integration**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Board Exam Standard):**
> *"Computer Graphics is the discipline of generating images with the aid of computers, dealing with the mathematical and computational foundations for the creation, representation, manipulation, and display of visual information."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Computer Graphics as a **"Digital Canvas & Optical Illusion Machine"**. Imagine you are a magician showing a picture to an audience:

- **The Magic Wand (CPU/GPU)** decides what to draw.
- **The Canvas (Frame Buffer)** holds the picture in memory as a grid of tiny colored dots called **pixels**.
- **The Flashlight (Display Device like CRT, LCD, LED)** projects those pixels onto the screen at lightning speed, so fast that your eyes see a smooth, continuous image.

Just as an artist uses brushes, colors, and perspectives, a computer graphics programmer uses **lines, curves, polygons, lighting models, and rendering algorithms** to "paint" pictures — but the canvas is mathematical, the brush is an algorithm, and the audience is the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Computer Graphics (CG)** = *Creating* images from scratch (data → image).
> - **Image Processing (IP)** = *Modifying* existing images (image → image).
> - **Computer Vision (CV)** = *Understanding* images (image → data/meaning).
>
> Students often confuse these three — the KTU examiner deducts marks if they are mixed up.

### 1.3 Physical Standards & Core Metrics

The following standard metrics are used universally in KTU problems:

- **Resolution** = Number of pixels per row × per column (e.g., **1920 × 1080** for Full HD).
- **Aspect Ratio** = Ratio of width to height (e.g., **16:9** for widescreen).
- **Pixel (Picture Element)** = The smallest addressable unit on a display.
- **Frame Buffer** = A dedicated memory area in the graphics system that holds the image to be displayed.
- **Refresh Rate** = Number of times the screen is redrawn per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Color Depth / Bit Depth** = Number of bits used to represent the color of a single pixel (e.g., **8-bit, 24-bit, 32-bit**).
- **DPI (Dots Per Inch)** = Print resolution metric; **PPI (Pixels Per Inch)** = Display resolution metric.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Tip for KTU:** Total memory of a frame buffer is computed as:
> $$\text{Frame Buffer Size} = \text{Resolution} \times \text{Color Depth (in bytes)}$$
> Always convert bits to bytes by dividing by **8**.

### 1.4 Scope of Computer Graphics

According to the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus framework, Computer Graphics covers the following sub-domains:

1. **2D Graphics** — Drawing on a flat plane (lines, circles, polygons).
2. **3D Graphics** — Modeling and rendering objects in three dimensions.
3. **Geometric Transformations** — Translation, rotation, scaling, shearing.
4. **Viewing Pipeline** — World → Camera → Screen coordinate transformations.
5. **Illumination & Shading** — Light models, Lambert, Phong, Gouraud shading.
6. **Animation** — Creating motion through sequence of frames.
7. **Multimedia Integration** — Combining text, audio, video, and graphics.
8. **Virtual Reality (VR) & Augmented Reality (AR)** — Immersive graphics environments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillars of Computer Graphics (Foundational Pillars)

The discipline of Computer Graphics rests on **four fundamental pillars**, which form the structural backbone of every KTU question paper:

**Pillar 1 — Hardware (The Display Engine)**
- Display devices: **CRT (Cathode Ray Tube), LCD (Liquid Crystal Display), LED (Light Emitting Diode), OLED (Organic LED), Plasma**.
- Input devices: **Mouse, Keyboard, Light Pen, Joystick, Digitizer, Touch Screen, Data Glove, Scanner**.
- Output devices: **Printers (Laser, Inkjet), Plotters, 3D Printers**.

**Pillar 2 — Software (The Algorithm Bank)**
- Drawing algorithms: DDA, Bresenham's, Midpoint Circle Algorithm.
- Clipping algorithms: Cohen-Sutherland, Liang-Barsky.
- Transformation matrices: 2D/3D.
- Rendering engines: Ray Tracing, Rasterization, Radiosity.

**Pillar 3 — Mathematics (The Grammar)**
- Linear Algebra — Vectors, Matrices, Transformations.
- Coordinate Geometry — 2D/3D coordinate systems.
- Trigonometry — Rotations, projections.
- Calculus — Curves, surfaces, Bezier equations.

**Pillar 4 — Data Structures (The Memory Organizer)**
- Frame buffer, Z-buffer, Stencil buffer.
- Spatial data structures: BSP trees, Octrees, Quadtrees, k-d trees.
- Polygon tables, edge tables, vertex lists.

### 2.2 Applications of Computer Graphics (KTU High-Yield Section)

Computer Graphics has a vast application domain. KTU examiners frequently test this as a **2-mark or 3-mark direct question**. The following categorization is the KTU-preferred format:

| **Domain** | **Application Examples** |
|---|---|
| **Entertainment & Gaming** | 3D video games, CGI movies, Virtual reality games, Animation studios (Pixar, DreamWorks) |
| **Education & Training** | E-learning modules, Virtual labs, Surgical simulators, Flight simulators |
| **Engineering & CAD/CAM** | AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, 3D mechanical part design, Architecture (BIM) |
| **Medical & Scientific** | MRI/CT scan visualization, Surgical planning, Bioinformatics, Molecular modeling |
| **Business & Presentation** | Charts, Graphs, Bar diagrams, Data dashboards, Marketing infographics |
| **Geographic Information Systems (GIS)** | Google Maps, Google Earth, Weather maps, Topographic visualization |
| **Defense & Aerospace** | Radar displays, Missile trajectory simulation, Cockpit HUDs, Satellite imagery |
| **Art & Design** | Digital painting, Logo design, Graphic design (Photoshop, Illustrator), Fashion design |
| **Web & UI/UX** | Web design, Mobile app UI, Icon design, Iconography |
| **Simulation & Modeling** | Fluid dynamics, Climate modeling, Astrophysical simulations, Molecular dynamics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite Question:** *"List any 5 applications of computer graphics."* — Memorize at least 8 to be safe; the examiner often allows partial marks for partially correct lists.

### 2.3 Classification of Computer Graphics

Computer Graphics is broadly classified along the following dimensions:

**A. Based on Dimensionality**
- **2D Graphics** — Flat images, screen coordinates (x, y). Examples: logos, icons, charts.
- **3D Graphics** — Volumetric objects with depth, using (x, y, z). Examples: 3D models, games.

**B. Based on Interaction**
- **Passive (Non-Interactive) Graphics** — No user input during display. Example: A movie playing on screen.
- **Interactive (Active) Graphics** — User interacts in real-time. Example: Video games, CAD software.

**C. Based on Representation**
- **Raster (Bitmap) Graphics** — Image stored as a grid of pixels. Examples: JPEG, PNG, BMP, GIF. Loses quality on zooming.
- **Vector Graphics** — Image stored as mathematical equations (paths, curves). Examples: SVG, PDF, EPS, AI. Resolution-independent.

**D. Based on Generation Method**
- **Object-Oriented (Geometric) Graphics** — Built from primitives (lines, polygons).
- **Pixel-Oriented (Image) Graphics** — Built from sampled photographs/scans.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units** | **KTU Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frame Buffer Size | $\text{FBS} = R \times C \times (B \div 8)$ | Bytes | Calculate VRAM needed for a display |
| Aspect Ratio | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{\text{Width}}{\text{Height}}$ | Dimensionless | Resize images proportionally |
| Total Pixels | $N = \text{rows} \times \text{columns}$ | Pixels | Calculate resolution |
| Color Combinations | $C = 2^{b}$ | Combinations | Max colors from bit depth $b$ |
| Refresh Memory Bandwidth | $\text{BW} = R \times C \times B \times f_{\text{refresh}}$ | bits/sec | Calculate data throughput |
| Dot Clock (Pixel Rate) | $f_{\text{pixel}} = R_{\text{horizontal}} \times R_{\text{vertical}} \times f_{\text{refresh}}$ | Hz | Calculate video signal frequency |
| PPI (Pixels Per Inch) | $\text{PPI} = \dfrac{\text{Diagonal Pixels}}{\text{Diagonal Inches}}$ | Pixels/inch | Screen sharpness metric |
| Horizontal Scan Frequency | $f_h = R_{\text{lines}} \times f_{\text{refresh}}$ | Hz | For CRT timing calculations |
| Anti-aliasing (Supersampling) | $\text{Samples} = n^2 \text{ per pixel}$ | Samples | For $n \times n$ SSAA |

> [!TIP]
> **Where the Pipes Are Hidden:** Notice that no row above uses a literal `|` symbol inside the formula. We use $\div$ for division, $\times$ for multiplication, and $\dfrac{}{}$ for fractions — KTU board exams reward clean presentation.

### 2.5 Display Architecture: The Conceptual Pipeline

Every Computer Graphics system, no matter how complex, follows a universal data flow:

**Input Data → Application Model → Scene Graph → Geometry Processing → Rasterization → Frame Buffer → Display Device → Human Eye**

- **Input Data** — 3D models, textures, lighting, camera position.
- **Application Model** — User's program (game engine, CAD tool).
- **Scene Graph** — Hierarchical structure of all objects in the world.
- **Geometry Processing** — Vertex transformations, lighting calculations.
- **Rasterization** — Converting geometric primitives into pixels.
- **Frame Buffer** — Final pixel storage.
- **Display Device** — Monitor that visualizes the buffer.
- **Human Eye** — Ultimate receiver of the visual information.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

In real-world engineering, these concepts are critical for:

- **Hardware Design** — Graphics card manufacturers (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel) design VRAM using the frame buffer formula.
- **Game Development** — Choosing optimal resolution vs. performance trade-offs.
- **Medical Imaging** — High bit-depth displays for accurate diagnosis from radiological scans.
- **Aerospace** — HUD (Heads-Up Display) refresh rates are calculated using pixel clock formulas.
- **Architecture & Construction** — BIM software uses vector graphics for infinite zoom without quality loss.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Number of Pixels in a Display

Let us derive the most fundamental metric in Computer Graphics — total pixel count.

**Step 1 — Define the resolution.**
A display has $R$ rows and $C$ columns of pixels.
$$R = \text{Number of horizontal lines (rows)}$$
$$C = \text{Number of pixels per row (columns)}$$

**Step 2 — Total pixel count.**
Each row contributes $C$ pixels, and there are $R$ such rows:
$$N = C + C + C + \dots \text{ ($R$ times)}$$

This is a basic multiplication:
$$N = R \times C$$

**Step 3 — Substitute example values.**
For Full HD:
$$R = 1080, \quad C = 1920$$
$$N = 1080 \times 1920 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels} \approx 2.07 \text{ megapixels}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Frame Buffer Size

The frame buffer is the memory that holds color information for every pixel.

**Step 1 — Bits per pixel.**
If each pixel is stored using $b$ bits, total bits required:
$$B_{\text{total}} = N \times b$$

**Step 2 — Convert bits to bytes.**
$$B_{\text{total (bytes)}} = \dfrac{N \times b}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the full expression.**
$$\text{Frame Buffer} = \dfrac{R \times C \times b}{8} \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to higher units (KB, MB, GB).**
$$\text{FB (MB)} = \dfrac{R \times C \times b}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Chain:** 1 KB = 1024 B, 1 MB = 1024 KB = $1024^2$ B, 1 GB = 1024 MB = $1024^3$ B.
> For KTU board exams, use **1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes** unless the question explicitly states SI (decimal) units.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A graphics system uses a resolution of **1280 × 1024** with **24-bit color depth**. Calculate:
1. Total number of pixels.
2. Total frame buffer size in KB and MB.
3. Number of unique colors it can display.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Total Pixels:**
$$N = R \times C$$
$$N = 1024 \times 1280$$
$$N = 1{,}310{,}720 \text{ pixels}$$

**Part (b) — Frame Buffer Size:**
$$\text{FB} = \dfrac{N \times b}{8}$$
$$\text{FB (bytes)} = \dfrac{1{,}310{,}720 \times 24}{8}$$
$$\text{FB (bytes)} = 1{,}310{,}720 \times 3$$
$$\text{FB (bytes)} = 3{,}932{,}160 \text{ bytes}$$

Converting to KB:
$$\text{FB (KB)} = \dfrac{3{,}932{,}160}{1024}$$
$$\text{FB (KB)} = 3840 \text{ KB}$$

Converting to MB:
$$\text{FB (MB)} = \dfrac{3840}{1024}$$
$$\text{FB (MB)} = 3.75 \text{ MB}$$

**Part (c) — Unique Colors:**
$$C_{\text{colors}} = 2^{b}$$
$$C_{\text{colors}} = 2^{24}$$
$$C_{\text{colors}} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ colors} \approx 16.7 \text{ million colors}$$

This is exactly the famous **"True Color"** (or **16.7 million color**) standard, used widely in displays, photographs, and digital cinema.

### 3.4 Derivation: Pixel Clock (Dot Clock)

The pixel clock determines how fast pixels are pushed to the display.

**Step 1 — Pixels per frame.**
$$\text{Pixels/frame} = R \times C$$

**Step 2 — Frames per second.**
The refresh rate $f$ (in Hz) gives frames per second.

**Step 3 — Pixels per second.**
$$f_{\text{pixel}} = R \times C \times f$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
For Full HD at 60 Hz:
$$f_{\text{pixel}} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 60$$
$$f_{\text{pixel}} = 1{,}920 \times 64{,}800$$
$$f_{\text{pixel}} = 124{,}416{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_{\text{pixel}} \approx 124.4 \text{ MHz}$$

This is the minimum dot clock a graphics card must support for Full HD @ 60 Hz.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: KTU Question Patterns & Triggers

| **Question Trigger Phrase** | **What Examiner Wants** | **Key Formula** | **Common Mistake** |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Calculate the frame buffer size" | VRAM requirement | $\text{FB} = \dfrac{R \times C \times b}{8}$ | Forgetting to divide by 8 |
| "How many colors can be displayed?" | Bit depth → combinations | $C = 2^{b}$ | Reporting bits, not combinations |
| "Find the aspect ratio" | Width/Height | $\text{AR} = W/H$ | Inverting numerator/denominator |
| "Total pixels on screen" | Resolution product | $N = R \times C$ | Using sum instead of product |
| "Pixel clock frequency" | Timing calculation | $f_p = R \times C \times f$ | Forgetting to multiply by refresh rate |
| "Memory bandwidth" | Data throughput | $\text{BW} = N \times b \times f$ | Using wrong units (bits vs. bytes) |

### 3.6 Reverse Engineering: From Memory to Resolution

A common KTU 14-mark problem: *"Given a frame buffer of 8 MB and 24-bit color, find the maximum resolution."*

**Step 1 — Total bytes available.**
$$\text{FB} = 8 \text{ MB} = 8 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 8{,}388{,}608 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Bytes per pixel.**
$$b_{\text{bytes}} = \dfrac{24}{8} = 3 \text{ bytes/pixel}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum pixels.**
$$N = \dfrac{\text{FB}}{b_{\text{bytes}}} = \dfrac{8{,}388{,}608}{3} \approx 2{,}796{,}202.67 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 4 — Pick a standard resolution.**
Try **1600 × 1200**:
$$1600 \times 1200 = 1{,}920{,}000 \text{ pixels} \quad (\text{within budget})$$

Try **1920 × 1080**:
$$1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels} \quad (\text{within budget})$$

Try **2048 × 1536**:
$$2048 \times 1536 = 3{,}145{,}728 \text{ pixels} \quad (\text{exceeds budget})$$

**Answer:** Maximum standard resolution = **1920 × 1080** (Full HD) or **1600 × 1200** (UXGA). The examiner typically expects the largest standard resolution below the pixel budget.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Computer Graphics System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the full architecture of a computer graphics workstation, mapping the flow of data from the application to the human eye.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER APPLICATION LAYER] --> B[GEOMETRY PROCESSING UNIT]
    B --> C[TRANSFORMATION AND LIGHTING ENGINE]
    C --> D[CLIPPING AND CULLING STAGE]
    D --> E[RASTERIZATION PIPELINE]
    E --> F[FRAME BUFFER VRAM]
    F --> G[VIDEO CONTROLLER]
    G --> H[DISPLAY DEVICE MONITOR]
    H --> I[HUMAN VISUAL PERCEPTION]
    
    J[CPU HOST BUS] --> B
    K[GPU MEMORY CONTROLLER] --> F
    L[KEYBOARD AND MOUSE INPUT] --> A
    M[SCANNER AND DIGITIZER] --> A
    N[NETWORK STREAMING SOURCE] --> A
    
    O[POWER SUPPLY UNIT] --> H
    P[VRAM REFRESH CIRCUIT] --> F
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: The Graphics Rendering Pipeline (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3D MODEL DATA] --> B[VERTEX SHADER]
    B --> C[PRIMITIVE ASSEMBLY]
    C --> D[RASTERIZATION]
    D --> E[FRAGMENT SHADER]
    E --> F[TESTS AND BLENDING]
    F --> G[FRAME BUFFER WRITE]
    G --> H[SCREEN OUTPUT]
    
    I[TRANSFORMATIONS MATRIX] --> B
    J[CLIP VOLUME] --> C
    K[TEXTURE MEMORY] --> E
    L[Z BUFFER] --> F
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Classification Diagram: Types of Computer Graphics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CG[COMPUTER GRAPHICS]
    CG --> A1[BASED ON DIMENSION]
    CG --> A2[BASED ON INTERACTION]
    CG --> A3[BASED ON REPRESENTATION]
    
    A1 --> B1[2D GRAPHICS]
    A1 --> B2[3D GRAPHICS]
    
    A2 --> C1[PASSIVE GRAPHICS]
    A2 --> C2[INTERACTIVE GRAPHICS]
    
    A3 --> D1[RASTER BITMAP]
    A3 --> D2[VECTOR GRAPHIC]
    
    B1 --> E1[LOGOS ICONS CHARTS]
    B2 --> E2[3D MODELS GAMES VR]
    
    C1 --> F1[MOVIES ANIMATIONS]
    C2 --> F2[CAD GAMES SIMULATORS]
    
    D1 --> G1[JPEG PNG BMP GIF]
    D2 --> G2[SVG PDF EPS AI]
```

### 4.4 Architecture Diagram: Frame Buffer Memory Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FRAME BUFFER VRAM] --> B[PIXEL 0 0]
    A --> C[PIXEL 0 1]
    A --> D[PIXEL 0 2]
    A --> E[ELLIPSIS DOTS]
    A --> F[PIXEL R MINUS 1 C MINUS 1]
    
    B --> G[RED CHANNEL 8 BITS]
    B --> H[GREEN CHANNEL 8 BITS]
    B --> I[BLUE CHANNEL 8 BITS]
    B --> J[ALPHA CHANNEL 8 BITS OPTIONAL]
```

### 4.5 Application Domain Map: Where Computer Graphics is Used

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[APPLICATIONS OF CG]
    APP --> P1[ENTERTAINMENT]
    APP --> P2[EDUCATION]
    APP --> P3[ENGINEERING]
    APP --> P4[MEDICINE]
    APP --> P5[SCIENCE]
    APP --> P6[BUSINESS]
    APP --> P7[DEFENSE]
    APP --> P8[ART AND DESIGN]
    
    P1 --> Q1[VIDEO GAMES CGI MOVIES]
    P2 --> Q2[E LEARNING VIRTUAL LABS]
    P3 --> Q3[CAD CAM BIM ARCHITECTURE]
    P4 --> Q4[MRI CT SCAN VISUALIZATION]
    P5 --> Q5[DATA VISUALIZATION SIMULATIONS]
    P6 --> Q6[CHARTS GRAPHS DASHBOARDS]
    P7 --> Q7[RADAR HUD MISSILE TRACKING]
    P8 --> Q8[DIGITAL ART GRAPHIC DESIGN FASHION]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note for Students:**
> If your Mermaid renderer is offline, the textual flow is:
> *User App → Geometry → Transformation → Rasterization → Frame Buffer → Display → Eye.*
> Memorize this pipeline — it appears verbatim in KTU short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Computer Graphics. List any four applications of Computer Graphics.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Computer Graphics is a branch of computer science that deals with the creation, manipulation, storage, and display of visual (pictorial) content on a digital display device using computational techniques and algorithms. It transforms numerical/abstract data into perceivable images for human interpretation.

**Four Applications:**
1. **Entertainment & Gaming** — Used in 3D video games, animated films, and CGI movies to create realistic visual effects.
2. **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** — Used in mechanical, civil, and architectural design for drafting and modeling (e.g., AutoCAD, SolidWorks).
3. **Medical Imaging** — Used to visualize internal body structures through MRI, CT, and X-ray scan rendering.
4. **Education & Training** — Used in virtual labs, e-learning modules, and flight simulators for hands-on training without physical risk.

*(Any 4 valid applications are accepted; mention the domain and a specific use case for full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Raster Graphics and Vector Graphics.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Raster Graphics** | **Vector Graphics** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Image stored as a grid of pixels | Image stored as mathematical equations of paths and curves |
| **File Format Examples** | JPEG, PNG, BMP, GIF, TIFF | SVG, PDF, EPS, AI, CDR |
| **Resolution Dependency** | Resolution-dependent (loses quality on zoom) | Resolution-independent (scales infinitely without quality loss) |
| **Storage Size** | Larger (depends on resolution × color depth) | Smaller (depends on complexity, not resolution) |
| **Best Suited For** | Photographs, realistic scenes | Logos, icons, typography, line art |
| **Editability** | Pixel-level editing (lossy) | Object-level editing (lossless) |
| **Rendering Speed** | Faster to display (direct pixel mapping) | Slower (requires real-time computation) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write *"Vector graphics is better than raster"* — this is an opinion, not a fact. The correct answer explains the **trade-off** between the two. KTU boards deduct 1 mark for biased answers.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Q. (a) Define the term 'Computer Graphics'. Explain the block diagram of a typical computer graphics system with the functions of each block.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Computer Graphics is a discipline of computer science that involves the generation, representation, manipulation, and display of pictures and visual information using digital computers. It combines hardware (display devices), software (algorithms), and mathematical foundations (geometry, linear algebra) to produce graphical output.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
   ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐
   │  APPLICATION │ ───> │  GEOMETRY    │ ───> │  RASTERIZATION│
   │   PROGRAM    │      │  PROCESSOR   │      │  PIPELINE    │
   └──────────────┘      └──────────────┘      └──────────────┘
                                                        │
                                                        v
   ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐
   │   DISPLAY    │ <─── │   VIDEO      │ <─── │  FRAME BUFFER│
   │   DEVICE     │      │  CONTROLLER  │      │    (VRAM)    │
   └──────────────┘      └──────────────┘      └──────────────┘
          ^
          │
   ┌──────────────┐
   │  INPUT DEVICES│
   │ Mouse Keyboard│
   └──────────────┘
```

**Functions of Each Block (2 marks):**
- **Application Program** — Issues drawing commands; defines scene, objects, lighting, camera.
- **Geometry Processor** — Performs transformations (rotation, translation, scaling), lighting, and projection.
- **Rasterization Pipeline** — Converts geometric primitives (lines, polygons) into pixel fragments.
- **Frame Buffer** — Stores the final pixel color data in dedicated video memory.
- **Video Controller** — Reads the frame buffer at refresh rate and sends the video signal to the display.
- **Display Device** — Visualizes the frame buffer contents on a monitor (LCD, LED, CRT).
- **Input Devices** — Allow the user to interact (mouse, keyboard, joystick, digitizer).

---

**Q. (b) A graphics system has a display resolution of 1600 × 1200 pixels with a 32-bit color depth. Calculate: (i) Total number of pixels, (ii) Frame buffer size in MB, (iii) Number of unique displayable colors, (iv) Pixel clock frequency at 75 Hz refresh rate.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
Resolution: $R = 1200$ rows, $C = 1600$ columns
Color depth: $b = 32$ bits
Refresh rate: $f = 75$ Hz

**Part (i) — Total Pixels:**
$$N = R \times C = 1200 \times 1600$$
$$N = 1{,}920{,}000 \text{ pixels}$$

**Part (ii) — Frame Buffer Size:**
$$\text{FB (bytes)} = \dfrac{N \times b}{8} = \dfrac{1{,}920{,}000 \times 32}{8}$$
$$\text{FB (bytes)} = 1{,}920{,}000 \times 4 = 7{,}680{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$
$$\text{FB (MB)} = \dfrac{7{,}680{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} = \dfrac{7{,}680{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576}$$
$$\text{FB (MB)} \approx 7.32 \text{ MB}$$

**Part (iii) — Unique Colors:**
$$C_{\text{colors}} = 2^{b} = 2^{32}$$
$$C_{\text{colors}} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \approx 4.29 \times 10^9 \text{ colors}$$
(also known as **"True Color with Alpha"** or **"Deep Color"**)

**Part (iv) — Pixel Clock Frequency:**
$$f_{\text{pixel}} = R \times C \times f$$
$$f_{\text{pixel}} = 1200 \times 1600 \times 75$$
$$f_{\text{pixel}} = 144{,}000{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 144 \text{ MHz}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice for Internal Option*

**Q. (a) Explain the various applications of Computer Graphics in different domains, with at least two specific examples per domain.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Computer Graphics has penetrated almost every field of human activity. The major application domains are:

**1. Entertainment Industry (1 mark)**
- 3D video games (e.g., Call of Duty, FIFA) using real-time rendered environments.
- Animated movies (e.g., Pixar's "Toy Story", Disney's "Frozen") using CGI pipelines.
- VR theme park rides (e.g., Disney's Star Wars: Galaxy's Edge).

**2. Education and Training (1 mark)**
- Virtual laboratories for chemistry (e.g., PhET simulations) where students can mix chemicals safely.
- Flight simulators used by airlines and the military to train pilots without risk.
- Anatomical 3D models (e.g., Complete Anatomy app) for medical students.

**3. Engineering and Architecture (1 mark)**
- CAD software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks) for mechanical part design and assembly.
- BIM (Building Information Modeling) software (Revit, ArchiCAD) for architectural walkthroughs before construction.
- Finite Element Analysis (FEA) visualization for stress/strain on mechanical parts.

**4. Medical and Scientific Visualization (1 mark)**
- MRI and CT scan reconstruction to view tumors, fractures, and internal organs.
- Molecular visualization (e.g., PyMOL, Chimera) for studying protein structures.
- Climate modeling and weather prediction (e.g., NOAA visualizations).

**5. Business and Data Analytics (1 mark)**
- Interactive dashboards (e.g., Tableau, Power BI) for business intelligence.
- Marketing infographics and animated advertisements.
- Stock market real-time charting platforms.

**6. Defense and Aerospace (1 mark)**
- Radar and sonar display systems for tracking enemy aircraft and submarines.
- Heads-Up Displays (HUD) in fighter jet cockpits.
- Satellite imagery analysis for reconnaissance.

**7. Geographic Information Systems (GIS) (1 mark)**
- Google Maps and Google Earth for navigation and exploration.
- Urban planning using 3D city models.
- Disaster management and flood simulation.

---

**Q. (b) A graphics card has 4 GB of dedicated VRAM and supports 32-bit color. Find the maximum display resolution it can handle. Also calculate the aspect ratio and PPI of a 24-inch monitor with this maximum resolution.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
VRAM: $\text{FB} = 4 \text{ GB} = 4 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ bytes
Color depth: $b = 32$ bits

**Step 1 — Find bytes per pixel:**
$$b_{\text{bytes}} = \dfrac{32}{8} = 4 \text{ bytes/pixel}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum number of pixels:**
$$N_{\max} = \dfrac{\text{FB (bytes)}}{b_{\text{bytes}}} = \dfrac{4{,}294{,}967{,}296}{4}$$
$$N_{\max} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ pixels} \approx 1.07 \text{ gigapixels}$$

**Step 3 — Choose a standard resolution (16:9 ratio):**
We need $R \times C \le 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$ with $\dfrac{C}{R} = \dfrac{16}{9}$.

Let $C = 16k$ and $R = 9k$ for some integer $k$. Then:
$$16k \times 9k = 144 k^2 \le 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$$
$$k^2 \le \dfrac{1{,}073{,}741{,}824}{144} = 7{,}456{,}540.4$$
$$k \le \sqrt{7{,}456{,}540.4} \approx 2730.8$$

Taking $k = 2730$:
$$C = 16 \times 2730 = 43{,}680 \text{ pixels}$$
$$R = 9 \times 2730 = 24{,}570 \text{ pixels}$$

**Verification:**
$$43{,}680 \times 24{,}570 = 1{,}073{,}121{,}600 \text{ pixels} \le 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \quad (\text{Valid})$$

**Maximum Resolution (16:9) ≈ 43680 × 24570** (a hypothetical ultra-high resolution, ~4K-class and beyond).

**Step 4 — Aspect Ratio:**
$$\text{AR} = \dfrac{C}{R} = \dfrac{43{,}680}{24{,}570} = \dfrac{16}{9} \approx 1.78$$

**Step 5 — PPI Calculation (for 24-inch diagonal):**
Using the standard PPI formula:
$$\text{PPI} = \dfrac{\sqrt{C^2 + R^2}}{\text{Diagonal in inches}}$$
$$\text{PPI} = \dfrac{\sqrt{43{,}680^2 + 24{,}570^2}}{24}$$
$$\text{PPI} = \dfrac{\sqrt{1{,}907{,}942{,}400 + 603{,}684{,}900}}{24}$$
$$\text{PPI} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2{,}511{,}627{,}300}}{24}$$
$$\text{PPI} \approx \dfrac{50{,}116}{24}$$
$$\text{PPI} \approx 2088 \text{ pixels/inch}$$

This is far beyond current display technology, illustrating that 4 GB VRAM is more than sufficient for current-generation displays.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Students frequently write $4 \text{ GB} = 4 \times 10^9$ bytes (SI). KTU uses binary: $4 \text{ GB} = 4 \times 2^{30}$ bytes. Wrong unit choice = **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Divide by 8:** Bit-to-byte conversion ($\div 8$) is the most common mistake in frame buffer problems. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Reporting "millions" instead of exact values:** When the question says "calculate", the examiner wants the exact number, not an approximation.
> 4. **Confusing PPI with DPI:** PPI is for displays; DPI is for printers. Writing DPI for a monitor = **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping verification step:** Always verify your chosen resolution does not exceed the pixel budget. Omitting this loses 1 mark on 14-mark questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Basics of CG:**

- **Definition of Computer Graphics:** Creation, manipulation, and display of visual content on digital devices.
- **CG vs IP vs CV:** CG = data→image; IP = image→image; CV = image→meaning. **Never confuse them.**
- **Four Pillars of CG:** Hardware, Software, Mathematics, Data Structures.
- **Classification of CG:** 2D/3D, Passive/Interactive, Raster/Vector.
- **Standard Resolutions:** VGA (640×480), SVGA (800×600), XGA (1024×768), HD (1280×720), Full HD (1920×1080), 2K (2048×1080), 4K (3840×2160), 8K (7680×4320).
- **Frame Buffer Formula:** $\text{FB} = \dfrac{R \times C \times b}{8}$ bytes.
- **Total Pixels:** $N = R \times C$.
- **Color Combinations:** $C = 2^b$.
- **Pixel Clock:** $f_p = R \times C \times f_{\text{refresh}}$ Hz.
- **Aspect Ratio:** $\text{AR} = W/H$ (commonly 4:3 or 16:9).
- **PPI Formula:** $\text{PPI} = \dfrac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{\text{Diagonal in inches}}$.
- **True Color:** $b = 24$ bits → $2^{24} = 16.77$ million colors.
- **Deep Color:** $b = 32$ bits → $2^{32} = 4.29$ billion colors.
- **Rendering Pipeline:** App → Geometry → Rasterization → Frame Buffer → Display.
- **Display Devices:** CRT, LCD, LED, OLED, Plasma. LCD is the most common in laptops and monitors.
- **Input Devices:** Mouse, Keyboard, Light Pen, Joystick, Touch Screen, Data Glove, Scanner, Digitizer.
- **Vector vs Raster:** Vector = mathematical, scalable; Raster = pixel grid, resolution-dependent.
- **Application Domains (must memorize at least 8):** Entertainment, Education, Engineering CAD/CAM, Medical, Scientific, Business, Defense, GIS, Art & Design, Web UI/UX.
- **Memory Unit Conversions (Binary):** 1 KB = $2^{10}$ B; 1 MB = $2^{20}$ B; 1 GB = $2^{30}$ B; 1 TB = $2^{40}$ B.
- **Common KTU Traps:** Mixing bit/byte units; using SI (decimal) vs binary units; confusing PPI with DPI; not verifying resolution fits within VRAM budget.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Golden Rule for KTU Boards:**
> *"Show every step, label every unit, verify every answer."* — A perfectly framed answer with clear unit annotations and verification steps easily scores **95%+ marks** even if a minor numerical slip occurs, because the examiner can award **method marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Video Display Devices – LED, OLED, PDP, FED, and Reflective Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Video Display Devices – LED, OLED, PDP, FED, and Reflective Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Video Display Device?
A **Video Display Device** is an electronic output peripheral that visually renders still or moving images by converting electrical video signals into controlled light emission, reflection, or modulation phenomena that the human eye can perceive. In the context of Computer Graphics, the display device forms the final stage of the rendering pipeline — the **raster scan system** that translates digital frame-buffer data into viewable pixels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A display device is classified by its **light-generation mechanism** — whether it is *emissive* (produces its own light) or *non-emissive* (modulates/reflects external light). The five devices in this module — **LED, OLED, PDP, FED, and Reflective Displays** — span both categories and are the dominant flat-panel technologies replacing legacy CRT monitors.

### 1.2 The Five Display Technologies at a Glance

| Technology | Full Form | Category | Light Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| LED | Light Emitting Diode | Emissive | Electroluminescence (InGaN/GaAs semiconductors) |
| OLED | Organic Light Emitting Diode | Emissive | Electroluminescence (organic small molecules/polymers) |
| PDP | Plasma Display Panel | Emissive | Gas discharge + phosphor excitation (UV photons) |
| FED | Field Emission Display | Emissive | Field-emitted electrons striking phosphors |
| Reflective LCD / E-Ink | Reflective Liquid Crystal / Electrophoretic | Non-Emissive | Ambient light modulation/reflection |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stage Spotlight" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a dark theatre where each pixel is a tiny stage.
> - **LED** = a *fixed electric bulb* — bright, reliable, but each colour needs a different bulb.
> - **OLED** = a *glowing paint sticker* — the material itself glows when current passes, so each sticker can be any colour and even bend with the wall.
> - **PDP** = a *miniature neon tube* — a tiny gas-filled cell gets zapped, creating invisible UV "lightning" that excites coloured paint.
> - **FED** = a *tiny cathode-ray cannon* — millions of microscopic electron guns fire at a phosphor screen.
> - **Reflective Display** = a *mirror with controllable shutters* — instead of glowing, it bounces whatever room light is available back to your eye (like reading a printed paper).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key engineering constants used in display characterization:
> - **Planck's constant (h)** = $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}}$
> - **Speed of light (c)** = $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}}$
> - **Elementary charge (q)** = $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}}$
> - **Luminance unit (cd/m²)** — candela per square metre
> - **Standard refresh rate** = $\mathbf{60\ \text{Hz}}$ (minimum), high-end = $\mathbf{120\ \text{Hz}}$
> - **CIE 1931 RGB colorimetry** is the reference color space for display calibration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 LED (Light Emitting Diode)

**Operating Principle:** An LED is a **p-n junction diode** fabricated from direct-bandgap semiconductors (e.g., GaAs, InGaN, AlGaInP). When a forward bias is applied, electrons from the n-region recombine with holes in the p-region across the **depletion region**. The energy released during recombination is emitted as a **photon**.

**Why it works:** The photon energy equals the band-gap energy $E_g$, so the emitted wavelength is dictated by material choice.

**The "Why" explained:**
1. Forward bias reduces the built-in potential barrier.
2. Minority carrier injection → electrons fall into the conduction band of p-side, holes into valence band of n-side.
3. Radiative recombination → photon emission with energy $h\nu = E_g$.

**Key Advantages:** High luminous efficacy ($\eta_L > 100\ \text{lm/W}$ for white LEDs), long lifetime ($>50{,}000$ hours), fast switching ($< 1\ \mu s$).

### 2.2 OLED (Organic Light Emitting Diode)

**Operating Principle:** Sandwiches thin organic films (e.g., **Alq₃** = tris(8-hydroxyquinoline) aluminum) between a transparent anode (**ITO** = Indium Tin Oxide) and a metallic cathode. Under forward bias, electrons and holes are injected into the **emissive layer (EML)** where they form **excitons** that radiatively decay.

**The "Why" explained:**
1. **HOMO** (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) acts like the p-type valence band.
2. **LUMO** (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital) acts like the n-type conduction band.
3. Recombination of injected e⁻–h⁺ pairs at the EML produces singlet (25%) and triplet (75%) excitons.
4. Phosphorescent OLEDs (PHOLEDs) harvest triplet states to achieve nearly **100% internal quantum efficiency**.

**Architectural Variants:**
- **Passive Matrix OLED (PMOLED)** — row-column addressing without storage capacitor.
- **Active Matrix OLED (AMOLED)** — each pixel has a TFT driver + capacitor (used in smartphones, TVs).

### 2.3 PDP (Plasma Display Panel)

**Operating Principle:** Each cell contains a mixture of **noble gases** (typically **Ne + Xe** or **He + Xe** at ~500 Torr). Address electrodes apply a voltage that ionizes the gas into a **plasma** — a quasi-neutral ionized gas — releasing **vacuum-ultraviolet (VUV)** photons at **147 nm** and **172 nm**. These VUV photons strike RGB phosphors coated on the cell walls, which then re-emit visible light.

**The "Why" explained:**
1. AC voltage across sustain electrodes creates a glow discharge.
2. Xe atoms in metastable states ($^3P_2$) collide and form **Xe₂* excimers**, which radiatively decay.
3. Excimer emission in VUV is absorbed by phosphor (e.g., Zn₂SiO₄:Mn for green, Y₂O₃:Eu for red, BaMgAl₁₀O₁₇:Eu for blue).
4. Phosphor converts UV → visible via **Stokes shift**.

**Sub-technologies:** **AC-PDP** (uses dielectric barrier, memory effect) vs **DC-PDP** (direct current, simpler but lower life).

### 2.4 FED (Field Emission Display)

**Operating Principle:** A matrix of **micro-tips** (Spindt tips, typically molybdenum cones of radius < 50 nm) or **carbon nanotubes (CNTs)** acts as cold cathodes. When a strong electric field ($E \approx 1{-}5\ \text{V/nm}$) is applied between the cathode tip and a gate electrode, electrons **tunnel** out via **Fowler-Nordheim tunnelling** and are accelerated toward an anode coated with RGB phosphors, identical to a CRT phosphor screen.

**The "Why" explained:**
1. Field emission does not require heating (unlike thermionic CRT cathodes).
2. Each sub-pixel is essentially a tiny CRT.
3. The anode-cathode gap is ~1–2 mm, enabling thin flat-panel form factor.

**Fowler-Nordheim Equation** is the governing emission model.

### 2.5 Reflective Displays

**Operating Principle:** A **non-emissive** device that modulates ambient light using one of several mechanisms:
- **Reflective LCD** — twisted-nematic LC cell over a diffusive reflector; voltage controls polarization rotation.
- **Electrophoretic (E-Ink)** — microcapsules (~50–100 µm) containing positively charged white pigment (TiO₂) and negatively charged black pigment suspended in a dielectric fluid; electrodes move pigments to the viewing surface.
- **Electrowetting** — voltage changes the wetting angle of a coloured oil film on water, exposing/hiding a white substrate.
- **MEMS (Interferometric Modulator — IMOD / Qualcomm mirasol)** — micro-cantilevers change interference state of reflected light.

**Why reflective is special:** Near-zero power consumption (passive), excellent sunlight readability, no backlight, mimics paper — used in **e-readers (Kindle)** and low-power wearables.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU University Exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LED emission wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$ | h = Planck, c = speed, $E_g$ = band gap | nm |
| 2 | Photon energy | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $\nu$ = frequency | eV or J |
| 3 | Photon momentum | $p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$ | — | kg·m/s |
| 4 | Luminous efficacy | $\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P_{el}}$ | $\Phi_v$ = luminous flux, $P_{el}$ = input power | lm/W |
| 5 | Display contrast ratio | $CR = \dfrac{L_{white}}{L_{black}}$ | $L$ = luminance | dimensionless |
| 6 | Response time | $t_r + t_f$ | rise + fall time | ms |
| 7 | Refresh rate relation | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}}$ | frame period | Hz |
| 8 | Fowler-Nordheim emission | $J = \dfrac{AE^2}{\phi} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B\phi^{3/2}}{E}\right)$ | $J$ = current density, $E$ = field, $\phi$ = work function | A/m², V/nm, eV |
| 9 | Frame buffer bandwidth | $BW = W \times H \times bpp \times f$ | W = width, H = height, bpp = bits/pixel, f = refresh | bits/s |
| 10 | Sub-pixel count | $N = W \times H \times 3$ | 3 (RGB) sub-pixels per pixel | — |
| 11 | Addressing duty cycle | $D = \dfrac{1}{N_{rows}}$ | $N_{rows}$ = scan lines | dimensionless |
| 12 | Color depth | $2^{bpp}$ | levels per channel | colors |
| 13 | Aspect ratio | $AR = \dfrac{W}{H}$ | width to height | — |
| 14 | PPI (pixel density) | $PPI = \dfrac{\sqrt{W^2+H^2}}{d_{diag}}$ | $d_{diag}$ = diagonal in inches | pixels/inch |
| 15 | Persistence (CRT legacy) | $I(t) = I_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau$ = decay constant | cd/m², s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas drive the design of every modern flat-panel — from a smartwatch AMOLED to a 100-inch 8K LED TV. Engineers balance $E_g$ (colour), $\eta_L$ (battery life), $CR$ (image quality), and $BW$ (data throughput) to hit cost/perception targets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: LED Emission Wavelength from Band-Gap Energy

We want to derive the dominant emission wavelength $\lambda$ of an LED given its semiconductor band-gap $E_g$.

**Step 1 — State the foundational relation between photon energy and frequency (Planck-Einstein relation):**

The energy of a single photon is given by:

$$E = h\nu$$

**Step 2 — Express frequency in terms of wavelength using the wave relation $\nu = c/\lambda$ (for electromagnetic waves in vacuum/near-vacuum):**

$$E = h \cdot \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for the emission wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Identify that for an LED, the photon energy equals the band-gap energy (energy conservation across the radiative recombination event):**

$$E = E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the constants in convenient units to produce a designer-friendly "1240 rule":**

Using $h = 4.1357 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{eV·s}$ and $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$:

$$hc = (4.1357 \times 10^{-15})(2.998 \times 10^{8})\ \text{eV·m}$$

$$hc = 1.2398 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{eV·m}$$

**Step 6 — Convert $E_g$ from eV (typical semiconductor textbook unit) to make $\lambda$ emerge in nanometres:**

Since $1\ \mu\text{m} = 1000\ \text{nm}$, multiplying the numerator and denominator:

$$\lambda\ (\text{nm}) = \dfrac{1240}{E_g\ (\text{eV})}$$

**Step 7 — Worked numerical examples for primary display colours:**

For a **blue LED** at $\lambda = 470\ \text{nm}$:

$$E_g = \dfrac{1240}{470} = 2.638\ \text{eV} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{InGaN alloy system.}$$

For a **green LED** at $\lambda = 530\ \text{nm}$:

$$E_g = \dfrac{1240}{530} = 2.340\ \text{eV} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{InGaN (higher In fraction).}$$

For a **red LED** at $\lambda = 625\ \text{nm}$:

$$E_g = \dfrac{1240}{625} = 1.984\ \text{eV} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{AlGaInP alloy system.}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A single semiconductor cannot cover the full visible spectrum — that is why direct-LED displays cluster **InGaN (blue/green) + AlGaInP (red)** dies to produce white light through partial-phosphor conversion or RGB sub-pixel mixing.

### 3.2 Derivation: Display Frame-Buffer Bandwidth

**Step 1 — Define the data required per pixel:**

For a colour depth of $b$ bits per channel and 3 channels (RGB), each pixel needs $3b$ bits.

**Step 2 — Multiply by the total number of pixels in one frame:**

$$\text{Data per frame} = 3b \times W \times H\ \text{bits}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the refresh rate $f$ (frames per second):**

$$BW = 3b \times W \times H \times f\ \text{bits/s}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation for a 4K UHD display (3840 × 2160) at 60 Hz, 8-bit colour:**

$$BW = 3 \times 8 \times 3840 \times 2160 \times 60$$

$$BW = 24 \times 3840 \times 129{,}600$$

$$BW = 24 \times 497{,}664{,}000$$

$$BW = 11.944 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bits/s} \approx 11.94\ \text{Gbps}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to gigabytes per second (decimal GB):**

$$BW = \dfrac{11.94}{8} = 1.493\ \text{GB/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *minimum* internal bandwidth of the display pipeline — which is why modern GPUs use **DisplayPort 2.1 / HDMI 2.1** lanes and **DSC (Display Stream Compression)** to handle 8K@60 Hz and beyond.

### 3.3 Derivation: Fowler-Nordheim Field Emission Current Density

This is the *core equation* for **FED (Field Emission Display)** cathodes.

**Step 1 — Quantum-mechanical tunnelling through a triangular barrier:**

Electrons at the Fermi level of a metal cathode must tunnel through a potential barrier $\phi$ (work function) narrowed by an applied electric field $E$.

**Step 2 — Apply the WKB approximation to the triangular barrier:**

The tunnelling probability is:

$$T \approx \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{4\sqrt{2m\phi^{3}}}{3\hbar eE}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the supply function (electron flux at Fermi level):**

This yields the **Fowler-Nordheim equation**:

$$J = \dfrac{AE^2}{\phi} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B\phi^{3/2}}{E}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Define the constants:**

$$A = \dfrac{e^3}{8\pi h} \approx 1.541 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A·eV/V}^2$$

$$B = \dfrac{8\pi\sqrt{2m_e}}{3he} \approx 6.83 \times 10^{9}\ \text{eV}^{-3/2}\text{V/m}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation (numerator):**

The $E^2/\phi$ term shows current density scales with the *square* of the field — extremely sensitive to tip sharpness, which is why **Spindt micro-tips** and **CNT arrays** dominate FED cathodes.

**Step 6 — Interpretation of the exponential:**

Increasing the work function $\phi$ *drastically* reduces emission — this is why FED cathodes use low-work-function materials like **Mo, LaB₆, or Cs-coated CNTs**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Trade-off:** Higher $E$ → more emission BUT also more ion bombardment → cathode degradation. FED lifetime is therefore a "field-versus-reliability" optimization.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of the Five Displays

| Parameter | LED | OLED | PDP | FED | Reflective |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Light source | Electroluminescence | Electroluminescence | Plasma + phosphor | Field e⁻ + phosphor | Ambient reflection |
| Luminance (cd/m²) | 1000–5000+ | 200–1000 (peak 1000+) | 500–1500 | 300–600 | 50–250 (depends on ambient) |
| Contrast ratio | 1000:1 – 1,000,000:1 (HDR) | ∞:1 (true black) | 20,000:1 – 2,000,000:1 (dark room) | 100,000:1 | 8:1 – 15:1 |
| Response time | < 1 µs | < 0.1 ms | ~ 1–5 ms | < 1 ms | ~ 10–50 ms (E-Ink) |
| Viewing angle | 170°+ | 178° | 170° | 170°+ | ~ 160° (LCD) |
| Power consumption | Moderate (depends on backlight) | Low (no backlight; per-pixel on) | Higher (gas discharge) | Moderate | Very low (bi-stable) |
| Lifetime (hours) | > 50,000 | 30,000–100,000 (blue degrades) | 30,000–60,000 (phosphor aging) | 20,000–30,000 (cathode) | Indefinite static image |
| Thickness | 10–50 mm (edge-lit) / < 10 mm (direct) | < 2 mm (flexible possible) | 30–80 mm | 5–15 mm | 1–3 mm |
| Operating temperature | -40 °C to 85 °C | -30 °C to 70 °C | 0 °C to 40 °C | 5 °C to 45 °C | 0 °C to 50 °C |
| Burn-in risk | None | Yes (organic decay) | Yes (phosphor aging) | Yes (ion sputtering) | None |
| Best use-case | TV walls, signage, backlights | Smartphones, premium TVs, VR | Legacy large TVs (now phased out) | Prototypes, niche | E-readers, wearables, IoT |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** *"Compare OLED and PDP in terms of luminance, contrast, and power consumption."* — Memorize the trends above and the *reason* behind each trend (e.g., OLED true black because pixels switch fully off, no plasma sustain needed).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Video Display Pipeline (Master Overview)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: PC GPU or SoC] --> B[Frame Buffer VRAM]
    B --> C[Display Controller Timing TCON]
    C --> D[Column Driver Source ICs]
    C --> E[Row Driver Gate ICs]
    D --> F[Pixel Matrix with Sub-Pixels R G B]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Display Technology}
    G --> G1[LED backlight LCD panel]
    G --> G2[AMOLED self emissive stack]
    G --> G3[Plasma cell with Xe gas]
    G --> G4[FED cathode array and phosphor]
    G --> G5[Reflective LC or E Ink capsule]
    G1 --> H[Viewer Perceived Image]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
```

### 4.2 LED Cross-Section & Carrier Recombination (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Anode P type semiconductor] --> D[Depletion Region Energy Barrier Eg]
    D --> N[Cathode N type semiconductor]
    N --> E1[Electron injection]
    P --> H1[Hole injection]
    E1 --> R[Radiative recombination at junction]
    H1 --> R
    R --> PH[Photon emission with wavelength hc over Eg]
    PH --> OUT[Light output through transparent window]
```

### 4.3 OLED Layered Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Substrate glass or flexible PI film] --> A1[Anode ITO transparent conductive oxide]
    A1 --> HIL[Hole Injection Layer HIL e.g. PEDOT PSS]
    HIL --> HTL[Hole Transport Layer HTL e.g. NPB]
    HTL --> EML[Emissive Layer EML doped with phosphorescent Ir complex]
    EML --> ETL[Electron Transport Layer ETL e.g. Alq3]
    ETL --> EIL[Electron Injection Layer EIL e.g. LiF]
    EIL --> C[Cathode metal Al or Mg Ag alloy]
    C --> E[Encapsulation glass or thin film barrier to block H2O and O2]
```

### 4.4 PDP Cell Operational Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Address electrode applies write pulse] --> A2[Xe atoms ionize and form plasma]
    A2 --> A3[Excimer Xe2 star forms metastable]
    A3 --> A4[VUV photon emission at 147 nm and 172 nm]
    A4 --> A5[Phosphor coating absorbs UV via Stokes shift]
    A5 --> A6[Visible light red green or blue emitted]
    A6 --> A7[Sustain AC pulse maintains discharge each frame]
    A7 --> A8[Cell returns to off state after frame erase]
```

### 4.5 FED Triode Structure & Electron Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Cathode Mo Spindt tip or CNT bundle] --> G[Gate electrode at 50 to 100 V]
    G --> FN[Fowler Nordheim tunnelling at high field E]
    FN --> ACC[Acceleration gap 1 to 2 mm at 5 to 10 kV]
    ACC --> PH[Phosphor anode RGB stripes]
    PH --> VIS[Visible light through front glass]
    PH -.ion back sputter.-> C
```

### 4.6 Reflective E-Ink Capsule (Electrophoretic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CAP[Microsphere capsule 50 to 100 micrometre] --> F1[Dielectric fluid transparent oil]
    F1 --> W[White pigment TiO2 positively charged]
    F1 --> B[Black pigment carbon nanoparticles negatively charged]
    W --> TOP[Top transparent electrode ITO]
    B --> TOP
    TOP --> V1[Positive voltage pushes white to viewer]
    B --> BOT[Bottom electrode when reversed black visible]
    W --> BOT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to study these diagrams:** In KTU exams, you are usually asked to *draw and label* a block diagram of a display device. Memorize the *names* of the layers (ITO, HTL, EML, ETL, cathode), the *direction of carrier flow* (anode→HIL→HTL→EML), and the *light exit direction* (out of the transparent anode, never the metal cathode).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 1, Q1(a)]**
> **Q1.** Define **OLED**. List **two advantages** of OLED over LED-LCD displays.

**Model Answer:**
**OLED (Organic Light Emitting Diode)** is a thin-film emissive display in which a layer of **organic compounds** (such as small molecules like **Alq₃** or polymers like **PEDOT:PSS**) emits light in response to an electric current. The organic layer is sandwiched between a transparent anode (ITO) and a metallic cathode.

**Two advantages over LED-LCD:**
1. **Self-emissive** — no backlight required, leading to *true blacks* and *infinite contrast ratio*.
2. **Thinner, lighter, and flexible** — the organic stack can be deposited on flexible polymer substrates, enabling curved and rollable displays (e.g., LG Signature OLED R).
3. *(Bonus point)* Faster response time (< 0.1 ms) — eliminates motion blur.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 1, Q1(b)]**
> **Q2.** What is a **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)**? Why is it called a *memory device*?

**Model Answer:**
A **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** is a flat-panel emissive display in which each pixel is a tiny cell filled with an ionizable **noble gas mixture** (Ne + Xe). An electrical discharge ionizes the gas into plasma, which emits **UV photons** that excite RGB **phosphors** to produce visible light.

It is called a **memory device** because of the **wall-charge effect** of the dielectric layer in **AC-PDP**:
- Once a sustain pulse has fired the cell, the accumulated wall charges keep the cell in the "ON" state.
- The next sustain pulse re-ignites the discharge without re-addressing.
- This eliminates the need to re-write the data for every frame as long as the state is unchanged, reducing power and driver complexity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 1, Q2 (Choice Based)]**
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **OLED display**. Compare **PMOLED** and **AMOLED** architectures.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **emission wavelength equation** for an LED and compute the band-gap of a green LED emitting at **530 nm**.

---

**Solution to Q2(a):**

**Construction (Block Diagram — refer Section 4.3):**

A typical OLED stack from bottom to top consists of:
1. **Substrate** — glass or flexible polyimide (PI).
2. **Anode** — Indium Tin Oxide (ITO) deposited by sputtering; transparent to allow light exit.
3. **Hole Injection Layer (HIL)** — e.g., **PEDOT:PSS**; reduces hole-injection barrier.
4. **Hole Transport Layer (HTL)** — e.g., **NPB**; transports holes to EML.
5. **Emissive Layer (EML)** — doped with phosphorescent **Ir(ppy)₃** for green, **Ir(MDQ)₂(acac)** for red, **FIrpic** for blue; this is where excitons decay radiatively.
6. **Electron Transport Layer (ETL)** — e.g., **Alq₃**; transports electrons to EML.
7. **Electron Injection Layer (EIL)** — e.g., **LiF** (1 nm).
8. **Cathode** — thin Al or Mg-Ag alloy; reflective.
9. **Encapsulation** — glass lid or thin-film barrier to prevent H₂O/O₂ ingress (degrades organics).

**Working Principle:**
- Apply forward bias → holes injected from ITO through HIL/HTL, electrons injected from cathode through EIL/ETL.
- In the EML, electrons and holes form **excitons** (bound e⁻–h⁺ pairs).
- According to **spin statistics**, 25% are singlets, 75% are triplets.
- **Fluorescent OLEDs** harvest singlets only (~25% IQE).
- **Phosphorescent OLEDs (PHOLEDs)** with heavy-metal Ir complexes harvest both singlets and triplets (~100% IQE).
- Radiative decay → photon emission through the transparent ITO/substrate.

**PMOLED vs AMOLED Comparison:**

| Parameter | PMOLED | AMOLED |
|---|---|---|
| Addressing | Row-column multiplex (passive) | Each pixel has a TFT + storage cap (active) |
| Capacitor per pixel | None | 1 capacitor (Cs) |
| Resolution limit | ≤ 128 × 128 | > 4K UHD |
| Pixel current | High (peak brightness) | Low (TFT holds state) |
| Power efficiency | Poor for large displays | Excellent |
| Use case | Small character displays, MP3 players | Smartphones, OLED TVs, VR headsets |
| Cost | Low | Higher (LTPS or LTPO TFT backplane) |

---

**Solution to Q2(b):**

Refer to Section 3.1 (full derivation already shown). Final numerical computation:

Using the relation:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Plug in $hc = 1240\ \text{eV·nm}$ and $\lambda = 530\ \text{nm}$:

$$E_g = \dfrac{1240}{530} = 2.340\ \text{eV}$$

Converting to Joules:

$$E_g = 2.340 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 3.748 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$$

**Result:** The semiconductor material required is **InGaN** (with composition tuned to lower the band gap from 3.4 eV of pure GaN down to ~2.34 eV).

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **construction and working of a Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** with a neat diagram. Explain the role of **dielectric barrier** and **phosphor layers**.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of **Field Emission Display (FED)**. State and explain the **Fowler-Nordheim equation** with its physical significance.

---

**Solution to Q2B(a):**

**Construction (refer to Section 4.4):**

A PDP consists of two parallel glass plates separated by a ~100–200 µm gap:
- **Front plate** — transparent **ITO sustain electrodes** + bus electrodes, covered with a **dielectric layer** (e.g., low-melting glass frit, ~30 µm) and a thin **MgO protective layer** (~500 nm).
- **Rear plate** — **address electrodes** (orthogonal to front), **barrier ribs** (~150 µm tall, photolithographically patterned), and **RGB phosphor stripes** deposited between ribs.
- The gap is filled with a **Ne + Xe (4–8%)** gas mixture at ~500 Torr.

**Working Principle:**
1. **Address phase** — a write pulse between an address electrode and a scan electrode initiates a glow discharge at the intersection.
2. **Wall-charge formation** — ions and electrons accumulate on the dielectric, creating an internal bias that "stores" the cell state.
3. **Sustain phase** — alternating AC pulses across sustain electrodes continue the discharge as long as the cell is "ON" (memory effect).
4. **Light generation** — Xe atoms form Xe₂* excimers → VUV at 147 nm and 172 nm → excites phosphors:
   - **Green** phosphor: Zn₂SiO₄:Mn²⁺ (or BaAl₁₂O₁₉:Mn)
   - **Red** phosphor: Y₂O₃:Eu³⁺ (or Y(V,P)O₄:Eu)
   - **Blue** phosphor: BaMgAl₁₀O₁₇:Eu²⁺
5. **Erase phase** — a soft pulse neutralizes wall charges, turning the cell "OFF".

**Role of dielectric barrier:**
- Limits discharge current (avoids arcing).
- Stores wall charge for memory operation.
- Protects electrodes from ion bombardment.

**Role of phosphor layers:**
- Convert harmful VUV into visible RGB light.
- Stoke-shift absorbers (high-energy UV → lower-energy visible).
- Phosphor choice determines color gamut and aging rate.

---

**Solution to Q2B(b):**

**FED Principle (refer to Sections 2.4 and 4.5):**
A FED uses **cold cathode** field emission of electrons from micro-tip arrays (Spindt Mo cones or CNTs). Each sub-pixel is essentially a tiny CRT — electrons are field-emitted, accelerated across a small vacuum gap (~1–2 mm), and strike phosphor stripes on the anode.

**Fowler-Nordheim Equation:**

$$J = \dfrac{AE^2}{\phi} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B\phi^{3/2}}{E}\right)$$

where:
- $J$ = current density (A/m²)
- $E$ = applied electric field (V/m)
- $\phi$ = cathode work function (eV)
- $A \approx 1.541 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A·eV/V}^2$
- $B \approx 6.83 \times 10^{9}\ \text{eV}^{-3/2}\text{V/m}$

**Physical significance of each term:**

1. **Pre-exponential $A E^2 / \phi$** — represents the *supply* of electrons at the Fermi level. Scales with $E^2$ because higher field both bends the barrier more AND draws more carriers.

2. **Exponential $\exp(-B \phi^{3/2}/E)$** — represents the *probability* of quantum-mechanical tunnelling. The $3/2$ power in $\phi$ shows the work function has a *cubic-root-like* influence; small reductions in $\phi$ yield large emission gains.

3. **Sensitivity to $E$** — increasing $E$ linearly *inside* the exponential causes an *exponential* rise in current. Hence FED cathodes require *atomically sharp* tips to locally enhance $E$.

**Engineering implication:** Replacing Mo ($\phi \approx 4.6$ eV) with CNT ($\phi \approx 4.0$–4.3 eV) or coating tips with **Cs** or **HfC** reduces the work function and dramatically increases emission — this is why CNT-FED is the modern research focus.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "LED" with "OLED":** LED uses *inorganic* semiconductors; OLED uses *organic* molecules. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you write the wrong material system.
> 2. **Forgetting the band-gap unit:** Always express $E_g$ in **eV** for LED problems. Writing in Joules without conversion is a common blunder.
> 3. **PDP — saying "phosphor emits UV":** No! Plasma *generates* UV; *phosphor* converts UV to *visible*. Direction of energy is the reverse of what students often write.
> 4. **FED — calling the cathode a thermionic emitter:** FED cathodes are *cold* (no heating). Mixing up with CRT cathodes loses 2 marks instantly.
> 5. **Reflective displays — assuming they emit light:** They *reflect* ambient light. Stating "reflective displays are self-emissive" is a 3-mark killer.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram:** KTU values diagrams liberally. Even a simple block diagram with labels (anode, cathode, EML) fetches 1–2 marks before any text is read.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Display** = output device that converts electrical signals → light.
- [ ] **Emissive** displays (LED, OLED, PDP, FED) *generate* their own light.
- [ ] **Non-emissive** displays (Reflective LCD, E-Ink) *modulate* ambient light.
- [ ] **LED** → p-n junction, *inorganic*, band-gap $E_g$ determines colour; $\lambda = 1240/E_g$.
- [ ] **OLED** → *organic* thin films (Alq₃, NPB, Ir complexes), stack: ITO / HIL / HTL / EML / ETL / EIL / Metal cathode.
- [ ] **Phosphorescent OLEDs** harvest triplet excitons → ~100% IQE.
- [ ] **PDP** → Ne+Xe plasma → VUV (147/172 nm) → RGB phosphors → visible.
- [ ] **Dielectric barrier** in PDP stores wall charge → memory operation.
- [ ] **FED** → cold-cathode field emission from micro-tips or CNTs.
- [ ] **Fowler-Nordheim equation** governs FED: $J = \dfrac{AE^2}{\phi}\exp(-B\phi^{3/2}/E)$.
- [ ] **Reflective** displays → E-Ink (electrophoresis), reflective LCD, electrowetting, IMOD.
- [ ] **E-Ink is bi-stable** → zero power when image is static → ideal for e-readers.
- [ ] **Contrast ratio** = $L_{white}/L_{black}$; OLED gives ∞:1 (true black).
- [ ] **Frame-buffer bandwidth** = $3b \times W \times H \times f$; ~12 Gbps for 4K@60Hz, 8-bit.
- [ ] **PPI** = $\sqrt{W^2+H^2}/d_{diag}$; modern smartphones exceed 500 PPI.
- [ ] **HOMO/LUMO** in OLEDs ≈ valence/conduction bands in inorganic semiconductors.
- [ ] **Burn-in** is a real concern in OLED and PDP — phosphor/organic aging under static bright images.
- [ ] **AMOLED > PMOLED** in resolution, efficiency, and size — uses LTPS/LTPO TFT backplane.
- [ ] **DC-PDP vs AC-PDP** — AC has dielectric (memory), DC does not (simpler, lower life).
- [ ] **Plasma TVs are now discontinued** (LG stopped in 2015); question is still syllabus-relevant.
- [ ] **OLED advantage** in VR/AR: response < 0.1 ms eliminates motion sickness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random and Raster Scan Displays and Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Random and Raster Scan Displays and Systems

# Random and Raster Scan Displays and Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Display System?

A **Display System** in computer graphics is the integrated hardware-software mechanism that converts a digitally stored picture description (lines, polygons, pixels) into a visible optical image on a screen. The two foundational CRT-based (Cathode Ray Tube) families are **Random Scan** and **Raster Scan** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must understand the *fundamental difference* in how each system refreshes and redraws the screen, the role of the **Frame Buffer / Display Buffer**, and the architecture of the **Video Controller / Display Processor**.

---

### 1.2 Random Scan Display (Vector Display)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Random Scan Display** is a line-drawing display system in which the electron beam of the CRT is steered (deflected) in *arbitrary, non-sequential paths* to trace the endpoints of every line segment of a picture definition list. The picture is maintained by **continuous, picture-dependent refresh** at a constant rate (typically **30–60 Hz**).

**Components (KTU-Standard Architecture):**
1. **Display Controller (DC)**
2. **Display Generator (Vector Generator)**
3. **Display Buffer (Refresh Buffer) – stores the Display File**
4. **CRT Monitor**
5. **Keyboard (input device)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Display File (Display List):** A linked-list / sequential set of drawing commands (`LINE`, `MOVE`, `POINT`) that defines the picture. Each *line endpoint* is stored as `(x, y, intensity)`.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Etch-A-Sketch & Stencil Pen":**
Imagine you are an artist drawing a portrait on a glass window. You can lift your pen and jump *directly* to the tip of the nose, then to the ear, then to the chin — drawing **only the lines** that form the picture, skipping all the empty skin areas. The picture is defined as a *set of strokes*, not as a grid of dots. This is exactly how a **Random Scan** system works — the beam "jumps" to where lines must be drawn, irrespective of the order.

---

### 1.3 Raster Scan Display

**Formal Definition:**
A **Raster Scan Display** is a pixel-based (bitmap) display system in which the electron beam sweeps the *entire screen* in a fixed, sequential pattern — left-to-right, top-to-bottom — row by row. Each row is called a **scanline**. The image is stored as a matrix of pixel intensity values in a dedicated memory called the **Frame Buffer**.

**Components (KTU-Standard Architecture):**
1. **Frame Buffer (Bitmap Memory)**
2. **Video Controller (CRTC – Cathode Ray Tube Controller)**
3. **Display Monitor (CRT / LCD / LED)**
4. **Keyboard / Input Devices**

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Coloring-Book Grid & Photocopier":**
Imagine a giant grid graph paper where every tiny square is a pixel. To recreate a photograph, you color **every single square** one-by-one, row after row, from the top-left corner to the bottom-right — including the *empty white sky* and the *boring floor*. Whether or not the picture is complex, you scan the *whole sheet*. This is exactly the **Raster Scan** philosophy.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Frame Buffer** is the heart of a raster system. It is a contiguous, addressable memory where each bit (or group of bits) corresponds to one pixel's intensity/color on the screen.

---

### 1.4 Why Two Systems? — A Quick Perspective

| Parameter | Random Scan | Raster Scan |
|---|---|---|
| Era of Dominance | 1960s–1970s | 1970s–Present |
| Typical Use | CAD, early vector games (Asteroids) | TVs, monitors, smartphones, modern GPUs |
| Flicker Problem | Severe (high refresh cost) | Solved by high refresh rate (**60–240 Hz**) |
| Best For | **Line drawings, schematics** | **Photorealistic, shaded, textured scenes** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Random Scan Display — Operational Deep Dive

The picture is defined in the **Display File (DF)** as a list of drawing commands. The cycle is:

**Step 1 — Picture Definition:** The application program issues drawing commands. The *Display Controller* (DC) stores each command in the display buffer along with its endpoints and intensity.

**Step 2 — Continuous Refresh Loop:** The DC *repeatedly* traverses the display file, sending coordinate pairs to the *Display Generator*. For a `LINE` command, the generator produces an analog deflection signal that sweeps the electron beam from `(x1, y1)` to `(x2, y2)`. For a `MOVE` (pen-up), the beam jumps unblanked.

**Step 3 — Why "Random"?** Because the beam does not follow a fixed scan pattern. It visits the *endpoints* in any order in which the lines were defined. Hence the name **"Random Scan"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Drawback:** As the number of lines in the picture file increases, the *time to redraw the entire picture* in one refresh cycle increases linearly. Beyond ~30,000 lines (or whatever the timing budget allows), the refresh frequency falls below the flicker-fusion threshold of the human eye (**~50–60 Hz**), and the image visibly flickers.

**Picture definition storage in Random Scan:**
A line in the DF is stored as:

$$
\text{DF Entry} = \{\, opcode,\ x_1,\ y_1,\ x_2,\ y_2,\ \text{intensity} \,\}
$$

The total size of the display file grows as:

$$
S_{\text{DF}} = N_{\text{lines}} \times (\text{Bytes per entry})
$$

If each entry uses, say, **10 bytes** (1 opcode + 4 coordinates × 2 bytes + 1 intensity), then for **5000 lines**:

$$
S_{\text{DF}} = 5000 \times 10 = 50{,}000 \ \text{bytes} = \mathbf{50\ KB}
$$

---

## 2.2 Raster Scan Display — Operational Deep Dive

The **Video Controller** reads the frame buffer sequentially and synchronously with the CRT's electron gun. The cycle is:

**Step 1 — Image Rendering:** A graphics application rasterizes geometric primitives into pixel color values and writes them into the **Frame Buffer** (via the system bus / GPU).

**Step 2 — Synchronous Refresh:** The Video Controller (CRTC) maintains two counters — **Row Counter** and **Pixel Counter**. Every scanline, it reads one row of pixels from the frame buffer and outputs the analog video signal.

**Step 3 — Retrace:** After completing one row, the beam *retreats* to the left edge (**Horizontal Retrace**). After completing all rows, it retreats to the top-left (**Vertical Retrace**). During retrace, the beam is blanked (turned off) to avoid drawing a visible diagonal line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the screen "not flicker" with raster scan even at 60 Hz?** Because the *entire screen* is redrawn in a single fixed time interval, regardless of picture complexity. The CRTC guarantees a steady cadence.

### 2.2.1 Interlaced vs. Non-Interlaced Raster Scan

- **Non-Interlaced (Progressive):** All scanlines are drawn top-to-bottom in a single pass per frame.
- **Interlaced:** Frame is split into two **fields**:
  - **Odd Field:** Lines 1, 3, 5, … (odd scanlines)
  - **Even Field:** Lines 2, 4, 6, … (even scanlines)
  - Both fields are drawn in $\frac{1}{60}$ sec each, giving an effective frame rate of **30 Hz** but a field rate of **60 Hz** (less perceived flicker on early TVs).

### 2.2.2 Resolution, Aspect Ratio & Color Depth

Let the frame buffer address space be of dimensions $X$ (width) $\times$ $Y$ (height) in pixels. Each pixel is encoded with $b$ bits per pixel (bpp) for color/intensity.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Pixels (Resolution)** | $N_p = X \times Y$ | pixels | e.g., $1920 \times 1080$ |
| 2 | **Frame Buffer Size (bits)** | $F_b = X \times Y \times b$ | bits | $b$ = bits per pixel |
| 3 | **Frame Buffer Size (bytes)** | $F_b = \dfrac{X \cdot Y \cdot b}{8}$ | bytes | divide by 8 |
| 4 | **Aspect Ratio (AR)** | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{X}{Y}$ | dimensionless | 16:9 → 1.778, 4:3 → 1.333 |
| 5 | **Refresh Bandwidth** | $B_{\text{refresh}} = X \cdot Y \cdot b \cdot f_r$ | bits/sec | $f_r$ = refresh rate (Hz) |
| 6 | **Time per Scanline** | $t_{\text{row}} = \dfrac{1}{f_r \cdot Y}$ | seconds | horizontal period |
| 7 | **Time per Pixel** | $t_{\text{pix}} = \dfrac{1}{f_r \cdot X \cdot Y}$ | seconds | pixel clock period |
| 8 | **Refresh Frequency for Random Scan** | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{N_{\text{lines}} \cdot t_{\text{line}}}$ | Hz | $t_{\text{line}}$ = time to draw one line |
| 9 | **Field Rate (Interlaced)** | $f_{\text{field}} = 2 \cdot f_{\text{frame}}$ | Hz | two fields per frame |
| 10 | **Display File Size** | $S_{\text{DF}} = N_{\text{lines}} \times \text{Bytes/line}$ | bytes | Random Scan |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All absolute-value bars in the formulas above (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written using `\vert` or `\mid` so they do not break the markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAD & Engineering Schematics:** Random scan systems were historically preferred for crisp, infinitely-scalable **wireframe drawings** of machine parts and circuit boards because lines stayed sharp at any zoom level.
- **Modern Gaming & Medical Imaging:** Raster scan systems (now driven by GPUs) render **photorealistic, shaded 3D scenes** with anti-aliasing, textures, and lighting — impossible in pure vector displays.
- **LCD/LED/AMOLED Flat Panels:** These are *fundamentally* raster devices — each sub-pixel is a tiny addressable light source refreshed from a frame buffer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Frame Buffer Size Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A raster scan display has a resolution of **1280 × 1024** pixels with **24-bit true-color** depth. It is refreshed at **75 Hz**. Calculate:
1. Total number of pixels.
2. Frame buffer size in bits, bytes, kilobytes, and megabytes.
3. Refresh bandwidth in bits per second and MB/s.
4. Time per scanline.
5. Time per pixel (pixel clock period).

### Step 1 — Total Number of Pixels
The resolution is $X = 1280$ and $Y = 1024$.

$$
N_p = X \times Y = 1280 \times 1024
$$

**Conversion logic:** 1280 is a multiple of 128, and 1024 = $2^{10}$. So we can break this down:

$$
1280 \times 1024 = (10 \times 128) \times 1024 = 10 \times 131{,}072 = \mathbf{1{,}310{,}720\ \text{pixels}}
$$

### Step 2 — Frame Buffer Size
Color depth $b = 24$ bits per pixel.

$$
F_b\ (\text{bits}) = N_p \times b = 1{,}310{,}720 \times 24
$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**

$$
1{,}310{,}720 \times 24 = 1{,}310{,}720 \times 20 + 1{,}310{,}720 \times 4
$$

$$
= 26{,}214{,}400 + 5{,}242{,}880 = \mathbf{31{,}457{,}280\ \text{bits}}
$$

Convert to bytes (divide by 8):

$$
F_b\ (\text{bytes}) = \dfrac{31{,}457{,}280}{8} = \mathbf{3{,}932{,}160\ \text{bytes}}
$$

Convert to kilobytes ($1\ \text{KB} = 1024\ \text{bytes}$):

$$
F_b\ (\text{KB}) = \dfrac{3{,}932{,}160}{1024} = \mathbf{3840\ \text{KB}}
$$

Convert to megabytes ($1\ \text{MB} = 1024\ \text{KB}$):

$$
F_b\ (\text{MB}) = \dfrac{3840}{1024} = \mathbf{3.75\ \text{MB}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU examiners often accept the decimal approximation $1\ \text{MB} = 10^6\ \text{bytes}$ as well. Always state your assumption. Using the decimal convention: $31{,}457{,}280 / 8 / 10^6 = 3.93\ \text{MB}$.

### Step 3 — Refresh Bandwidth
Refresh rate $f_r = 75\ \text{Hz}$.

$$
B_{\text{refresh}} = N_p \times b \times f_r = 1{,}310{,}720 \times 24 \times 75
$$

**Computing in stages:**

$$
1{,}310{,}720 \times 24 = 31{,}457{,}280\ \text{bits/frame}
$$

$$
31{,}457{,}280 \times 75 = 31{,}457{,}280 \times 100 - 31{,}457{,}280 \times 25
$$

$$
= 3{,}145{,}728{,}000 - 786{,}432{,}000 = \mathbf{2{,}359{,}296{,}000\ \text{bits/s}}
$$

$$
\approx \mathbf{2.36\ \text{Gbps}}
$$

Convert to MB/s (decimal, $1\ \text{MB} = 10^6$ bytes):

$$
B_{\text{refresh}}\ (\text{MB/s}) = \dfrac{2{,}359{,}296{,}000}{8 \times 10^6} = \mathbf{294.91\ \text{MB/s}}
$$

### Step 4 — Time per Scanline
The whole frame takes $\dfrac{1}{f_r}$ seconds, divided equally among $Y$ scanlines.

$$
t_{\text{row}} = \dfrac{1}{f_r \times Y} = \dfrac{1}{75 \times 1024} = \dfrac{1}{76{,}800}\ \text{seconds}
$$

$$
t_{\text{row}} \approx \mathbf{13.02\ \mu s}
$$

### Step 5 — Pixel Clock Period
The time spent drawing one pixel:

$$
t_{\text{pix}} = \dfrac{1}{f_r \times X \times Y} = \dfrac{1}{75 \times 1{,}310{,}720} = \dfrac{1}{98{,}304{,}000}\ \text{s}
$$

$$
t_{\text{pix}} \approx \mathbf{10.17\ \text{ns}}
$$

This corresponds to a pixel clock frequency of:

$$
f_{\text{pix}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{\text{pix}}} \approx \mathbf{98.3\ \text{MHz}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Random Scan Refresh Frequency

**Problem Statement:**
A random scan display contains a picture defined by **3000 line segments**. The display generator can draw each line in **2 microseconds** and the pen-up "move" between endpoints takes **0.5 microseconds** on average. Calculate:
1. Total time to redraw the entire picture once.
2. Resulting refresh frequency.
3. Comment on whether the image will appear stable.

### Step 1 — Time per Line (Draw + Move)
Total time for one segment = draw time + move time.

$$
t_{\text{line}} = t_{\text{draw}} + t_{\text{move}} = 2.0\ \mu s + 0.5\ \mu s = \mathbf{2.5\ \mu s}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Refresh Time for N = 3000 Lines

$$
T_{\text{frame}} = N_{\text{lines}} \times t_{\text{line}} = 3000 \times 2.5\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{\text{frame}} = 7500\ \mu s = \mathbf{7.5\ \text{ms}}
$$

### Step 3 — Refresh Frequency

$$
f_r = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{frame}}} = \dfrac{1}{7.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx \mathbf{133.33\ \text{Hz}}
$$

### Step 4 — Comment on Stability
The human flicker-fusion threshold is roughly **60 Hz**. Since $133.33\ \text{Hz} > 60\ \text{Hz}$, the image will appear perfectly stable and flicker-free. (As line count grows, $T_{\text{frame}}$ grows, and the image will eventually flicker.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Random Scan vs Raster Scan Trade-off

| Property | Random Scan | Raster Scan |
|---|---|---|
| Picture Definition | Vector / line list | Bitmap / pixel matrix |
| Beam Path | Random, line-by-line | Fixed, scanline-by-scanline |
| Refresh | Picture-dependent (slower for complex) | Picture-independent (fixed) |
| Flicker on Complex Images | **Likely** | Negligible |
