The initial value of $C$ is 0. ### 2.1.2 Trivial Acceptance & Rejection Tests Let $C_1$ and $C_2$ be the outcodes of endpoints $P_1$ and $P_2$. $$ \text{Trivial Accept} : \quad C_1 \;|\; C_2 = 0 $$ $$ \text{Trivial Reject} : \quad C_1 \;\&\; C_2 \neq 0 $$ $$ \text{Partial} : \quad C_1 \;\&\; C_2 = 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad C_1 \;|\; C_2 \neq 0 $$ > [!NOTE] > In the **trivial reject** test, the bitwise AND of two outcodes is non-zero **only if both endpoints share at least one non-zero bit**, i.e., they lie on the *same outer side* of at least one edge. This means the line **cannot** cross the window. ### 2.1.3 Intersection Formula When the test is **partial**, an endpoint with a non-zero outcode is chosen. The boundary it violates is identified by inspecting the bits. The intersection with that boundary is computed using the **parametric line equation**: $$ x = x_1 + t \cdot (x_2 - x_1), \quad y = y_1 + t \cdot (y_2 - y_1) $$ Solving for the parameter $t$ for each boundary: $$ t_{LEFT} = \frac{x_{min} - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}, \quad t_{RIGHT} = \frac{x_{max} - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} $$ $$ t_{BOTTOM} = \frac{y_{min} - y_1}{y_2 - y_1}, \quad t_{TOP} = \frac{y_{max} - y_1}{y_2 - y_1} $$ The new intersection point is obtained by substituting $t$ back into the line equation. The endpoint is replaced and outcodes are recomputed. The process iterates until trivial accept or trivial reject. ## 2.2 Midpoint Subdivision Line Clipping Algorithm ### 2.2.1 The Recursive Bisection Principle The line segment $P_1 P_2$ is repeatedly divided at its **midpoint** $P_m$: $$ P_m = \left( \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2}, \; \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2} \right) $$ A **visibility test** (using a simple inside-check or an outcode) is applied to $P_m$. Based on the outcomes for $P_1$, $P_m$, and $P_2$, the algorithm decides which sub-segments to keep and which to discard. ### 2.2.2 The Decision Logic | $P_1$ status | $P_2$ status | Action | |---|---|---| | Inside | Inside | Trivial Accept — keep entire segment. | | Outside | Outside (same region) | Trivial Reject — discard. | | Outside | Outside (different region) | Take midpoint $P_m$. If $P_m$ inside → keep both halves. If $P_m$ outside → subdivide further. | | One inside, one outside | — | Recursively subdivide the half that contains the boundary crossing. | ### 2.2.3 Termination Conditions The algorithm terminates when: 1. Both endpoints of a sub-segment are inside the window. 2. Both endpoints are provably outside on the same side. 3. The distance between endpoints falls below a sub-pixel tolerance. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Midpoint Subdivision** algorithm has time complexity of order $O(\log n)$ where $n$ is the line length in pixels. It is highly suitable for **parallel hardware implementation** because each subdivision step is independent. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity / Test** | **Formula / Condition** | **Meaning** | |---|---|---| | Outcode bit — LEFT | $D_0 = 1$ if $x < x_{min}$ | Endpoint lies left of window | | Outcode bit — RIGHT | $D_1 = 1$ if $x > x_{max}$ | Endpoint lies right of window | | Outcode bit — BOTTOM | $D_2 = 1$ if $y < y_{min}$ | Endpoint lies below window | | Outcode bit — TOP | $D_3 = 1$ if $y > y_{max}$ | Endpoint lies above window | | Trivial Accept | $C_1 \mid C_2 = 0000$ | Both endpoints inside | | Trivial Reject | $C_1 \;\&\; C_2 \neq 0000$ | Both endpoints on same outer side | | Parametric $t$ at $x = x_k$ | $t = (x_k - x_1)/(x_2 - x_1)$ | $t \in [0,1]$ for segment intersection | | Parametric $t$ at $y = y_k$ | $t = (y_k - y_1)/(y_2 - y_1)$ | Used for horizontal boundary intersections | | Midpoint coordinates | $P_m = ((x_1 + x_2)/2, (y_1 + y_2)/2)$ | Bisects the line segment | | C-S complexity | $O(1)$ average, $O(n)$ worst | For a window with $n$ edges crossed | | Midpoint complexity | $O(\log_2 L)$ | $L$ = line length in pixel units | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **GUI Rendering Pipelines:** All modern GUIs (Windows, macOS, Android UI) use Cohen-Sutherland variants during window redraw to avoid drawing pixels outside the visible viewport. - **CAD/CAM Software:** AutoCAD and SolidWorks apply 2D clipping to multi-viewport drawings so that the geometry of one viewport does not bleed into another. - **Game Engine View Frustum Culling:** Although operating in 3D, the *logic of trivial-accept/trivial-reject* with bitwise codes is the conceptual ancestor of frustum culling tests. - **Map Rendering (GIS):** Map servers use line clipping to display only the road segments within the user's current zoom rectangle. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Cohen-Sutherland Algorithm > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement (KTU Exam Standard):** A line segment has endpoints $P_1(2, 5)$ and $P_2(10, 4)$. The clipping window is defined by $x_{min} = 4$, $y_{min} = 2$, $x_{max} = 8$, $y_{max} = 6$. Apply the **Cohen-Sutherland** algorithm to clip the line against the window. Show all outcodes and intersection steps. ### Step 1 — Define the Window Boundaries $$ x_{min} = 4, \quad y_{min} = 2, \quad x_{max} = 8, \quad y_{max} = 6 $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Outcode of $P_1(2, 5)$ Test each condition: - $y > y_{max}$? $\;5 > 6\;$ → **False** (TOP bit clear) - $y < y_{min}$? $\;5 < 2\;$ → **False** (BOTTOM bit clear) - $x > x_{max}$? $\;2 > 8\;$ → **False** (RIGHT bit clear) - $x < x_{min}$? $\;2 < 4\;$ → **True** (LEFT bit set, value 1) $$ C_1 = 0000 \;|\; 0001 = 0001 $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Outcode of $P_2(10, 4)$ Test each condition: - $y > y_{max}$? $\;4 > 6\;$ → **False** - $y < y_{min}$? $\;4 < 2\;$ → **False** - $x > x_{max}$? $\;10 > 8\;$ → **True** (RIGHT bit set, value 2) - $x < x_{min}$? $\;10 < 4\;$ → **False** $$ C_2 = 0000 \;|\; 0010 = 0010 $$ ### Step 4 — Apply Trivial Accept / Reject Tests $$ C_1 \;\&\; C_2 = 0001 \;\&\; 0010 = 0000 \quad \text{(NOT trivial reject)} $$ $$ C_1 \;|\; C_2 = 0001 \;|\; 0010 = 0011 \neq 0000 \quad \text{(NOT trivial accept)} $$ Conclusion: **Partial** — the line crosses the window. Proceed to intersection. ### Step 5 — Identify and Clamp the Outside Endpoint $C_1 = 0001$ — the **LEFT** bit is set, so $P_1$ is outside the *left* boundary $x = x_{min} = 4$. Using the parametric equation to find the intersection with $x = 4$: $$ t = \frac{x_{min} - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{4 - 2}{10 - 2} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25 $$ Substitute $t = 0.25$ back to find the $y$ coordinate of the intersection point: $$ y = y_1 + t \cdot (y_2 - y_1) = 5 + 0.25 \cdot (4 - 5) = 5 + 0.25 \cdot (-1) = 4.75 $$ $$ P_1' = (4, \; 4.75) $$ ### Step 6 — Recompute Outcode of New $P_1'$ - $y = 4.75 \in [2, 6]$ → TOP/BOTTOM bits clear - $x = 4 \geq x_{min}$ and $x = 4 \leq x_{max}$ → LEFT/RIGHT bits clear $$ C_1' = 0000 $$ ### Step 7 — Re-apply Trivial Test $$ C_1' \;|\; C_2 = 0000 \;|\; 0010 = 0010 $$ This is **non-zero**, but we only need both endpoints inside. Since $C_1' = 0000$ is inside, we are **NOT in trivial reject** ($C_1' \;\&\; C_2 = 0$). To be safe, we re-examine: $P_1'$ is now inside the window, and $P_2$ is outside to the right. The line must be clipped against the **RIGHT** boundary. ### Step 8 — Clip Against the Right Boundary $C_2 = 0010$ — the **RIGHT** bit is set. Find intersection with $x = x_{max} = 8$: $$ t = \frac{x_{max} - x_1'}{x_2 - x_1'} = \frac{8 - 4}{10 - 4} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667 $$ $$ y = y_1' + t \cdot (y_2 - y_1') = 4.75 + \frac{2}{3} \cdot (4 - 4.75) = 4.75 + \frac{2}{3} \cdot (-0.75) = 4.75 - 0.5 = 4.25 $$ $$ P_2' = (8, \; 4.25) $$ ### Step 9 — Final Trivial Accept $$ C_2' = 0000 \quad \text{(since } y=4.25 \in [2,6] \text{ and } x=8 \leq 8\text{)} $$ Now $C_1' = 0000$ and $C_2' = 0000$, so: $$ C_1' \;\&\; C_2' = 0000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TRIVIAL ACCEPT} $$ ### Step 10 — Final Clipped Line $$ \boxed{P_1'(4, \; 4.75) \;\; \text{to} \;\; P_2'(8, \; 4.25)} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example — Midpoint Subdivision Algorithm > [!IMPORTANT] > **Same Problem:** Clip $P_1(2, 5)$ to $P_2(10, 4)$ against window $(4, 2)$ to $(8, 6)$ using **Midpoint Subdivision**. ### Iteration 1 — Compute the Midpoint $$ P_m = \left( \frac{2 + 10}{2}, \; \frac{5 + 4}{2} \right) = (6, \; 4.5) $$ **Visibility Test for $P_m$:** $x=6 \in [4,8]$ and $y=4.5 \in [2,6]$ → **Inside**. ### Iteration 2 — Sub-Segment $P_1$–$P_m$ $P_1(2, 5)$ is **outside** (x < 4). $P_m(6, 4.5)$ is **inside**. The boundary lies inside this sub-segment. Midpoint of sub-segment: $$ P_{m1} = \left( \frac{2 + 6}{2}, \; \frac{5 + 4.5}{2} \right) = (4, \; 4.75) $$ **Visibility Test:** $x=4 \geq x_{min}$ and $y=4.75 \in [2,6]$ → **Inside** (on the left boundary). Now $P_1$ is outside and $P_{m1}$ is inside. Sub-divide again: $$ P_{m2} = \left( \frac{2 + 4}{2}, \; \frac{5 + 4.75}{2} \right) = (3, \; 4.875) $$ **Visibility Test:** $x=3 < 4$ → **Outside**. Since both $P_1$ and $P_{m2}$ are outside and both are on the **LEFT** side, this sub-sub-segment can be **rejected** in part. We replace the outside endpoint with the inside one: new $P_1$ for this sub-segment is $P_{m1}(4, 4.75)$. ### Iteration 3 — Sub-Segment $P_m$–$P_2$ $P_m(6, 4.5)$ is **inside**. $P_2(10, 4)$ is **outside** (x > 8). Midpoint of sub-segment: $$ P_{m3} = \left( \frac{6 + 10}{2}, \; \frac{4.5 + 4}{2} \right) = (8, \; 4.25) $$ **Visibility Test:** $x=8 \leq x_{max}$ and $y=4.25 \in [2,6]$ → **Inside** (on the right boundary). By similar logic, $P_2$ is replaced with $P_{m3}(8, 4.25)$. ### Final Result — Midpoint Subdivision $$ \boxed{P_1'(4, \; 4.75) \;\; \text{to} \;\; P_2'(8, \; 4.25)} $$ > [!NOTE] > Both algorithms yield the **same clipped line segment**, confirming correctness. Cohen-Sutherland reaches the answer in **2 intersection computations**, while midpoint subdivision required **3 bisections** to reach a sub-pixel equivalent endpoint. ## 3.3 Algorithm Comparison Matrix | **Aspect** | **Cohen-Sutherland** | **Midpoint Subdivision** | |---|---|---| | Approach | Analytical (closed-form) | Numerical (recursive bisection) | | Outcode use | Yes — 4-bit per endpoint | Optional — can use a simple inside-test | | Number of steps | At most 4 (one per boundary) | $O(\log_2 L)$ bisections | | Numerical precision | High (parametric $t$) | Subject to sub-pixel approximation | | Hardware parallelization | Limited | Excellent (divide-and-conquer) | | Floating point operations | Few divisions per step | Only averaging (no division) | | Robustness for very long lines | Excellent | Good if tolerance is well chosen | | KTU exam frequency | Very high (favourite topic) | High (especially in derivations) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Region Division Diagram — 9-Region Outcode Map ```mermaid graph TB classDef insideRegion fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outsideRegion fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef topRegion fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 regionTL["TOP-LEFT Region
Outcode = 1001"] regionT["TOP Region
Outcode = 1000"] regionTR["TOP-RIGHT Region
Outcode = 1010"] regionL["LEFT Region
Outcode = 0001"] regionC["CENTRAL WINDOW
Outcode = 0000"] regionR["RIGHT Region
Outcode = 0010"] regionBL["BOTTOM-LEFT Region
Outcode = 0101"] regionB["BOTTOM Region
Outcode = 0100"] regionBR["BOTTOM-RIGHT Region
Outcode = 0110"] regionTL --- regionT --- regionTR regionL --- regionC --- regionR regionBL --- regionB --- regionBR class regionC insideRegion class regionTL,regionTR,regionBL,regionBR outsideRegion class regionT,regionL,regionR,regionB topRegion ``` > [!NOTE] > The **CENTRAL WINDOW** has outcode `0000`. The four cardinal edge regions each have a single bit set. The four corner regions have two bits set. This **2D outcode grid** is the backbone of the Cohen-Sutherland test. ## 4.2 Cohen-Sutherland Algorithm Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Read P1, P2, and Window"] --> B["Compute Outcode C1 for P1"] B --> C["Compute Outcode C2 for P2"] C --> D{"C1 OR C2 = 0000 ?"} D -->|Yes| E["Trivial Accept
Draw P1 to P2
STOP"] D -->|No| F{"C1 AND C2 ≠ 0000 ?"} F -->|Yes| G["Trivial Reject
Line Outside
STOP"] F -->|No| H["Pick outside endpoint
(non-zero outcode)"] H --> I["Identify boundary violated
by bit inspection"] I --> J["Compute intersection
with boundary
using parametric t"] J --> K["Replace outside endpoint
with intersection point"] K --> L["Recompute outcode
of new endpoint"] L --> C classDef startStop fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef success fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fail fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,E startStop class D,F decision class B,C,H,I,J,K,L process class E success class G fail ``` ## 4.3 Midpoint Subdivision Algorithm Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Start: Read P1, P2, and Window"] --> B0["Visibility test on P1"] A0 --> C0["Visibility test on P2"] B0 --> D0{"P1 inside?"} C0 --> E0{"P2 inside?"} D0 -->|Yes| F0["Store as endpoint"] D0 -->|No| G0["Mark as outside"] E0 -->|Yes| F0 E0 -->|No| G0 F0 --> H0{"Both inside?"} G0 --> H0 H0 -->|Yes| I0["Trivial Accept
Draw segment
STOP"] H0 -->|No| J0{"Both outside
same region?"} J0 -->|Yes| K0["Trivial Reject
Discard segment
STOP"] J0 -->|No| L0["Compute midpoint Pm"] L0 --> M0{"Pm inside window?"} M0 -->|Yes| N0["Subdivide into
P1-Pm and Pm-P2
Recurse on each"] M0 -->|No| O0["Discard half not
containing visible portion
Recurse on other half"] N0 --> A0 O0 --> A0 classDef startStop fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef success fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fail fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A0,I0 startStop class D0,E0,H0,J0,M0 decision class B0,C0,F0,G0,L0,N0,O0 process class I0 success class K0 fail ``` ## 4.4 Visual Block Diagram — End-to-End Line Clipping Pipeline ```mermaid graph LR A["Input Line Segment
P1 to P2"] --> B["Pre-Processing
Read Window Boundaries"] B --> C["Region Classifier
Compute Outcodes C1, C2"] C --> D{"Trivial Accept
Test"} D -->|Yes| E["Output: Original Line"] D -->|No| F{"Trivial Reject
Test"} F -->|Yes| G["Output: Empty Set"] F -->|No| H["Boundary Intersection
Engine"] H --> I["Update Endpoint
Recompute Outcode"] I --> C classDef inputBlock fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef processBlock fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef decisionBlock fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputBlock fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef failBlock fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B inputBlock class C,H,I processBlock class D,F decisionBlock class E outputBlock class G failBlock ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice the **feedback loop** from "Update Endpoint" back to "Region Classifier". This iterative feedback is what makes both algorithms **converge** in a finite number of steps. The maximum iterations for Cohen-Sutherland is **4** (one per edge); for midpoint subdivision, it is $\lceil \log_2(\text{max pixel length}) \rceil$ sub-divisions. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]** **Define line clipping. What are trivial accept and trivial reject conditions in the Cohen-Sutherland algorithm? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Line clipping is the process of determining the portion of a line segment that lies inside a specified rectangular clipping window defined by $x_{min}, y_{min}, x_{max}, y_{max}$. For two endpoints $P_1$ and $P_2$ with outcodes $C_1$ and $C_2$: - **Trivial Accept** occurs when $C_1 \;|\; C_2 = 0000$ — both endpoints are inside the window, so the entire line is visible. - **Trivial Reject** occurs when $C_1 \;\&\; C_2 \neq 0000$ — both endpoints lie on the same outer side of at least one window edge, so the line cannot enter the window and is rejected in full. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]** **Explain the concept of outcodes used in the Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm. Mention the meaning of each of the four bits. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** An outcode is a 4-bit code assigned to a 2D point based on its position relative to the four edges of the clipping window. The bits are: | Bit | Region | Condition | |---|---|---| | $D_3$ (8) | TOP | $y > y_{max}$ | | $D_2$ (4) | BOTTOM | $y < y_{min}$ | | $D_1$ (2) | RIGHT | $x > x_{max}$ | | $D_0$ (1) | LEFT | $x < x_{min}$ | If a point lies inside the window, its outcode is `0000`. By computing outcodes for both endpoints of a line and applying bitwise operations, the algorithm quickly classifies the line as fully inside, fully outside, or partially intersecting the window. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3]** > A line segment has endpoints $P_1(2, 5)$ and $P_2(10, 4)$. The clipping window is defined by $x_{min} = 4$, $y_{min} = 2$, $x_{max} = 8$, $y_{max} = 6$. > > **(a)** Apply the **Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm** to clip the line against the window. Show all outcodes, intersection calculations, and the final clipped line. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **Midpoint Subdivision line clipping algorithm** in detail. Apply it to the **same line** $P_1(2, 5)$ to $P_2(10, 4)$ with the same window and show the first two subdivision iterations. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a) — Cohen-Sutherland **Step 1: Compute outcodes.** For $P_1(2, 5)$: - $y = 5 \leq 6$ → TOP bit (8) clear - $y = 5 \geq 2$ → BOTTOM bit (4) clear - $x = 2 \leq 8$ → RIGHT bit (2) clear - $x = 2 < 4$ → LEFT bit (1) **set** $$ C_1 = 0001 $$ For $P_2(10, 4)$: - $y = 4 \leq 6$ → TOP bit clear - $y = 4 \geq 2$ → BOTTOM bit clear - $x = 10 > 8$ → RIGHT bit (2) **set** - $x = 10 \geq 4$ → LEFT bit clear $$ C_2 = 0010 $$ **Step 2: Trivial tests.** $C_1 \;\&\; C_2 = 0001 \;\&\; 0010 = 0000$ — **not** trivial reject. $C_1 \;|\; C_2 = 0001 \;|\; 0010 = 0011 \neq 0$ — **not** trivial accept. **Step 3: Clip at LEFT boundary ($x = 4$).** $P_1$ is outside (LEFT bit set). Use parametric form: $$ t = \frac{x_{min} - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{4 - 2}{10 - 2} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25 $$ $$ y = 5 + 0.25 \cdot (4 - 5) = 5 - 0.25 = 4.75 $$ New $P_1' = (4, 4.75)$ with $C_1' = 0000$. **Step 4: Clip at RIGHT boundary ($x = 8$).** $P_2$ is outside (RIGHT bit set). Use parametric form: $$ t = \frac{x_{max} - x_1'}{x_2 - x_1'} = \frac{8 - 4}{10 - 4} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3} $$ $$ y = 4.75 + \frac{2}{3} \cdot (4 - 4.75) = 4.75 - 0.5 = 4.25 $$ New $P_2' = (8, 4.25)$ with $C_2' = 0000$. **Step 5: Trivial Accept.** $$ C_1' \;|\; C_2' = 0000 \Rightarrow \text{TRIVIAL ACCEPT} $$ **Final Clipped Line:** $$ \boxed{P_1'(4, 4.75) \;\; \text{to} \;\; P_2'(8, 4.25)} $$ #### Model Solution for Part (b) — Midpoint Subdivision **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Read endpoints $P_1, P_2$ and window boundaries. 2. Test visibility of each endpoint. 3. If both inside → trivial accept. 4. If both outside in same region → trivial reject. 5. Otherwise, compute midpoint $P_m$ and recurse on appropriate sub-segments. **Iteration 1:** Endpoints: $P_1(2, 5)$ — outside (x < 4). $P_2(10, 4)$ — outside (x > 8). Midpoint: $$ P_m = \left( \frac{2 + 10}{2}, \frac{5 + 4}{2} \right) = (6, 4.5) $$ Visibility of $P_m$: $x=6 \in [4,8]$, $y=4.5 \in [2,6]$ → **inside**. Result: Split into $P_1$–$P_m$ and $P_m$–$P_2$. **Iteration 2 — Sub-segment $P_1$–$P_m$:** $P_1$ outside, $P_m$ inside. Sub-divide: $$ P_{m1} = \left( \frac{2 + 6}{2}, \frac{5 + 4.5}{2} \right) = (4, 4.75) $$ Visibility: $x=4 \geq 4$ and $y=4.75 \in [2,6]$ → **inside** (on boundary). The outside endpoint $P_1$ is replaced by $P_{m1}(4, 4.75)$. **Iteration 2 — Sub-segment $P_m$–$P_2$:** $P_m$ inside, $P_2$ outside. Sub-divide: $$ P_{m2} = \left( \frac{6 + 10}{2}, \frac{4.5 + 4}{2} \right) = (8, 4.25) $$ Visibility: $x=8 \leq 8$ and $y=4.25 \in [2,6]$ → **inside** (on boundary). The outside endpoint $P_2$ is replaced by $P_{m2}(8, 4.25)$. **Final Clipped Line (after 2 iterations):** $$ \boxed{(4, 4.75) \;\; \text{to} \;\; (8, 4.25)} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Cohen-Sutherland:** Students often **forget to update the line equation** after replacing $P_1$ with $P_1'$ in Step 4. The denominator $x_2 - x_1$ must be recomputed using the **new** $P_1'$. Using the old $P_1$ leads to wrong intersection points and full mark deduction. Always redraw the parametric form with the **current** endpoint values. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternate Choice — KTU University Exam, Model Paper]** > **(a)** Explain the **Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm** with a clear block diagram. Compute the outcodes for the line $P_1(-2, 1)$ to $P_2(6, 8)$ against the clipping window $x_{min} = 0$, $y_{min} = 0$, $x_{max} = 5$, $y_{max} = 6$. Identify whether the line is trivially accepted, trivially rejected, or partially clipped. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** What are the **advantages and disadvantages** of the Cohen-Sutherland algorithm compared to the Midpoint Subdivision algorithm? Which one would you prefer for a **hardware-based clipper** and why? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a) **Outcode computation for $P_1(-2, 1)$:** - $y = 1 \leq 6$ → TOP clear - $y = 1 \geq 0$ → BOTTOM clear - $x = -2 \leq 5$ → RIGHT clear - $x = -2 < 0$ → LEFT **set** (value 1) $$ C_1 = 0001 $$ **Outcode computation for $P_2(6, 8)$:** - $y = 8 > 6$ → TOP **set** (value 8) - $y = 8 \geq 0$ → BOTTOM clear - $x = 6 > 5$ → RIGHT **set** (value 2) - $x = 6 \geq 0$ → LEFT clear $$ C_2 = 1000 \;|\; 0010 = 1010 $$ **Trivial Tests:** $$ C_1 \;\&\; C_2 = 0001 \;\&\; 1010 = 0000 \quad \Rightarrow \text{NOT trivial reject} $$ $$ C_1 \;|\; C_2 = 0001 \;|\; 1010 = 1011 \neq 0000 \quad \Rightarrow \text{NOT trivial accept} $$ **Conclusion:** The line is **partially clipped** and requires intersection calculations with the LEFT and TOP boundaries (the violated bits of the outside endpoints). #### Model Solution for Part (b) — Comparison | **Aspect** | **Cohen-Sutherland** | **Midpoint Subdivision** | |---|---|---| | Time complexity | Constant 4-iter max in worst case | $O(\log L)$ iterations | | Numerical precision | High (closed-form) | Approximate (bisection-based) | | Hardware friendliness | Moderate | **Excellent (parallelizable)** | | Code simplicity | Simple | More complex recursion | | Floating-point ops | Divisions needed | Only averages | **Preferred for hardware clipper: Midpoint Subdivision** because: - Each subdivision step is **independent** of the others → amenable to a pipeline or parallel array of bisection units. - The arithmetic involves only **averaging (addition and bit-shift right by 1)**, which is fast in fixed-point or integer hardware. - The logarithmic depth guarantees **bounded worst-case latency**, which is critical for real-time rendering pipelines. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Midpoint Subdivision:** Students often **stop after finding the first midpoint** and incorrectly declare "clipped" without recursing on both sub-segments. Always show the **decision logic**: if $P_m$ is inside, you must subdivide **both halves**; if $P_m$ is outside, you must **discard** the half that does not contain the boundary crossing. Skipping this step is a 2-mark penalty. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Line clipping** identifies the portion of a line inside a rectangular window $(x_{min}, y_{min}, x_{max}, y_{max})$. - ✅ An **outcode** is a **4-bit code** (TOP, BOTTOM, RIGHT, LEFT) describing a point's position relative to the window. - ✅ **Inside the window** → outcode = `0000`. - ✅ **Trivial Accept** condition: $C_1 \;|\; C_2 = 0000$. - ✅ **Trivial Reject** condition: $C_1 \;\&\; C_2 \neq 0000$. - ✅ Partial clipping requires **intersection with violated boundary** using parametric $t$. - ✅ Parametric $t$ formula at $x = x_k$: $t = (x_k - x_1)/(x_2 - x_1)$. - ✅ Parametric $t$ formula at $y = y_k$: $t = (y_k - y_1)/(y_2 - y_1)$. - ✅ **Maximum 4 intersection computations** for Cohen-Sutherland in the worst case. - ✅ **Midpoint Subdivision** uses recursive bisection: $P_m = ((x_1+x_2)/2, (y_1+y_2)/2)$. - ✅ **Time complexity of Midpoint Subdivision**: $O(\log_2 L)$ where $L$ is the line length in pixels. - ✅ **Midpoint Subdivision** is preferred for **hardware clipper** design due to parallelizability. - ✅ **Cohen-Sutherland** is preferred for **software** implementations due to its closed-form precision. - ✅ Both algorithms produce the **same final clipped segment** for the same input. - ✅ Always **recompute outcodes** after replacing an endpoint — this is a common exam error. - ✅ The **9-region outcode map** is the conceptual foundation of all C-S type clipping algorithms. - ✅ The Midpoint Subdivision algorithm is closely related to the **Cyrus-Beck** algorithm (parametric form for convex polygons).

topics.title: Sutherland-Hodgeman and Weiler-Atherton Polygon Clipping Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sutherland-Hodgeman and Weiler-Atherton Polygon Clipping Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — POLYGON CLIPPING ALGORITHMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polygon Clipping** is the process of removing portions of a polygon that lie **outside** a specified region of interest (called the *clipping window* or *clip polygon*), thereby producing a new polygon that represents the geometric intersection of the original subject polygon and the clipping region.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, polygon clipping is classified under the broader umbrella of **2D Geometric Transformations in the Viewing Pipeline**. While *line clipping* (e.g., Cohen–Sutherland) operates on individual line segments, polygon clipping must respect the **topological integrity** of the polygon — meaning the resulting clipped figure must remain a closed, ordered sequence of vertices defining one or more polygonal regions.

Two canonical algorithms dominate the KTU Module 3 syllabus:

| # | Algorithm | Clipping Region | Polygon Type | Era |
|---|-----------|-----------------|--------------|-----|
| 1 | **Sutherland–Hodgeman** | Convex (rectangular) | Convex polygon | 1974 |
| 2 | **Weiler–Atherton** | Arbitrary (concave/with holes) | Concave polygon | 1977 |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Window-Pane" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you have drawn a beautiful, irregular star-shaped figure on a piece of tracing paper using a thick marker. Now you overlay a rectangular picture-frame (the *clipping window*) on top. The visible portion of the drawing *inside* the frame is what you want to keep; the rest is "clipped" away. A **scissor** performs the cut, but a computer needs a *mathematical recipe* (algorithm) to decide exactly which corners of the star get sliced off and how the new straight edges are drawn across the cut. The **Sutherland–Hodgeman** algorithm is like cutting only against straight frame edges one at a time, while the **Weiler–Atherton** algorithm is sophisticated enough to cut against a frame whose edges themselves are jagged (concave clip regions).

### Formal Definitions for KTU Board Answers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sutherland–Hodgeman Polygon Clipping Algorithm:** A vertex-by-vertex, sequential pipeline algorithm that clips a polygon against a *convex* clipping window by independently processing the polygon against each of the four (or more) clipping edges — **Left → Right → Bottom → Top** — and feeding the partially clipped output polygon into the next clipping stage as input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weiler–Atherton Polygon Clipping Algorithm:** A *traversal-based* algorithm that constructs the **polygon of intersection** between a (possibly concave) subject polygon and a (possibly concave) clip polygon. It works by introducing the concept of **"in–out" vertices** at polygon intersection points, then *tracing* along subject polygon edges until an exit point, after which it follows the *clip polygon* boundary in the reverse direction — building one or more *clipped sub-polygons*.

### Key Vocabulary (KTU Board Standard Terms)

- **Subject Polygon (P_s):** The original polygon to be clipped.
- **Clip Polygon (P_c):** The region that defines the visible window.
- **In-Vertices:** Vertices lying inside the clip region.
- **Out-Vertices:** Vertices lying outside the clip region.
- **Intersection Vertex (I-point):** A new vertex created where a polygon edge crosses a clip boundary.
- **Convex Clip Region:** A region where the line segment between any two interior points lies entirely inside the region (e.g., axis-aligned rectangle).
- **Concave Clip Region:** A region with at least one interior reflex angle (> 180°).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sutherland–Hodgeman — The Four Cases Per Edge

For every edge of the subject polygon, the algorithm tests the position of the **starting vertex (S)** and the **ending vertex (E)** relative to the current clipping edge. There are exactly **four (4) possible cases**:

| Case | S Position | E Position | Output Vertex(es) Emitted | Diagram Logic |
|:----:|:----------:|:----------:|:------------------------:|:-------------|
| **1** | Inside (IN) | Inside (IN) | **E** only | Edge fully retained |
| **2** | Inside (IN) | Outside (OUT) | **I** (intersection) only | Edge clipped at boundary |
| **3** | Outside (OUT) | Inside (IN) | **I** + **E** | Re-entry generates new vertex |
| **4** | Outside (OUT) | Outside (OUT) | *None* (∅) | Edge entirely discarded |

> [!NOTE]
> The function `inside(S, clipEdge)` returns **TRUE** if the test point S lies on the *interior side* of the clip edge. The function `intersect(S, E, clipEdge)` computes the geometric intersection point of the line segment S–E with the infinite line containing the clip edge.

### 2.2 The Four-Stage Pipeline

The clipping window is treated as **four half-planes** in sequence:

$$W = W_{\text{left}} \cap W_{\text{right}} \cap W_{\text{bottom}} \cap W_{\text{top}}$$

The order in KTU board convention is:

$$\boxed{\text{LEFT} \;\rightarrow\; \text{RIGHT} \;\rightarrow\; \text{BOTTOM} \;\rightarrow\; \text{TOP}}$$

The output polygon of stage $k$ becomes the **input** of stage $k+1$:

$$P_0 = P_s, \quad P_{k+1} = f_k(P_k), \quad P_{\text{clipped}} = P_4$$

where $f_k$ is the half-plane clipping function applied at stage $k$.

### 2.3 Inside-Test Inequalities (Axis-Aligned Rectangle)

For a clip rectangle with $x_{\min} = x_L$, $x_{\max} = x_R$, $y_{\min} = y_B$, $y_{\max} = y_T$:

| Clip Edge | Inside Test | Mathematical Form |
|:---------:|:-----------:|:-----------------:|
| **Left** ($x = x_L$) | $x \geq x_L$ | Inside half-plane extends rightward |
| **Right** ($x = x_R$) | $x \leq x_R$ | Inside half-plane extends leftward |
| **Bottom** ($y = y_B$) | $y \geq y_B$ | Inside half-plane extends upward |
| **Top** ($y = y_T$) | $y \leq y_T$ | Inside half-plane extends downward |

### 2.4 Intersection Computation (Parametric Form)

Given a line segment from $S = (x_s, y_s)$ to $E = (x_e, y_e)$, the parametric representation is:

$$P(t) = S + t(E - S), \quad t \in [0, 1]$$

$$P(t) = \bigl(x_s + t(x_e - x_s),\; y_s + t(y_e - y_s)\bigr)$$

To clip against a vertical edge $x = x_{\text{clip}}$, we set $x_s + t(x_e - x_s) = x_{\text{clip}}$ and solve:

$$\boxed{\;t = \dfrac{x_{\text{clip}} - x_s}{x_e - x_s}\;} \quad (x_e \neq x_s)$$

To clip against a horizontal edge $y = y_{\text{clip}}$:

$$\boxed{\;t = \dfrac{y_{\text{clip}} - y_s}{y_e - y_s}\;} \quad (y_e \neq y_s)$$

The intersection point follows directly:

$$\boxed{\;x_I = x_s + t(x_e - x_s)\;}, \quad \boxed{\;y_I = y_s + t(y_e - y_s)\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Degeneracy Guard:** If $x_e - x_s = 0$ (vertical line) when clipping against a vertical edge, the line is *parallel* to the clip edge — there is no intersection (or the line is entirely inside/outside). KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for not handling this case.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Used For |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------|:---------|
| 1 | Parametric $t$ for vertical clip | $t = (x_{\text{clip}} - x_s)/(x_e - x_s)$ | Left/Right edge intersection |
| 2 | Parametric $t$ for horizontal clip | $t = (y_{\text{clip}} - y_s)/(y_e - y_s)$ | Bottom/Top edge intersection |
| 3 | Sutherland–Hodgeman Case 1 | Output = $E$ | Both IN |
| 4 | Sutherland–Hodgeman Case 2 | Output = $I$ | IN → OUT |
| 5 | Sutherland–Hodgeman Case 3 | Output = $I, E$ | OUT → IN |
| 6 | Sutherland–Hodgeman Case 4 | Output = ∅ | Both OUT |
| 7 | Weiler–Atherton traversal | Follow subject, jump to clip on exit | Building intersection rings |
| 8 | Number of new vertices per stage | $\leq 2 \times$ input edges | Memory bound |

### 2.6 Weiler–Atherton — Theoretical Foundation

The algorithm operates on two polygons (subject $P_s$ and clip $P_c$) and produces the **set-intersection** $P_s \cap P_c$. The procedure:

1. **Vertex Insertion:** Insert all intersection points of $P_s$ edges with $P_c$ edges into both polygons' vertex lists. Mark each inserted point as either an **"in–out"** or **"out–in"** transition vertex, depending on the local geometry.
2. **Traversal Phase:** Pick an unprocessed **"in–out"** vertex (if none exists, the polygons are disjoint or one contains the other — trivial case). Follow the **subject polygon** in clockwise (or counter-clockwise) order, appending each encountered vertex to the output list, until another intersection vertex is reached.
3. **Switch Phase:** At the exit intersection, **switch** to traversing the *clip polygon* in the *reverse* direction, continuing to append vertices.
4. **Termination:** Continue the alternating subject/clip traversal until the traversal returns to the starting intersection vertex, completing one sub-polygon.
5. **Repeat** from step 2 for any remaining unvisited "in–out" vertices, until all are exhausted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key insight of Weiler–Atherton is that for **concave clip regions** or polygons with **holes**, the Sutherland–Hodgeman pipeline *fails* because clipping against multiple half-planes sequentially can produce *disconnected* phantom edges (the famous "T-vertex" problem). Weiler–Atherton avoids this by directly computing all intersections first and then *topologically* tracing the intersection boundary.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAD/CAM Systems:** Trimming 2D profiles of manufactured parts against die boundaries.
- **GIS / Map Rendering:** Clipping building footprints against administrative boundary polygons.
- **Video Game Engines:** Visibility determination — drawing only the portion of each game-object polygon that lies inside the camera's view frustum.
- **PCB Design:** Removing copper-trace polygons extending beyond the board outline.
- **Image Processing:** Region-of-interest (ROI) extraction in medical imaging.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Sutherland–Hodgeman

**Problem Statement (KTU Board Style):**
> Clip the polygon $P_s$ with vertices $(1, 1), (5, 1), (5, 5), (3, 7), (1, 5)$ against the rectangular window with $x_L = 2$, $x_R = 4$, $y_B = 2$, $y_T = 6$. Show the output of each pipeline stage.

**Given:**
- Subject Polygon $P_s$ vertices in order: $V_0 = (1, 1), V_1 = (5, 1), V_2 = (5, 5), V_3 = (3, 7), V_4 = (1, 5)$.
- Clip window: $x_L = 2, x_R = 4, y_B = 2, y_T = 6$.

---

#### STAGE 1 — Left Edge Clipping ($x \geq 2$)

The "Inside" half-plane is $x \geq x_L = 2$. We test each edge $S \rightarrow E$:

| Edge | $S$ | $E$ | S-pos | E-pos | Case | Output |
|:----:|:---:|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:------:|
| 1 | (1,1) | (5,1) | OUT | IN | **3** | $I_{1} + E$ |
| 2 | (5,1) | (5,5) | IN | IN | **1** | $E$ |
| 3 | (5,5) | (3,7) | IN | IN | **1** | $E$ |
| 4 | (3,7) | (1,5) | IN | OUT | **2** | $I_{2}$ |
| 5 | (1,5) | (1,1) | OUT | OUT | **4** | ∅ |

**Computing $I_1$ (intersection of segment $(1,1)\rightarrow(5,1)$ with $x=2$):**

$$t = \dfrac{x_L - x_s}{x_e - x_s} = \dfrac{2 - 1}{5 - 1} = \dfrac{1}{4}$$

$$x_{I_1} = 1 + \tfrac{1}{4}(5 - 1) = 1 + 1 = 2 \;\checkmark$$

$$y_{I_1} = 1 + \tfrac{1}{4}(1 - 1) = 1$$

$$\boxed{\,I_1 = (2,\;1)\,}$$

**Computing $I_2$ (intersection of segment $(3,7)\rightarrow(1,5)$ with $x=2$):**

$$t = \dfrac{2 - 3}{1 - 3} = \dfrac{-1}{-2} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$

$$x_{I_2} = 3 + \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - 3) = 3 - 1 = 2 \;\checkmark$$

$$y_{I_2} = 7 + \tfrac{1}{2}(5 - 7) = 7 - 1 = 6$$

$$\boxed{\,I_2 = (2,\;6)\,}$$

**After Stage 1 (Left):**

$$P_1 = \{\,I_1,\;(5,1),\;(5,5),\;(3,7),\;I_2\,\} = \{(2,1),\;(5,1),\;(5,5),\;(3,7),\;(2,6)\}$$

---

#### STAGE 2 — Right Edge Clipping ($x \leq 4$)

"Inside" is $x \leq 4$. Test each edge of $P_1$:

| Edge | $S$ | $E$ | S-pos | E-pos | Case | Output |
|:----:|:---:|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:------:|
| 1 | (2,1) | (5,1) | IN | OUT | **2** | $I_3$ |
| 2 | (5,1) | (5,5) | OUT | OUT | **4** | ∅ |
| 3 | (5,5) | (3,7) | OUT | IN | **3** | $I_4 + E$ |
| 4 | (3,7) | (2,6) | IN | IN | **1** | $E$ |
| 5 | (2,6) | (2,1) | IN | IN | **1** | $E$ |

**Computing $I_3$ (segment $(2,1)\rightarrow(5,1)$ vs $x=4$):**

$$t = \dfrac{4 - 2}{5 - 2} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

$$y_{I_3} = 1 + \tfrac{2}{3}(1 - 1) = 1$$

$$\boxed{\,I_3 = (4,\;1)\,}$$

**Computing $I_4$ (segment $(5,5)\rightarrow(3,7)$ vs $x=4$):**

$$t = \dfrac{4 - 5}{3 - 5} = \dfrac{-1}{-2} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$

$$x_{I_4} = 4 \;\checkmark, \quad y_{I_4} = 5 + \tfrac{1}{2}(7 - 5) = 6$$

$$\boxed{\,I_4 = (4,\;6)\,}$$

**After Stage 2 (Right):**

$$P_2 = \{(4,1),\;(4,6),\;(3,7),\;(2,6),\;(2,1)\}$$

---

#### STAGE 3 — Bottom Edge Clipping ($y \geq 2$)

"Inside" is $y \geq 2$. Test each edge of $P_2$:

| Edge | $S$ | $E$ | S-pos | E-pos | Case | Output |
|:----:|:---:|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:------:|
| 1 | (4,1) | (4,6) | OUT | IN | **3** | $I_5 + E$ |
| 2 | (4,6) | (3,7) | IN | IN | **1** | $E$ |
| 3 | (3,7) | (2,6) | IN | IN | **1** | $E$ |
| 4 | (2,6) | (2,1) | IN | OUT | **2** | $I_6$ |
| 5 | (2,1) | (4,1) | OUT | OUT | **4** | ∅ |

**Computing $I_5$ (segment $(4,1)\rightarrow(4,6)$ vs $y=2$):**

$$t = \dfrac{2 - 1}{6 - 1} = \dfrac{1}{5}$$

$$x_{I_5} = 4 + \tfrac{1}{5}(4 - 4) = 4, \quad y_{I_5} = 1 + \tfrac{1}{5}(6 - 1) = 2$$

$$\boxed{\,I_5 = (4,\;2)\,}$$

**Computing $I_6$ (segment $(2,6)\rightarrow(2,1)$ vs $y=2$):**

$$t = \dfrac{2 - 6}{1 - 6} = \dfrac{-4}{-5} = \tfrac{4}{5}$$

$$x_{I_6} = 2, \quad y_{I_6} = 6 + \tfrac{4}{5}(1 - 6) = 6 - 4 = 2$$

$$\boxed{\,I_6 = (2,\;2)\,}$$

**After Stage 3 (Bottom):**

$$P_3 = \{(4,2),\;(4,6),\;(3,7),\;(2,6),\;(2,2)\}$$

---

#### STAGE 4 — Top Edge Clipping ($y \leq 6$)

"Inside" is $y \leq 6$. Test each edge of $P_3$:

| Edge | $S$ | $E$ | S-pos | E-pos | Case | Output |
|:----:|:---:|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:------:|
| 1 | (4,2) | (4,6) | IN | IN | **1** | $E$ |
| 2 | (4,6) | (3,7) | IN | OUT | **2** | $I_7$ |
| 3 | (3,7) | (2,6) | OUT | OUT | **4** | ∅ |
| 4 | (2,6) | (2,2) | IN | IN | **1** | $E$ |
| 5 | (2,2) | (4,2) | IN | IN | **1** | $E$ |

**Computing $I_7$ (segment $(4,6)\rightarrow(3,7)$ vs $y=6$):**

$$t = \dfrac{6 - 6}{7 - 6} = 0$$

$$x_{I_7} = 4 + 0 \cdot (3 - 4) = 4, \quad y_{I_7} = 6$$

$$\boxed{\,I_7 = (4,\;6)\,}$$

Wait — $I_7$ coincides with the start vertex. This means the segment is *tangent* to the top edge. The output is simply $(4,6)$.

**After Stage 4 (Top) — FINAL RESULT:**

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{clipped}} = \{(4,2),\;(4,6),\;(3,7),\;(2,6),\;(2,2)\}\,}$$

This is a **pentagon** — the original 5-vertex polygon clipped against the rectangle.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation — Weiler–Atherton (Conceptual Trace)

**Setup:** Subject polygon $P_s$ (concave) with vertices $\{(1,3), (5,3), (5,5), (3,3), (3,1)\}$ — a U-shape. Clip polygon $P_c$ (concave pentagon) $\{(2,0), (4,0), (4,4), (3,5), (2,4)\}$.

**Step 1 — Identify Intersections:**

For every edge of $P_s$ intersected with every edge of $P_c$, compute the intersection using the parametric $t$ and check $0 \leq t \leq 1$ for both segments. Suppose we find two intersection points: $A = (2, 2.5)$ and $B = (4, 2.5)$ (purely illustrative).

**Step 2 — Mark In/Out Status:**

For each vertex of $P_s$:

- $(1,3)$: outside $P_c$ (since $x < 2$) → **OUT**
- $(5,3)$: outside $P_c$ (since $x > 4$) → **OUT**
- $(5,5)$: outside → **OUT**
- $(3,3)$: inside (check winding or ray-cast) → **IN**
- $(3,1)$: inside → **IN**

**Step 3 — Insert Intersection Markers:**

In $P_s$ list, between $(1,3)$ and $(5,3)$ insert $A$ as an **"in–out"** vertex. Similarly insert $B$ in the next edge.

**Step 4 — Begin Traversal at $A$:**

Output list: $[A]$. Follow $P_s$ forward → $B$. Output: $[A, B]$. At $B$ (an exit vertex), **switch** to $P_c$ and traverse in reverse.

**Step 5 — Continue on $P_c$ (reverse):**

Traverse $P_c$ vertices in reverse: $\ldots, (2,4), A, (2,0), \ldots$ Append until we reach $A$ again.

**Step 6 — Close the Loop:**

When we re-encounter $A$, the sub-polygon is complete. Output:

$$P_{\text{out},1} = [A \rightarrow (5,3) \rightarrow (5,5) \rightarrow (3,5)? \rightarrow B \rightarrow \ldots \text{(along $P_c$)} \rightarrow A]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **deep structural advantage** of Weiler–Atherton is that it handles **multiple disjoint sub-polygons** and **holes** elegantly. If the subject polygon intersects the clip polygon in *N* separate regions, the algorithm produces *N* separate output polygons after *N* separate traversal cycles.

### 3.3 Comparative Complexity Analysis

| Metric | Sutherland–Hodgeman | Weiler–Atherton |
|:-------|:-------------------:|:---------------:|
| Time Complexity (worst case) | $O(n \cdot m)$ | $O(n^2 + m^2)$ |
| Memory | $O(n)$ per stage | $O(n + m + I)$ |
| Supports concave clip | **No** | **Yes** |
| Supports polygon with holes | **No** | **Yes** |
| Output topology guarantee | Single polygon (convex clip) | Multiple polygons + holes |
| Implementation complexity | Simple pipeline | Topological traversal |

where $n$ = number of subject polygon vertices, $m$ = number of clip polygon edges, $I$ = number of intersection points.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sutherland–Hodgeman — Four-Stage Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Subject Polygon P0] --> B[Stage 1: Left Edge x greater equal xL]
    B --> C[Intermediate P1]
    C --> D[Stage 2: Right Edge x less equal xR]
    D --> E[Intermediate P2]
    E --> F[Stage 3: Bottom Edge y greater equal yB]
    F --> G[Intermediate P3]
    G --> H[Stage 4: Top Edge y less equal yT]
    H --> I[Final Clipped Polygon P4]

    subgraph CASE_LOOKUP[Case Lookup Per Edge]
        C1[Case 1 IN to IN: Output E]
        C2[Case 2 IN to OUT: Output I]
        C3[Case 3 OUT to IN: Output I plus E]
        C4[Case 4 OUT to OUT: Output nothing]
    end

    B -.uses.-> CASE_LOOKUP
    D -.uses.-> CASE_LOOKUP
    F -.uses.-> CASE_LOOKUP
    H -.uses.-> CASE_LOOKUP
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic — Single Edge Clip

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Edge from S to E] --> TEST1{Is S inside clip half plane}
    TEST1 -- YES --> TEST2{Is E inside clip half plane}
    TEST1 -- NO --> TEST3{Is E inside clip half plane}
    TEST2 -- YES --> OUT1[Case 1: Output E only]
    TEST2 -- NO --> OUT2[Case 2: Compute I Output I only]
    TEST3 -- YES --> OUT3[Case 3: Compute I Output I then E]
    TEST3 -- NO --> OUT4[Case 4: Output nothing]
    OUT1 --> DONE[Append to output list]
    OUT2 --> DONE
    OUT3 --> DONE
    OUT4 --> DONE
    DONE --> NEXT{More edges}
    NEXT -- YES --> START
    NEXT -- NO --> FINISH[Return output polygon]
```

### 4.3 Weiler–Atherton — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Subject Polygon Ps] --> MERGE
    IN2[Clip Polygon Pc] --> MERGE
    MERGE[Step 1: Compute All Intersections Insert I points] --> MARK
    MARK[Step 2: Classify Each Vertex as IN or OUT] --> TRAV
    TRAV[Step 3: Pick First Unprocessed IN to OUT Vertex] --> FORWARD
    FORWARD[Step 4: Traverse Ps Forward Append Vertices] --> SWITCHTEST{Reached Next Intersection}
    SWITCHTEST -- YES --> SWITCH[Step 5: Switch to Pc and Traverse Backward]
    SWITCHTEST -- NO --> FORWARD
    SWITCH --> CLOSETEST{Returned to Start Vertex}
    CLOSETEST -- YES --> EMIT[Emit One Clipped Sub Polygon]
    CLOSETEST -- NO --> SWITCH
    EMIT --> REMAIN{More Unprocessed IN to OUT}
    REMAIN -- YES --> TRAV
    REMAIN -- NO --> FINISH[Concatenate All Sub Polygons Final Output]
```

### 4.4 High-Level Comparative Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SH_BLOCK[Sutherland Hodgeman Module]
        SH1[Convex Clip Region Only] --> SH2[Pipeline of 4 Edge Clips]
        SH2 --> SH3[Single Output Polygon]
    end

    subgraph WA_BLOCK[Weiler Atherton Module]
        WA1[Arbitrary Concave Clip] --> WA2[Intersection Computation]
        WA2 --> WA3[In Out Classification]
        WA3 --> WA4[Boundary Traversal]
        WA4 --> WA5[Multiple Output Polygons Allowed]
    end

    INPUT[Raw 2D Polygonal Scene] --> SH_BLOCK
    INPUT --> WA_BLOCK
    SH_BLOCK --> DISPLAY[Frame Buffer / Render Target]
    WA_BLOCK --> DISPLAY
```

### 4.5 Worked Example Schematic (Sutherland–Hodgeman)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["P0 Subject<br>1,1  5,1  5,5  3,7  1,5"] --> P1["P1 After Left<br>2,1  5,1  5,5  3,7  2,6"]
    P1 --> P2["P2 After Right<br>4,1  4,6  3,7  2,6  2,1"]
    P2 --> P3["P3 After Bottom<br>4,2  4,6  3,7  2,6  2,2"]
    P3 --> P4["P4 After Top FINAL<br>4,2  4,6  3,7  2,6  2,2"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)**
*State and explain the four cases that arise when a polygon edge is processed against a clipping edge in the Sutherland–Hodgeman algorithm.*

**Model Answer:**
The four cases for each edge $(S, E)$ are:

1. **Case 1 — Both Inside (S:IN, E:IN):** Output the endpoint $E$ only. The entire edge is retained.
2. **Case 2 — Inside to Outside (S:IN, E:OUT):** Compute intersection $I$ with the clip boundary; output $I$ only. The edge is clipped at the boundary.
3. **Case 3 — Outside to Inside (S:OUT, E:IN):** Compute intersection $I$ with the clip boundary; output both $I$ and $E$ in that order. A new vertex is introduced.
4. **Case 4 — Both Outside (S:OUT, E:OUT):** Output nothing. The edge is entirely discarded.

These four cases are applied sequentially to each of the four (or more) clipping edges of the window.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)**
*Why is the Sutherland–Hodgeman algorithm restricted to convex clipping regions? Illustrate with a diagram of the failure case.*

**Model Answer:**
Sutherland–Hodgeman processes the polygon against one clip-edge at a time using a half-plane intersection. For a **convex** clip region, the intersection of multiple half-planes remains a single connected region, and the polygon never produces phantom (ghost) edges. For a **concave** clip region, the sequential half-plane intersection can disconnect the polygon, producing spurious edges across the reflex (concave) interior angle. The clipped output may then have *crossing edges* or *duplicate* vertices, which is topologically invalid. Hence the algorithm is mathematically valid only for convex clip polygons (typically axis-aligned rectangles).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Explain the Sutherland–Hodgeman polygon clipping algorithm with a neat block diagram. List all four cases with the output conditions.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *Clip the polygon with vertices $(1, 1), (7, 1), (7, 4), (5, 6), (3, 4)$ against the rectangular window with corners $(2, 2), (6, 2), (6, 5), (2, 5)$ using the Sutherland–Hodgeman algorithm. Show all four stages and state the final clipped polygon.*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 Marks):**

The Sutherland–Hodgeman algorithm (1974) is a *divide-and-conquer* style clipper that processes the polygon sequentially against each clip boundary.

**Pipeline Order:** Left → Right → Bottom → Top (for axis-aligned rectangle).

**Block Diagram (in text form for the answer sheet):**

```
        Subject Polygon P_s
              |
              v
     [ Stage 1: Left Edge x = x_L ]
              |
              v
           P_1
              |
              v
   [ Stage 2: Right Edge x = x_R ]
              |
              v
           P_2
              |
              v
 [ Stage 3: Bottom Edge y = y_B ]
              |
              v
           P_3
              |
              v
    [ Stage 4: Top Edge y = y_T ]
              |
              v
      P_4 (Final Clipped)
```

Each stage takes a polygon list and applies the four-case logic, emitting a new polygon list. **Memory reuse** is key — only two buffers are needed.

**Four Cases** (as enumerated in Q1 of Part A above).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Subject polygon: $V_0 = (1, 1), V_1 = (7, 1), V_2 = (7, 4), V_3 = (5, 6), V_4 = (3, 4)$.
- Window: $x_L = 2, x_R = 6, y_B = 2, y_T = 5$.

**Stage 1 — Left ($x \geq 2$):**

| Edge | S | E | S-pos | E-pos | Case | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (1,1) | (7,1) | OUT | IN | 3 | $I_1, E$ |
| 2 | (7,1) | (7,4) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 3 | (7,4) | (5,6) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 4 | (5,6) | (3,4) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 5 | (3,4) | (1,1) | IN | OUT | 2 | $I_2$ |

$I_1$ on $x = 2$ from $(1,1)\to(7,1)$: $t = 1/6$, $y = 1$ → $I_1 = (2, 1)$.
$I_2$ on $x = 2$ from $(3,4)\to(1,1)$: $t = (2-3)/(1-3) = 1/2$, $y = 4 + 0.5(1-4) = 2.5$ → $I_2 = (2, 2.5)$.

**$P_1 = \{(2, 1), (7, 1), (7, 4), (5, 6), (3, 4), (2, 2.5)\}$**

**Stage 2 — Right ($x \leq 6$):**

| Edge | S | E | S-pos | E-pos | Case | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (2,1) | (7,1) | IN | OUT | 2 | $I_3$ |
| 2 | (7,1) | (7,4) | OUT | OUT | 4 | ∅ |
| 3 | (7,4) | (5,6) | OUT | IN | 3 | $I_4, E$ |
| 4 | (5,6) | (3,4) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 5 | (3,4) | (2,2.5) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 6 | (2,2.5) | (2,1) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |

$I_3$ on $x = 6$: $t = 4/5$, $y = 1$ → $I_3 = (6, 1)$.
$I_4$ on $x = 6$ from $(7,4)\to(5,6)$: $t = (6-7)/(5-7) = 1/2$, $y = 4 + 0.5(6-4) = 5$ → $I_4 = (6, 5)$.

**$P_2 = \{(6, 1), (6, 5), (5, 6), (3, 4), (2, 2.5), (2, 1)\}$**

**Stage 3 — Bottom ($y \geq 2$):**

| Edge | S | E | S-pos | E-pos | Case | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (6,1) | (6,5) | OUT | IN | 3 | $I_5, E$ |
| 2 | (6,5) | (5,6) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 3 | (5,6) | (3,4) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 4 | (3,4) | (2,2.5) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 5 | (2,2.5) | (2,1) | IN | OUT | 2 | $I_6$ |
| 6 | (2,1) | (6,1) | OUT | OUT | 4 | ∅ |

$I_5$ on $y = 2$ from $(6,1)\to(6,5)$: $t = 1/4$, $x = 6$ → $I_5 = (6, 2)$.
$I_6$ on $y = 2$ from $(2, 2.5)\to(2,1)$: $t = (2-2.5)/(1-2.5) = 1/3$, $x = 2$ → $I_6 = (2, 2)$.

**$P_3 = \{(6, 2), (6, 5), (5, 6), (3, 4), (2, 2.5), (2, 2)\}$**

**Stage 4 — Top ($y \leq 5$):**

| Edge | S | E | S-pos | E-pos | Case | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (6,2) | (6,5) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 2 | (6,5) | (5,6) | IN | OUT | 2 | $I_7$ |
| 3 | (5,6) | (3,4) | OUT | OUT | 4 | ∅ |
| 4 | (3,4) | (2,2.5) | OUT | IN | 3 | $I_8, E$ |
| 5 | (2,2.5) | (2,2) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |
| 6 | (2,2) | (6,2) | IN | IN | 1 | $E$ |

$I_7$ on $y = 5$ from $(6,5)\to(5,6)$: $t = 0$, → $I_7 = (6, 5)$ (tangent).
$I_8$ on $y = 5$ from $(5,6)\to(3,4)$: $t = (5-6)/(4-6) = 1/2$, $x = 5 + 0.5(3-5) = 4$ → $I_8 = (4, 5)$.

**Final Clipped Polygon:**

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{final}} = \{(6, 2),\; (6, 5),\; (5, 6)? \to \text{replaced},\; (4, 5),\; (2, 2.5),\; (2, 2)\}\,}$$

Simplifying (after the tangent correction), the final vertex sequence is:

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{final}} = \{(6, 2),\; (6, 5),\; (4, 5),\; (2, 2.5),\; (2, 2)\}\,}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Discuss the limitations of the Sutherland–Hodgeman algorithm. Under what condition does it fail and why?*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *Explain the Weiler–Atherton polygon clipping algorithm with its traversal strategy. How does it handle concave clipping regions and polygons with holes?*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Limitations of Sutherland–Hodgeman (7 Marks):**

1. **Convex Clip Restriction:** The algorithm assumes the clip region is the *intersection of half-planes*, i.e., convex. For concave clip regions, sequential half-plane clipping can produce **spurious edges** that span across the reflex (concave) interior angle, leading to incorrect self-intersecting polygons.

2. **No Hole Support:** If the subject polygon has holes (e.g., a donut shape), the simple pipeline cannot preserve the hole boundary — holes are filled in.

3. **Single Output Polygon:** The pipeline produces at most one polygon. If the subject polygon overlaps the clip window in *two disjoint regions* (e.g., a U-shape overlapping a rectangle), the algorithm may merge them incorrectly.

4. **Numerical Precision:** Repeated re-evaluation of intersections through four stages can accumulate floating-point errors.

5. **Failure Example — L-shaped (concave) clip window:** When clipping a square against an L-shape, Sutherland–Hodgeman produces a polygon with a *diagonal* spurious edge across the missing quadrant of the L, which is geometrically invalid.

---

**(b) Weiler–Atherton Traversal Strategy (7 Marks):**

The Weiler–Atherton algorithm (1977) was designed precisely to overcome the limitations above. Its traversal strategy is:

**Step 1 — Intersection Construction:** Compute all edge-edge intersections between subject $P_s$ and clip $P_c$. Insert these intersection points into both polygon vertex lists.

**Step 2 — Vertex Classification:** Mark each vertex as **IN** (inside the other polygon) or **OUT** (outside), and each intersection as either an **"in–out"** (entering) or **"out–in"** (exiting) transition.

**Step 3 — Clipped Sub-polygon Construction:** Begin at an unprocessed "in–out" vertex. Traverse the subject polygon boundary in forward direction, appending each encountered vertex to the output list, until the next intersection is reached. At this intersection, **switch** to the clip polygon and traverse in the *reverse* direction, continuing to append vertices.

**Step 4 — Closure:** Continue the alternating subject/clip traversal until the start vertex is re-encountered. This forms **one clipped sub-polygon**.

**Step 5 — Repeat:** For each unvisited "in–out" vertex, build another sub-polygon. Concatenate them at the end.

**Handling Concave Clips and Holes:**

- **Concave clip polygon:** Because Weiler–Atherton uses the *actual* clip polygon edges (not just half-planes), the algorithm correctly handles reflex angles. The traversal jumps between subject and clip boundaries only at true intersection points, eliminating phantom edges.

- **Polygons with holes:** The clip polygon is represented as a list of *multiple closed loops* — the outer boundary and the inner hole boundaries. The algorithm naturally traces the outer boundary, the hole boundary, etc., producing an output with both filled regions and correctly preserved holes.

- **Multiple sub-polygons:** If the subject overlaps the clip in two or more disjoint regions, each is constructed as a separate sub-polygon in independent traversal cycles, and they are all retained in the final output.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Deduction per error):**
> 1. **Wrong case identification (–1 mark):** Students often misclassify IN/OUT. Always re-verify by plugging coordinates into the inequality of the current clip edge.
> 2. **Forgetting to append $E$ in Case 3 (–1 mark):** The endpoint is *always* emitted in Cases 1 and 3.
> 3. **Skipping the intersection computation in numerical answers (–2 marks):** KTU 2024 scheme requires *explicit* values of all $I$ points, not just qualitative labels.
> 4. **Pipeline order confusion (–1 mark):** The order is **Left → Right → Bottom → Top**, not arbitrary.
> 5. **Forgetting parametric $t$ derivation (–1 mark):** Showing $t = (x_{\text{clip}} - x_s)/(x_e - x_s)$ earns full method marks.
> 6. **Applying Sutherland–Hodgeman to concave clip (–2 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly test whether the student *recognizes* the convex-only constraint.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Points to Remember — Sutherland–Hodgeman:**
> - [ ] Algorithm processes polygon against **4 clip edges sequentially**: Left → Right → Bottom → Top.
> - [ ] **4 cases per edge**: (IN, IN) → E; (IN, OUT) → I; (OUT, IN) → I + E; (OUT, OUT) → ∅.
> - [ ] Output of stage $k$ is **input** of stage $k+1$.
> - [ ] **Parametric $t$ formula**: $t = (x_{\text{clip}} - x_s)/(x_e - x_s)$ for vertical; analogous for horizontal.
> - [ ] Only **valid for convex clip regions** (typically axis-aligned rectangles).
> - [ ] **Time complexity** $O(nm)$ where $n$ = subject vertices, $m$ = clip edges.
> - [ ] Single output polygon guaranteed.
> - [ ] Pipeline of 4 edge clips is the canonical architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Points to Remember — Weiler–Atherton:**
> - [ ] Uses **traversal** of subject and clip polygon boundaries alternately.
> - [ ] **In–out** and **out–in** classification of intersection points is central.
> - [ ] **Switch** from subject to clip traversal at exit intersections.
> - [ ] Traverse clip polygon in **reverse** direction when switched.
> - [ ] Supports **concave clip regions**, polygons **with holes**, and **multiple disjoint output polygons**.
> - [ ] More complex implementation but more powerful than Sutherland–Hodgeman.
> - [ ] **Time complexity** $O(n^2 + m^2)$ in worst case due to all-pairs intersection search.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Boost Mnemonics:**
> - **S–H order:** "**L**azy **R**abbits **B**ounce **T**all" → **L**eft, **R**ight, **B**ottom, **T**op.
> - **S–H Cases:** "**IN-IN** keeps E; **IN-OUT** keeps I; **OUT-IN** keeps I-then-E; **OUT-OUT** keeps none."
> - **Weiler–Atherton direction flip:** "Subject forward, Clip reverse."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three-Dimensional Viewing Pipeline
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Three-Dimensional Viewing Pipeline

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Dimensional Viewing Pipeline:** The ordered sequence of geometric transformations and operations that converts a scene defined in three-dimensional object/modeling coordinates into a two-dimensional image suitable for display on a raster output device. The pipeline in KTU-2024 scheme (per Hearn–Baker reference framework) consists of five canonical stages: **Modeling Transformation**, **Viewing Transformation**, **Projection Transformation**, **Clipping**, and **Workstation (Viewport) Transformation**.

The pipeline operates on five distinct coordinate systems:

| # | Coordinate System | Abbrev. | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | World (Modeling) Coordinates | **MC / WC** | Object positions in a global 3D scene |
| 2 | Viewing Coordinates | **VC** | Scene aligned to camera axes |
| 3 | Projection (Perspective/Parallel) Coordinates | **PC** | 3D camera-space after projection |
| 4 | Normalized Device Coordinates | **NDC / NPC** | Canonical view volume (-1 to 1 or 0 to 1) |
| 5 | Device (Screen) Coordinates | **DC** | Final pixel positions on display |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Photographer's Camera"

Imagine you are a **photographer** capturing a 3D statue inside a museum:

- **Modeling Transformation** → You place the statue on a stage and arrange other objects around it (placing everything into one shared world stage).
- **Viewing Transformation** → You walk to a specific spot, point your camera in a chosen direction, and tilt it to frame the shot (defining the *View Reference Point*, *View Normal*, and *View-Up Vector*).
- **Projection Transformation** → The camera lens collapses the 3D scene onto a flat 2D film plane (either *perspective* like a normal lens, or *parallel/orthographic* like a telephoto lens).
- **Clipping** → The lens automatically crops away everything outside the camera's field of view.
- **Workstation Transformation** → The developed photograph is printed at a chosen size and pasted onto a specific location on a scrapbook page (mapping to screen pixels).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** The *Projection* stage preserves a **3D z-coordinate** (depth) so that hidden-surface removal can later occur. Only after clipping and the workstation mapping does the result become a true 2D raster image.

## 1.3 Pipeline Stages — Quick Summary

1. **Modeling Transformation** — Object coordinates → World coordinates.
2. **Viewing Transformation** — World coordinates → Viewing coordinates (camera-aligned).
3. **Projection Transformation** — Viewing coordinates → Projection coordinates (view volume normalized).
4. **Clipping** — Remove geometry outside the canonical view volume.
5. **Workstation Transformation** — NPC → Device coordinates (screen pixels).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Stage-by-Stage Theory

### Stage 1 — Modeling Transformation (MC → WC)
Each object is described in its own local coordinate frame (e.g., a car's wheel vs. the car body). The modeling transformation places these objects into a single **shared world coordinate system** via translation, rotation, and non-uniform scaling matrices:

$$
M_{\text{model}} \;=\; T(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z)\;\cdot\;R_z(\theta_z)\;\cdot\;R_y(\theta_y)\;\cdot\;R_x(\theta_x)\;\cdot\;S(s_x, s_y, s_z)
$$

### Stage 2 — Viewing Transformation (WC → VC)
The viewing coordinate system is defined by three orthogonal vectors:

$$
\vec{n} \;=\; \frac{\overrightarrow{VPN}}{\vert\overrightarrow{VPN}\vert} \qquad
\vec{u} \;=\; \frac{\overrightarrow{VUP}\times\vec{n}}{\vert\overrightarrow{VUP}\times\vec{n}\vert} \qquad
\vec{v} \;=\; \vec{n}\times\vec{u}
$$

where **VRP** = View Reference Point, **VPN** = View-Plane Normal, **VUP** = View-Up vector.

The viewing transformation is the composition of:
- **Translation**: $T(-\overrightarrow{VRP})$ — moves VRP to origin.
- **Rotation**: $R_{\text{view}}$ — aligns $\vec{n}$ with the negative Z-axis and $\vec{v}$ with the positive Y-axis.

$$
M_{\text{view}} \;=\; R_{\text{view}} \;\cdot\; T(-\overrightarrow{VRP})
$$

### Stage 3 — Projection Transformation (VC → PC)
Two projection families:

**Parallel (Orthographic) Projection:**
$$
M_{\text{par}} \;=\; S_{\text{par}} \;\cdot\; SH_{\text{par}} \;\cdot\; T(-\overrightarrow{DOP})
$$
where **DOP** = Direction Of Projection. The shear matrix $SH_{\text{par}}$ aligns DOP with the Z-axis; the scale matrix maps the parallelepiped view volume to the canonical cube.

**Perspective Projection:**
$$
M_{\text{per}} \;=\; S_{\text{per}} \;\cdot\; SH_{\text{per}} \;\cdot\; T(-\overrightarrow{PRP})
$$
where **PRP** = Projection Reference Point (the camera location in VC). The shear aligns the **Center Of Projection (COP)** with the Z-axis; the scale maps the perspective frustum to a canonical frustum.

### Stage 4 — Clipping
Six clipping planes of the canonical view volume are tested against primitives:
- Left/Right: $x = \pm 1$
- Bottom/Top: $y = \pm 1$
- Near/Far: $z = 0$ and $z = 1$ (for perspective) or $z = 0, 1$ (for parallel)

Standard algorithms: **Cohen–Sutherland** (line clipping), **Sutherland–Hodgman** (polygon clipping).

### Stage 5 — Workstation Transformation (NDC → DC)
Maps NPC coordinates $[-1, 1]^3$ (or $[0,1]^3$) to integer pixel positions:

$$
M_{ws} \;=\; T\!\left(\tfrac{s_x}{2}+p_x,\ \tfrac{s_y}{2}+p_y\right)\;\cdot\;S\!\left(\tfrac{s_x}{2},\ \tfrac{s_y}{2}\right)
$$

The Z-component is also scaled to fit the device's depth-buffer range.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU 2024 board examination problems.**

| # | Transformation | Composite Matrix Form | Key Inputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modeling | $T \cdot R_z \cdot R_y \cdot R_x \cdot S$ | Object placement params |
| 2 | View Translation | $T(-VRP_x, -VRP_y, -VRP_z)$ | VRP |
| 3 | View Rotation | $R_{\text{view}} = [\vec{u}\ \vec{v}\ \vec{n}\ 0]$ rows | VPN, VUP |
| 4 | Perspective Projection | $S_{\text{per}} \cdot SH_{\text{per}} \cdot T(-PRP)$ | PRP, window center CW |
| 5 | Parallel Projection | $S_{\text{par}} \cdot SH_{\text{par}} \cdot T(-DOP)$ | DOP, window center CW |
| 6 | Workstation Mapping | $T(p_x, p_y) \cdot S(\tfrac{s_x}{2}, \tfrac{s_y}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2})$ | Screen $s_x, s_y$, offset $p_x, p_y$ |
| 7 | Orthogonal axes | $\vec{u} = \dfrac{\vec{V}\times\vec{n}}{\vert\vec{V}\times\vec{n}\vert}$, $\vec{v}=\vec{n}\times\vec{u}$ | VUP, VPN |
| 8 | Canonical view volume | $-1 \le x,y \le 1,\ 0 \le z \le 1$ | For clipping |
| 9 | DC pixel coordinate | $x_{\text{dc}} = (x_{\text{npc}}+1)\tfrac{s_x}{2} + p_x$ | Pixel mapping |
| 10 | DC pixel coordinate | $y_{\text{dc}} = (y_{\text{npc}}+1)\tfrac{s_y}{2} + p_y$ | Pixel mapping |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAD/CAM systems** use this exact pipeline for solid-model visualization.
- **Flight simulators** rely on the perspective stage for cockpit out-the-window views.
- **Medical imaging** (CT/MRI) applies viewing + parallel projection for volumetric slicing.
- **3D video games** (Unity, Unreal) implement all five stages on the GPU every frame.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Viewing Transformation Matrix

**Given:** VRP = $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$, VPN = $(a, b, c)$, VUP = $(a_u, b_u, c_u)$.

**Step 1 — Normalize VPN to get $\vec{n}$:**

$$
\vec{n} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}} \,(a,\ b,\ c)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\vec{u}$ perpendicular to both VUP and $\vec{n}$:**

$$
\vec{u} \;=\; \frac{\vec{VUP}\times\vec{n}}{\vert\vec{VUP}\times\vec{n}\vert} \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert\vec{VUP}\times\vec{n}\vert}\,(b_u c - c_u b,\ c_u a - a_u c,\ a_u b - b_u a)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute true up vector $\vec{v} = \vec{n}\times\vec{u}$** to guarantee orthonormal right-handed basis.

**Step 4 — Form the rotation matrix $R_{\text{view}}$** that maps the viewing axes $(\vec{u}, \vec{v}, \vec{n})$ to the standard axes $(X, Y, Z)$:

$$
R_{\text{view}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} u_x & u_y & u_z & 0 \\ v_x & v_y & v_z & 0 \\ n_x & n_y & n_z & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Why this form?** Each row of $R_{\text{view}}$ is a basis vector of the new (viewing) frame expressed in world coordinates. Post-multiplying world coordinates by this matrix rotates them into alignment with the viewing frame.

**Step 5 — Combined viewing transformation:**

$$
M_{\text{view}} \;=\; R_{\text{view}} \;\cdot\; T(-x_0, -y_0, -z_0)
$$

$$
M_{\text{view}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} u_x & u_y & u_z & -u\!\cdot\!VRP \\ v_x & v_y & v_z & -v\!\cdot\!VRP \\ n_x & n_y & n_z & -n\!\cdot\!VRP \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where the dot product shorthand means, e.g., $u\!\cdot\!VRP = u_x x_0 + u_y y_0 + u_z z_0$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Perspective Projection Transformation

**Given:** PRP = $(p_x, p_y, p_z)$ in VC, window center on view plane: CW = $(c_x, c_y, c_z)$.

**Step 1 — Translate PRP to origin:**

$$
T(-PRP) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & -p_x \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -p_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & -p_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Shear to align COP with the Z-axis.** After Step 1, COP lies at $(c_x - p_x,\ c_y - p_y,\ c_z - p_z)$. The shear matrix removes the X and Y offsets of COP so that COP lies on the Z-axis:

$$
SH_{\text{per}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\tfrac{c_x - p_x}{c_z - p_z} & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -\tfrac{c_y - p_y}{c_z - p_z} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Geometric meaning:** After this shear, the line from COP to any point on the view plane is preserved, but the COP has been "rotated" to lie directly above (on the Z-axis).

**Step 3 — Scale to the canonical view volume $[-1,1]\times[-1,1]\times[0,1]$.** Let the view-plane rectangle have half-extents $(w_u, w_v)$ in u and v directions, and front/back clipping distances $F, B$ along Z (measured after the shear, with $F < B$).

$$
S_{\text{per}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{F}{(B-F)\,\tfrac{w_u}{2}} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \tfrac{F}{(B-F)\,\tfrac{w_v}{2}} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \tfrac{F}{B\,(B-F)} & -\tfrac{1}{B} \\ 0 & 0 & -\tfrac{1}{B\,(B-F)} & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Derivation hint:** A point $(0,0,F)$ in view-plane coordinates must map to $z = 0$ (near plane), and $(0,0,B)$ to $z = 1$ (far plane), while the view-plane rectangle $(-w_u, w_u) \times (-w_v, w_v)$ at $z = F$ must map to $(-1, 1) \times (-1, 1)$. Solving these four constraints gives the four non-zero entries shown above.

**Step 4 — Combined perspective matrix:**

$$
M_{\text{per}} \;=\; S_{\text{per}} \;\cdot\; SH_{\text{per}} \;\cdot\; T(-PRP)
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Workstation (Viewport) Transformation

**Given:** NDC ranges $[-1,1]\times[-1,1]\times[-1,1]$, screen size $s_x \times s_y$ pixels, viewport origin $(p_x, p_y)$.

**Step 1 — Map $[-1,1] \to [0, s_x]$ and $[-1,1] \to [0, s_y]$:**

$$
S_{\text{scale}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{s_x}{2} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \tfrac{s_y}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Translate origin to $(p_x, p_y)$:**

$$
T_{\text{offset}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & p_x \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & p_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Final workstation transformation:**

$$
M_{ws} \;=\; T_{\text{offset}} \;\cdot\; S_{\text{scale}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{s_x}{2} & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{s_x}{2} + p_x \\ 0 & \tfrac{s_y}{2} & 0 & \tfrac{s_y}{2} + p_y \\ 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A point with NDC $(0.5, 0.25, 0)$ on a $1024\times 768$ screen with no offset gives:

$$
x_{dc} \;=\; (0.5 + 1) \cdot \tfrac{1024}{2} \;=\; 768,\quad y_{dc} \;=\; (0.25 + 1)\cdot\tfrac{768}{2} \;=\; 480
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the 3D Viewing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    objCoords[Object MC Coordinates] --> stage1[Stage 1 Modeling Transform]
    stage1 --> worldCoords[World WC Coordinates]
    worldCoords --> stage2[Stage 2 Viewing Transform]
    stage2 --> viewCoords[Viewing VC Coordinates]
    viewCoords --> projBranch{Projection Type}
    projBranch -->|Parallel| stage3a[Stage 3a Parallel Projection]
    projBranch -->|Perspective| stage3b[Stage 3b Perspective Projection]
    stage3a --> pcCoords[Projection PC Coordinates]
    stage3b --> pcCoords
    pcCoords --> stage4[Stage 4 Clipping]
    stage4 --> ndcCoords[Normalized NPC Coordinates]
    ndcCoords --> stage5[Stage 5 Workstation Mapping]
    stage5 --> screenCoords[Device DC Screen Pixels]
    screenCoords --> display[2D Raster Display]

    VRP[VRP VPN VUP Inputs] -.-> stage2
    PRP[PRP CW Window F B Inputs] -.-> stage3b
    DOP[DOP CW Window F B Inputs] -.-> stage3a
    screensize[Screen Size sx sy Offset px py] -.-> stage5
```

## 4.2 Coordinate-System Transformation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Scene Definition] --> mc[Modeling Coordinates MC]
    mc -->|M model| wc[World Coordinates WC]
    wc -->|M view| vc[Viewing Coordinates VC]
    vc -->|M proj type parallel or perspective| pc[Projection Coordinates PC]
    pc -->|clip to canonical volume| npc[Normalized Projection Coordinates NPC]
    npc -->|M ws viewport mapping| dc[Device Coordinates DC]
    dc --> raster[Raster Image Output]

    subgraph inputs [Required Inputs at Each Stage]
        i1[Object placement transforms] -.-> mc
        i2[VRP VPN VUP] -.-> wc
        i3[PRP or DOP window center near far planes] -.-> vc
        i4[Screen size viewport offset] -.-> npc
    end
```

## 4.3 Canonical View Volume Schematic (Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    apex[Apex at Origin COP] --> frustum[Truncated Pyramid Frustum]
    frustum --> near[Near Plane z equals 0 x y in minus 1 to 1]
    frustum --> far[Far Plane z equals 1 x y in minus 1 to 1]
    near --> inside[Region x in minus 1 to 1 y in minus 1 to 1 z in 0 to 1 KEPT]
    far --> inside
    outside[Anything outside frustum CLIPPED] -.-> discard[Discarded]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the five stages of the 3D viewing pipeline and state the coordinate system used at the output of each stage.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

1. **Modeling Transformation:** MC (object/modeling) → WC (world coordinates).
2. **Viewing Transformation:** WC → VC (viewing coordinates, camera-aligned).
3. **Projection Transformation:** VC → PC (projection coordinates, perspective or parallel).
4. **Clipping:** PC → NPC (normalized projection coordinates inside canonical view volume).
5. **Workstation Transformation:** NPC → DC (device/screen pixel coordinates).

The pipeline sequentially performs the composite mapping $P_{\text{DC}} \;=\; M_{ws} \cdot M_{\text{proj}} \cdot M_{\text{view}} \cdot M_{\text{model}} \cdot P_{\text{MC}}$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of VRP, VPN, and VUP in establishing the viewing coordinate system.**

**Model Answer:**
- **VRP (View Reference Point)** is a point in world coordinates that becomes the origin of the viewing coordinate system.
- **VPN (View-Plane Normal)** specifies the direction perpendicular to the view plane; the view plane passes through VRP and is perpendicular to VPN.
- **VUP (View-Up Vector)** indicates the desired "up" direction in the image; it is used (together with VPN) to construct an orthonormal basis $\\{\vec{u}, \vec{v}, \vec{n}\\}$ for the viewing frame.

The orthonormal axes are derived as $\vec{n}=\overrightarrow{VPN}/\vert\overrightarrow{VPN}\vert$, $\vec{u}=(\vec{VUP}\times\vec{n})/\vert\vec{VUP}\times\vec{n}\vert$, and $\vec{v}=\vec{n}\times\vec{u}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Derive the complete viewing transformation matrix from world coordinates to viewing coordinates. State clearly the role of each component matrix.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define viewing axes from given vectors:**

Given VRP $=(x_0, y_0, z_0)$, VPN $=(a, b, c)$, VUP $=(d, e, f)$:

$$
\vec{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}}\,(a,b,c)
$$

$$
\vec{u} = \frac{\vec{VUP}\times\vec{n}}{\vert\vec{VUP}\times\vec{n}\vert}
$$

$$
\vec{v} = \vec{n}\times\vec{u}
$$

**Step 2 — Translation matrix $T(-VRP)$** shifts the world so that VRP coincides with the viewing origin:

$$
T(-VRP) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & -x_0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -y_0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & -z_0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Rotation matrix $R_{\text{view}}$** rotates the translated world frame so that $\vec{u}, \vec{v}, \vec{n}$ align with $X, Y, Z$ axes:

$$
R_{\text{view}} = \begin{bmatrix} u_x & u_y & u_z & 0 \\ v_x & v_y & v_z & 0 \\ n_x & n_y & n_z & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined matrix:**

$$
M_{\text{view}} = R_{\text{view}} \cdot T(-VRP) = \begin{bmatrix} u_x & u_y & u_z & -u\!\cdot\!VRP \\ v_x & v_y & v_z & -v\!\cdot\!VRP \\ n_x & n_y & n_z & -n\!\cdot\!VRP \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Roles:** $T(-VRP)$ repositions the scene; $R_{\text{view}}$ reorients it so that subsequent projection steps use standard axis-aligned formulas.

---

**(b) With reference to a perspective projection, derive the perspective transformation matrix and explain the geometric meaning of each sub-matrix.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The complete perspective matrix is $M_{\text{per}} = S_{\text{per}} \cdot SH_{\text{per}} \cdot T(-PRP)$.

**Step 1 — $T(-PRP)$:** Translates the Projection Reference Point to the origin so that the camera is at $(0,0,0)$ and the view plane center is at $(c_x - p_x, c_y - p_y, c_z - p_z)$.

**Step 2 — $SH_{\text{per}}$:** Shears the scene so that the COP (which after Step 1 lies at the offset above) is moved onto the Z-axis. The shear parameters are $-\tfrac{c_x - p_x}{c_z - p_z}$ (row 1, column 3) and $-\tfrac{c_y - p_y}{c_z - p_z}$ (row 2, column 3).

**Step 3 — $S_{\text{per}}$:** Scales the asymmetric frustum into the canonical rectangular frustum $-1 \le x, y \le 1$ and $0 \le z \le 1$, using the front/back clipping distances $F, B$ and the half-widths $w_u, w_v$ of the view-plane window. Non-zero entries are:

$$
S_{\text{per}} = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{F}{(B-F)\,w_u/2} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \tfrac{F}{(B-F)\,w_v/2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \tfrac{F}{B(B-F)} & -\tfrac{1}{B} \\ 0 & 0 & -\tfrac{1}{B(B-F)} & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Geometric meaning:** The combined $M_{\text{per}}$ is a non-affine (perspective) map because $z=0$ becomes a finite point while $z=\infty$ collapses to a point — producing foreshortening.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) Differentiate between parallel and perspective projections. Derive the parallel projection transformation matrix assuming the direction of projection (DOP) is the negative Z-axis.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Parallel Projection | Perspective Projection |
|---|---|---|
| Projection center | At infinity | At finite PRP |
| Parallel lines remain parallel | **Yes** | No |
| Foreshortening | **Uniform** | Distance-dependent |
| Use case | Engineering drawings, blueprints | Realistic rendering, games |

**Derivation for DOP = $(0, 0, -1)$:**

Since DOP already lies along the negative Z-axis, both the translation $T(-DOP)$ and the shear $SH_{\text{par}}$ reduce to identity matrices (no offset needs correction). Only the scale matrix $S_{\text{par}}$ remains:

$$
S_{\text{par}} = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{2}{u_{\max} - u_{\min}} & 0 & 0 & -\tfrac{u_{\max}+u_{\min}}{u_{\max}-u_{\min}} \\ 0 & \tfrac{2}{v_{\max} - v_{\min}} & 0 & -\tfrac{v_{\max}+v_{\min}}{v_{\max}-v_{\min}} \\ 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{B - F} & -\tfrac{F}{B-F} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore $M_{\text{par}} = S_{\text{par}}$ and the canonical view volume is $[-1,1]^2 \times [0,1]$.

---

**(b) Given a workstation transformation that maps NPC $[-1,1]^3$ to a screen of $800 \times 600$ pixels with viewport offset $(50, 40)$, derive $M_{ws}$ and compute the screen pixel coordinates of an NPC point $(0.3, -0.4, 0.7)$.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the general workstation formula:**

$$
M_{ws} = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{s_x}{2} & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{s_x}{2}+p_x \\ 0 & \tfrac{s_y}{2} & 0 & \tfrac{s_y}{2}+p_y \\ 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 400 & 0 & 0 & 450 \\ 0 & 300 & 0 & 340 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by NPC point $\bigl(0.3,\ -0.4,\ 0.7,\ 1\bigr)^T$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{dc} &= 400(0.3) + 450 = 120 + 450 = 570 \\
y_{dc} &= 300(-0.4) + 340 = -120 + 340 = 220 \\
