# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define multimedia. List its five major components.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Multimedia** is the integration of multiple forms of digital media — text, graphics/ images, audio, video, and animation — into a single computer-delivered application that may also be interactive.
>
> The five major components are:
> 1. **Text** — characters, words, fonts, styles.
> 2. **Graphics / Images** — still visuals like photos, icons, diagrams.
> 3. **Audio** — sound, music, narration, effects.
> 4. **Video** — moving pictures with synchronized sound.
> 5. **Animation** — simulated motion using sequence or vector transforms.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Hypertext and Hypermedia with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hypertext | Hypermedia |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Definition | Non-linear organisation of **text only** linked via references. | Non-linear organisation of **all media types** linked via references. |
| Media used | Text | Text, images, audio, video, animation |
| Era | Ted Nelson, 1965 (Project Xanadu) | Tim Berners-Lee, 1989–1991 (WWW) |
| Example | Early gopher systems, "Help" links in a single document. | World Wide Web, Wikipedia, e-learning portals. |

> The WWW is **hypermedia** because pages contain text, images, embedded videos, and audio, all interconnected by hyperlinks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five components of multimedia** in detail with suitable examples and standard file formats.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A digital image of size **$1024 \times 768$** pixels uses **16-bit color depth**. Calculate (i) the **total pixels**, (ii) the **file size in bits**, (iii) the **file size in KB**, and (iv) the **file size in MB**.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **layered architecture of a multimedia system** with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-minute audio file is recorded in stereo at a sampling rate of **22.05 kHz** with **8-bit** resolution. If it is compressed to **1/5th** of its original size, find (i) the **uncompressed size in KB**, (ii) the **compressed size in KB**, and (iii) the **compression ratio**.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Five Components of Multimedia** *(7 Marks)*

> **1. Text [1.5 Marks]** — The most fundamental medium. Carries precise, indexable information. Properties: font, size, style, colour, alignment. Examples: subtitles, captions, e-book body text. Files: `.txt`, `.docx`, `.pdf`.

> **2. Graphics / Images [1.5 Marks]** — Still visuals. Two broad types: **bitmap (raster)** — pixel-based, e.g., JPEG, PNG, BMP; and **vector** — equation-based, e.g., SVG. Used for diagrams, photos, illustrations.

> **3. Audio [1 Mark]** — Continuous time-domain signal. Captured via microphone, digitised using ADC per Nyquist rule. Used for narration, music, alerts. Files: WAV (lossless), MP3 (lossy).

> **4. Video [1.5 Marks]** — Time-sequenced frames, typically 24–60 fps, with synchronized audio. Largest bandwidth consumer. Examples: lectures, films, CCTV. Files: MP4, MKV, AVI.

> **5. Animation [1.5 Marks]** — Motion produced either by frame-sequencing (2D animation) or by transforming vector objects (Flash). Distinguished from video in that the content is *synthetically generated*, not captured.

**Part (b) — Image File Size Calculation** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Total pixels.**

$$
P = W \times H = 1024 \times 768 = 786{,}432 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step 2 — File size in bits.**

$$
S_{\text{bits}} = P \times D = 786{,}432 \times 16 = 12{,}582{,}912 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — File size in bytes.**

$$
S_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{12{,}582{,}912}{8} = 1{,}572{,}864 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — File size in KB.**

$$
S_{\text{KB}} = \frac{1{,}572{,}864}{1024} \approx 1536 \text{ KB} = 1.5 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 5 — File size in MB (re-check).**

$$
S_{\text{MB}} = \frac{1{,}536}{1024} \approx 1.5 \text{ MB}
$$

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Total pixels | **786,432 pixels** |
| Size in bits | **12,582,912 bits** |
| Size in KB | **1,536 KB** |
| Size in MB | **≈ 1.5 MB** |

---

### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Multimedia System Architecture** *(7 Marks)*

A multimedia system is a **coordinated stack of six layers**, each performing a distinct function:

1. **Capture / Authoring Layer** — Acquires raw media using devices (camera, mic, scanner) and authoring tools (Photoshop, Audacity). *(1 Mark)*
2. **Storage Layer** — Stores raw and processed media on disks, SSDs, optical media, or the cloud. *(1 Mark)*
3. **Processing / Compression Layer** — Codecs (JPEG, MP3, H.264) compress media for efficient storage and transmission. *(1.5 Marks)*
4. **Delivery / Network Layer** — Transports media using protocols (HTTP, RTP) over wired/wireless networks. *(1 Mark)*
5. **Presentation / User-Interface Layer** — Renders media to the user via browser, player, or app. *(1.5 Marks)*
6. **Interaction / Feedback Layer** — Accepts user input (touch, voice, gestures) to enable interactivity. *(1 Mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Required:** Students are expected to draw the layered block diagram (see Section 4.2 of these notes for reference). A missing diagram typically attracts a 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

**Part (b) — Audio File Size with Compression** *(7 Marks)*

**Given:** Stereo (C = 2), $f_s = 22{,}050$ Hz, $D_b = 8$ bits, $T = 4 \times 60 = 240$ s, CR = 5.

**Step 1 — Uncompressed size in bits.**

$$
S_{\text{uncomp}} = f_s \times D_b \times C \times T
$$

$$
S_{\text{uncomp}} = 22{,}050 \times 8 \times 2 \times 240
$$

$$
= 22{,}050 \times 8 = 176{,}400
$$

$$
176{,}400 \times 2 = 352{,}800
$$

$$
352{,}800 \times 240 = 84{,}672{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Uncompressed size in KB.**

$$
S_{\text{KB,uncomp}} = \frac{84{,}672{,}000}{8 \times 1024} = \frac{84{,}672{,}000}{8192} \approx 10{,}336 \text{ KB} \approx 10.09 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compressed size.**

$$
S_{\text{comp}} = \frac{S_{\text{uncomp}}}{\text{CR}} = \frac{10{,}336}{5} = 2067.2 \text{ KB} \approx 2.02 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4 — Compression ratio.**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{10{,}336}{2067.2} = 5:1
$$

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Uncompressed size | **≈ 10,336 KB (≈ 10.09 MB)** |
| Compressed size | **≈ 2,067 KB (≈ 2.02 MB)** |
| Compression ratio | **5 : 1** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion chain.** A common mistake is to divide by 1000 instead of 1024 when converting KB↔MB. KTU strictly expects **1024** because the answers are based on binary storage. Losing 1 mark per wrong conversion is typical.
> 2. **Not stating Nyquist explicitly.** When asked about sampling rate, the magic number $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ must be **named** to fetch full marks.
> 3. **Mixing up Hypertext and Hypermedia.** Many students answer "WWW is hypertext" — this loses 1 mark. WWW is **hypermedia** because it carries non-text media.
> 4. **Omitting the diagram in architecture questions.** Even if described in words, the absence of a labelled diagram attracts a flat 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Using "channels" wrongly in audio.** Stereo = 2 channels, Mono = 1 channel. Forgetting to multiply by the channel count is a frequent silent error.
> 6. **Conflating animation and video.** Animation is *synthetically generated*; video is *captured*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet-Point Cheat Sheet**

- **Definition:** Multimedia = integration of **text + images + audio + video + animation** delivered by a computer, often with interactivity.
- **Five components:** Text, Images, Audio, Video, Animation. *All five must be named explicitly for full credit.*
- **Hypertext vs Hypermedia:** Hypertext = text only; Hypermedia = all media. WWW = Hypermedia.
- **Linear vs Non-Linear:** Linear = fixed sequence (film); Non-Linear = user navigates (e-learning).
- **Six-layer architecture:** Capture → Storage → Processing/Compression → Delivery → Presentation → Interaction.
- **Image size formula:** $S = W \times H \times D$ (bits).
- **Audio size formula:** $S = f_s \times D_b \times C \times T$ (bits).
- **Video size formula:** $S = W \times H \times D \times f \times T \div \text{CR}$ (bits).
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ → CD uses 44.1 kHz.
- **Compression ratio:** $CR = \frac{\text{Original}}{\text{Compressed}}$.
- **Space savings:** $\left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100$ %.
- **Unit conversions:** **1 byte = 8 bits**; **1 KB = 1024 bytes**; **1 MB = 1024 KB**. Use 1024, not 1000.
- **Real-world relevance:** Telemedicine, e-learning, OTT streaming, AR/VR, gaming, engineering simulations.
- **Common exam traps:** Wrong conversion factor (1000 vs 1024); missing channels in audio; missing diagram in architecture questions; confusing animation with video.

> **End of Module 4 — Introduction to Multimedia Notes** ✓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authoring and Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Authoring and Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Multimedia Authoring Systems

A **Multimedia Authoring System** is an integrated software environment (or programming toolkit) that enables creators — designers, instructional technologists, content developers, and engineers — to **assemble, organize, integrate, and publish** heterogeneous multimedia assets (text, 2D/3D graphics, digital audio, digital video, animation, and interactivity scripts) into a single, coherent, navigable, and distributable application, without necessarily writing low-level source code.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST527)** reference framework, an authoring tool provides:
- A **visual/programmatic canvas** for content placement.
- An **event-driven interaction model** linking user actions to media responses.
- A **timeline or flowchart** for temporal and logical control.
- A **playback/run-time engine** that compiles or interprets the project for delivery on a target platform (Web, Desktop, Mobile, Kiosk, CD-ROM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Authoring and Tools):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires the learner to differentiate between **authoring metaphors** (icon-based, time-line based, card/slide-based, scripting-based), identify **representative commercial tools**, and apply **selection criteria** when choosing an authoring platform for a given multimedia project.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Film Director's Workbench"

Think of an **authoring system** as the **digital workbench of a film director**:

| Film Director's Activity | Authoring Tool Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Gathering raw footage, audio clips, props | Importing media assets (images, video, WAV/MP3 files) |
| Sticking stills on a storyboard | Placing icons/cards on a canvas or slides |
| Deciding the order of scenes | Defining the timeline or sequence of events |
| Telling an actor: "When the audience presses this button, you do X" | Writing an event-handler script (interaction logic) |
| Final cut & distribution master | Compilation to an executable / Web player file |

In short, an authoring tool **hides the complexity of programming**, while still offering enough power to choreograph every element of the multimedia experience.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** An authoring tool is to a multimedia developer what an **IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** is to a programmer — it provides editing, debugging, asset management, and a delivery mechanism, all rolled into one.

---

## 1.3 Why Authoring Tools Matter — The Engineering Justification

Without a structured authoring environment, the developer would have to manually:
1. Write code in C/C++/Java for every graphical draw call.
2. Hand-code audio playback APIs (e.g., DirectSound, OpenAL).
3. Implement windowing, event loops, and rendering pipelines from scratch.
4. Manually manage memory, threads, and synchronization of media streams.

Authoring tools **abstract all of this**, dramatically reducing the **Man-Months of effort**, **defect density**, and **time-to-market**, while enabling non-programmers (instructional designers, artists, marketing teams) to build rich interactive media.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric:** Industry data consistently places authoring-tool productivity gains in the range of **3× to 10×** compared to hand-coding equivalent multimedia logic in a general-purpose language — a critical *Return on Investment (ROI)* figure cited in **KTU multimedia project planning** modules.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Authoring Systems (Four Canonical Metaphors)

The KTU 2024 syllabus, in alignment with standard multimedia textbooks (e.g., Vaughan, Tay Vaughan), recognizes **four primary authoring metaphors**. Each represents a fundamentally different mental model for the developer.

### 2.1.1 Icon-Based / Event-Driven Authoring
- **Mental Model:** A **flowchart** of icons. Each icon represents a media event (play sound, show image, jump to frame).
- **Strength:** Extremely intuitive for **CBT (Computer-Based Training)**, simulations, and branching narratives.
- **Weakness:** Becomes unwieldy for large projects with hundreds of icons.
- **Representative Tools:** **Adobe Authorware**, **IconAuthor**, **MediaForge**.

### 2.1.2 Timeline-Based Authoring
- **Mental Model:** A horizontal **timeline** (frames × layers). Media is synchronized to time.
- **Strength:** Excellent for **animation-heavy** and **time-synchronized** content (music videos, cartoons, presentations).
- **Weakness:** Interaction logic is awkward to model on a pure timeline.
- **Representative Tools:** **Adobe Flash (Animate)**, **Macromedia Director**, **Apple Motion**.

### 2.1.3 Card / Slide-Based Authoring (Page-Based)
- **Mental Model:** A **stack of cards or slides**, navigated linearly or via hyperlinks/buttons.
- **Strength:** Mirrors the **presentation mindset**; easy to author, easy to review.
- **Weakness:** Poor support for complex branching or synchronization.
- **Representative Tools:** **Microsoft PowerPoint**, **Adobe Captivate**, **HyperStudio**.

### 2.1.4 Scripting / Object-Based Authoring
- **Mental Model:** A **programming environment** with media objects as first-class citizens. Developer writes event handlers in a scripting language (Lingo, ActionScript, JavaScript).
- **Strength:** Maximum flexibility, scalability, and procedural control.
- **Weakness:** Steepest learning curve; requires programming competence.
- **Representative Tools:** **Adobe Director (Lingo)**, **Flash (ActionScript)**, **Unity (C#)**.

---

## 2.2 Authoring Tool Categories (by Application Domain)

| Category | Purpose | Typical Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Presentation Tools** | Linear slide-based communication | MS PowerPoint, Google Slides, Keynote |
| **Time-Based Tools** | Animation, video, audio sync | Adobe Animate, After Effects, Director |
| **Interactive Authoring** | Branching CBT, simulations | Authorware, Captivate, Articulate Storyline |
| **Web / Mobile Multimedia** | Browser/app delivery | Adobe Animate + HTML5, Hype, Tumult Hype |
| **Game / VR Authoring** | Real-time 3D, physics, scripting | Unity, Unreal Engine, Godot |
| **E-Learning / LMS-aware** | SCORM/xAPI compliance | Articulate, iSpring, Adobe Learning Manager |

---

## 2.3 Authoring Process (The Standard Production Pipeline)

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies the following **six-stage authoring pipeline**:

1. **Requirement Analysis** — Define audience, objectives, platform, delivery medium.
2. **Storyboarding / Scripting** — Create a textual & visual blueprint of every screen/scene.
3. **Asset Acquisition / Creation** — Produce or import text, graphics, audio, video, animation.
4. **Assembly / Authoring** — Place assets on the canvas; define interactions; synchronize.
5. **Testing & Debugging** — Verify playback, navigation, performance, and usability.
6. **Publishing / Distribution** — Compile to a deliverable (EXE, Web bundle, mobile package, SCORM zip).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Selection Criteria Matrix

When the KTU board asks *"How do you choose an authoring tool for a project?"*, examiners expect students to discuss the following **nine canonical selection criteria** (Vaughan's framework, KTU-mandated):

| # | Criterion | What to Evaluate |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Media Integration Capability** | Does it natively support required media (text, 2D, 3D, audio, video, streaming)? |
| 2 | **Interactivity Support** | What level of interaction is achievable (linear → branching → procedural)? |
| 3 | **Ergonomics / Learning Curve** | How quickly can a competent developer become productive? |
| 4 | **Programming Flexibility** | Is a scripting language available for custom logic? |
| 5 | **Performance & Footprint** | Runtime size, RAM/CPU consumption, scalability. |
| 6 | **Portability / Platform Support** | Web, Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, kiosk. |
| 7 | **Cost / Licensing Model** | One-time, subscription, royalty-free runtime. |
| 8 | **Standards Compliance** | SCORM, xAPI, AICC, LTI for e-learning. |
| 9 | **Documentation & Community** | Tutorials, forums, vendor support, third-party assets. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Authoring tools underpin several billion-dollar industries:
- **EdTech** — SCORM-compliant e-learning modules delivered via LMS platforms (Moodle, Canvas, SAP SuccessFactors).
- **Digital Marketing** — Interactive web ads, product configurators, AR try-on experiences.
- **Industrial Training** — VR/AR-based maintenance simulators (Unity + Authoring pipeline).
- **Heritage & Museums** — Interactive kiosk walkthroughs (Director + Authorware legacy stacks).
- **Healthcare Simulation** — Surgical training modules with branching patient scenarios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Principle:** The choice of authoring tool is a **software-architecture decision** that constrains every subsequent layer — performance budget, deployment topology, accessibility, and even copyright compliance. Choosing poorly *early* can result in costly refactors later.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Because "Authoring and Tools" is a **systems-and-methodology** topic rather than a closed-form mathematical topic, this section presents an **analytical derivation of the authoring workflow** using a structured engineering-decision framework, followed by **comparative analytical matrices** that the KTU board treats with the same rigor as formula derivations.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Choosing an Authoring Tool — A 5-Step Decision Algorithm

**Problem Statement:** Given a multimedia project with a defined set of constraints, systematically arrive at the most suitable authoring tool.

Let us define the project constraint vector:

$$
\vec{P} \;=\; (M, I, T, B, D, S, P_f)
$$

Where each component is a constraint variable:
- $M$ = **Media complexity index** (types and count of media elements).
- $I$ = **Interactivity depth** (1 = linear, 2 = branching, 3 = procedural).
- $T$ = **Timeline synchronization requirement** (boolean / scaled 0–10).
- $B$ = **Budget** (in INR / USD for licensing).
- $D$ = **Deployment target** (Web, Desktop, Mobile, Kiosk).
- $S$ = **Skill level of author** (1 = novice, 5 = expert programmer).
- $P_f$ = **Performance footprint ceiling** (max MB for final deliverable).

---

### Step 1 — Compute the *Interactivity Quotient* ($IQ$)

$$
IQ \;=\; \alpha \cdot I + \beta \cdot T + \gamma \cdot M
$$

Where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are **project-weighted coefficients** assigned by the requirement analyst, subject to:

$$
\alpha + \beta + \gamma \;=\; 1.0
$$

> *Explanation:* A CBT-heavy project (high $I$, low $T$) yields a **branching-leaning** $IQ$ favouring icon-based tools. An animation-heavy promo (low $I$, high $T$) yields a **timeline-leaning** $IQ$ favouring time-based tools.

---

### Step 2 — Map $IQ$ to a Metaphor Class

$$
\text{Metaphor} \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\text{Icon-Based} & \text{if } I \geq 2 \;\land\; T < 5 \;\land\; M \leq 7 \\
\text{Timeline-Based} & \text{if } T \geq 5 \;\land\; I \leq 1 \\
\text{Card/Slide-Based} & \text{if } I = 1 \;\land\; T < 3 \;\land\; M \leq 5 \\
\text{Scripting/Object-Based} & \text{if } S \geq 4 \;\lor\; I = 3
\end{cases}
$$

> *Explanation:* This logic table is the **decision kernel** of the algorithm. It maps the abstract project constraint vector onto one of the four canonical authoring metaphors.

---

### Step 3 — Apply Deployment Filter

Filter candidate tools by the deployment vector $D$:

$$
\text{Tool}_{\text{eligible}} \;=\; \{ t \in \text{ToolSet} \;\mid\; \text{DeploysTo}(t) \supseteq D \}
$$

> *Explanation:* Tools whose runtime cannot reach the deployment target are eliminated outright. For example, a project targeting iOS/Android eliminates pure desktop-only stacks.

---

### Step 4 — Apply Budget & Performance Constraints

$$
\text{Tool}_{\text{feasible}} \;=\; \{ t \in \text{Tool}_{\text{eligible}} \;\mid\; \text{Cost}(t) \leq B \;\land\; \text{Footprint}(t) \leq P_f \}
$$

> *Explanation:* Surviving tools must satisfy the *commercial* and *engineering performance* ceilings.

---

### Step 5 — Select Maximum-Ergonomics Tool

From the feasible set, pick the tool maximizing **ergonomics score**:

$$
t^{\star} \;=\; \arg\max_{t \in \text{Tool}_{\text{feasible}}} \; \text{Ergonomics}(t)
$$

Where $\text{Ergonomics}(t)$ is computed as:

$$
\text{Ergonomics}(t) \;=\; w_1 \cdot \text{Docs}(t) + w_2 \cdot \text{Community}(t) + w_3 \cdot \frac{1}{\text{LearningCurve}(t)}
$$

> *Explanation:* Among all technically and commercially feasible tools, the one with the highest combined documentation, community, and ease-of-learning score is selected — minimizing project delivery risk.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Evaluation of Major Authoring Tools

| Tool (Vendor) | Metaphor | Scripting | Deployment | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Adobe Animate** (Adobe Inc.) | Timeline + Object | ActionScript / JavaScript | Web (HTML5), Mobile, Desktop | Industry standard for web animation; HTML5 export | ActionScript legacy being phased out |
| **Adobe Director** (Adobe Inc.) | Timeline + Scripting | Lingo | Desktop, Web (Shockwave — discontinued) | Powerful multimedia for CD-ROM era | Runtime discontinued; legacy use only |
| **Adobe Authorware** (Adobe Inc.) | Icon-Based | Authorware Script | Desktop (legacy), Web plug-in | Industry gold standard for CBT | Discontinued; replaced by Captivate |
| **Microsoft PowerPoint** (Microsoft) | Card/Slide-Based | VBA Macros | Desktop, Web (PowerPoint Online) | Ubiquitous, low learning curve | Limited interactivity and media types |
| **Articulate Storyline** (Articulate) | Slide + Branching | JavaScript-like triggers | Web (HTML5), SCORM/xAPI compliant | Dominant in corporate e-learning | Subscription cost; limited video editing |
| **iSpring Suite** (iSpring) | PowerPoint + Add-on | None directly | Web (HTML5), SCORM/xAPI | Converts PowerPoint to e-learning | Depends on PowerPoint skill |
| **Unity** (Unity Technologies) | Object / Scene-based | C# | All platforms incl. VR/AR | Real-time 3D, vast asset store | Steep learning curve; overkill for 2D |
| **Unreal Engine** (Epic Games) | Object / Blueprint-based | Blueprint (visual) / C++ | All platforms incl. AAA, VR | Photorealistic 3D, Blueprint visual scripting | Heavy; not suited for simple 2D |
| **Adobe Captivate** (Adobe Inc.) | Slide + Interactive | JavaScript | Web, SCORM/xAPI | Strong responsive e-learning | Heavy footprint; subscription only |
| **Apple Keynote** (Apple) | Card/Slide-Based | AppleScript (limited) | macOS, iCloud, Web | Excellent visual quality | Apple ecosystem lock-in |
| **HyperStudio** (Roger Wagner) | Card-Based | HyperTalk | macOS / Windows (legacy) | Educational use | Niche; limited modern support |
| **ToolBook** (SumTotal Systems) | Card + Object | OpenScript | Desktop, Web (legacy) | CBT-grade interactivity | Legacy; declining usage |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Selection Algorithm

**Scenario:** A KTU final-year project requires building an **interactive biology simulation** for Class 11 students. It must (a) include animations synchronized to narration, (b) have branching quizzes, (c) be deliverable as an **HTML5 web package** under a **₹20,000 student budget**, and (d) the team has 2 members with basic JavaScript exposure but **no C++/Unreal** experience.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute $IQ$. Suppose the team assigns:
- $\alpha = 0.5$ (high interactivity weight)
- $\beta = 0.3$ (animation importance)
- $\gamma = 0.2$ (media variety)
- $I = 2$ (branching quizzes)
- $T = 7$ (animation sync critical)
- $M = 6$ (text + 2D graphics + audio + video + animation)

$$
IQ \;=\; (0.5 \cdot 2) + (0.3 \cdot 7) + (0.2 \cdot 6) \;=\; 1.0 + 2.1 + 1.2 \;=\; 4.3
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the metaphor decision rules:
- $I = 2$ is *not* $\geq 2$ and $< 5$ simultaneously → icon rule fails by boundary.
- $T \geq 5$ holds → **Timeline-based is viable**.
- $S = 3$ (basic JS), so $S \geq 4$ fails → scripting rule fails.
- $I = 1$ fails → card rule fails.

**Decision:** **Timeline-based metaphor** with optional scripting extension.

**Step 3:** Apply deployment filter $D = \{\text{Web}\}$:
- Surviving: Adobe Animate, Articulate Storyline, iSpring, Captivate.
- Eliminated: Unity (heavy), PowerPoint (weak scripting), Director (legacy).

**Step 4:** Apply budget $B = 20{,}000$ and footprint $P_f = 50$ MB:
- Adobe Animate: subscription ≈ ₹17,500/year ✓ → footprint low ✓
- Articulate Storyline: ≈ ₹60,000/year ✗ (over budget)
- Captivate: ≈ ₹30,000/year ✗
- iSpring: ≈ ₹25,000/year ✗ (over budget but with student discount may work)

**Decision:** **Adobe Animate** wins the budget-and-deployment filter.

**Step 5:** Maximize ergonomics. Animate has:
- Massive community and Adobe tutorials.
- JavaScript compatibility (team already knows JS).
- HTML5 Canvas export for Web delivery.

$$
t^{\star} \;=\; \text{Adobe Animate}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The team's project should be built using **Adobe Animate**, leveraging its timeline for animation sync and ActionScript/JavaScript for the branching quiz logic.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — The Authoring Workflow Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Requirements] --> A1[Storyboard & Script]
    A1 --> A2[Asset Acquisition]
    A2 --> A3[Authoring Environment]
    A3 --> A4[Testing and Debugging]
    A4 --> A5[Publishing and Distribution]
    A5 --> A6[End User Runtime]

    A3 --> A3a[Canvas or Timeline]
    A3 --> A3b[Event Handlers]
    A3 --> A3c[Media Synchronization]
    A3 --> A3d[Navigation Logic]

    A5 --> A5a[EXE Desktop Bundle]
    A5 --> A5b[HTML5 Web Package]
    A5 --> A5c[Mobile App APK or IPA]
    A5 --> A5d[SCORM LMS Zip]

    A6 --> A6a[Learner / Viewer]
    A6 --> A6b[Interactive Kiosk]
    A6 --> A6c[Mobile User]
    A6 --> A6d[VR Headset]

    classDef startNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class A3a,A3b,A3c,A3d,A5a,A5b,A5c,A5d choiceNode
```

> *Figure 1:* End-to-end authoring-and-distribution pipeline, showing the four internal sub-systems of the **Authoring Environment** and the four possible **publishing output formats**.

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Map — The Four Authoring Metaphors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[Authoring Systems]

    M0 --> M1[Icon or Event Driven]
    M0 --> M2[Timeline Based]
    M0 --> M3[Card or Slide Based]
    M0 --> M4[Scripting or Object Based]

    M1 --> M1a[Adobe Authorware]
    M1 --> M1b[IconAuthor]
    M1 --> M1c[Strength Branching CBT]

    M2 --> M2a[Adobe Animate]
    M2 --> M2b[Adobe Director]
    M2 --> M2c[Strength Animation Sync]

    M3 --> M3a[MS PowerPoint]
    M3 --> M3b[HyperStudio]
    M3 --> M3c[Strength Quick Presentations]

    M4 --> M4a[Unity C Sharp]
    M4 --> M4b[Director Lingo]
    M4 --> M4c[Strength Full Procedural Control]

    classDef root fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef metaphor fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef tool fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef strength fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class M1,M2,M3,M4 metaphor

```

> *Figure 2:* Decision-tree mapping from the abstract concept of "Authoring System" down to specific commercial tools, grouped by authoring metaphor.

---

## 4.3 Event-Driven Interaction Model (How an Authoring Tool Responds to User Action)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U0[User Action] --> U1{Event Detected by Runtime}

    U1 -->|Mouse Click| U2[OnClick Handler]
    U1 -->|Key Press| U3[OnKey Handler]
    U1 -->|Touch| U4[OnTouch Handler]
    U1 -->|Timer Elapsed| U5[OnFrame Handler]

    U2 --> R0[Routing Logic]
    U3 --> R0
    U4 --> R0
    U5 --> R0

    R0 --> R1[Play Audio Clip]
    R0 --> R2[Display Image or Video]
    R0 --> R3[Navigate to Next Scene]
    R0 --> R4[Update Variable State]
    R0 --> R5[Trigger Animation]

    R1 --> X0[Updated Visual and Audio Output]
    R2 --> X0
    R3 --> X0
    R4 --> X0
    R5 --> X0

    classDef userNode fill:#17becf,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eventNode fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef handlerNode fill:#8c564b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef actionNode fill:#e377c2,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> *Figure 3:* The runtime event loop of a generic multimedia application. Each user-triggered event is dispatched to a routing layer that invokes media or logic responses, producing updated visual/audio output — this is the **core execution model** of every interactive authoring tool.

---

## 4.4 Multimedia Building Blocks — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    BB5 --> BB5a[MPEG4, WebM, HLS, DASH]

```

> *Figure 4:* Block diagram of a multimedia application's **eight major building blocks**. An authoring tool integrates and orchestrates all of these into a single deliverable.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a multimedia authoring system. List the four major types of authoring tools.

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **multimedia authoring system** is a software environment that allows the creation and integration of multiple media types — text, graphics, audio, video, and animation — into a single interactive application, often without requiring low-level programming.

**Four Major Types:**
1. **Icon-Based / Event-Driven** (e.g., Adobe Authorware) — flowcharts of icons.
2. **Timeline-Based** (e.g., Adobe Animate) — synchronized frames on a timeline.
3. **Card / Slide-Based** (e.g., MS PowerPoint) — sequential or linked pages.
4. **Scripting / Object-Based** (e.g., Unity with C#) — programmatic control over media objects.

---

### Q2. State any three factors that should be considered while selecting an authoring tool for a multimedia project.

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Media Integration Capability** — the tool must natively support all media types required (e.g., if the project needs streaming video, the tool must support H.264/HLS).
2. **Interactivity Support** — the depth of interaction achievable (linear playback vs. branching vs. procedural logic).
3. **Platform / Deployment Support** — the tool must be able to publish to the target environment (Web, mobile, desktop, kiosk, or LMS-compliant SCORM package).

*(Other valid factors: cost, learning curve, performance footprint, scripting flexibility, standards compliance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each 14-mark question typically contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice is **across questions**, not within.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **four types of multimedia authoring tools** with one representative example and one suitable application domain for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **nine selection criteria** for choosing an authoring tool. Apply any **five** of these criteria to compare **Adobe Animate** and **Microsoft PowerPoint** for building an interactive e-learning module.

#### Model Answer — (a)

**1. Icon-Based / Event-Driven Authoring**
- **Mental Model:** A flowchart of icons, each representing an event (e.g., display image, play audio, wait for user input).
- **Example:** Adobe Authorware.
- **Application Domain:** Computer-Based Training (CBT), branching simulations, decision-making drills where users navigate a flowchart based on choices.

**2. Timeline-Based Authoring**
- **Mental Model:** A horizontal timeline of frames, with multiple layers stacked vertically. Media events are placed along the time axis.
- **Example:** Adobe Animate (formerly Flash).
- **Application Domain:** Web animations, cartoon shorts, multimedia ads where animation is synchronized to narration or music.

**3. Card / Slide-Based Authoring**
- **Mental Model:** A stack of cards or slides, navigated sequentially or via hyperlinks/buttons.
- **Example:** Microsoft PowerPoint.
- **Application Domain:** Business presentations, classroom lectures, simple educational content with linear structure.

**4. Scripting / Object-Based Authoring**
- **Mental Model:** A full programming environment where media objects are properties of classes, and event handlers are written in a scripting language.
- **Example:** Unity (with C# scripting).
- **Application Domain:** Games, VR/AR experiences, complex interactive simulations requiring procedural logic.

#### Model Answer — (b)

**Nine Selection Criteria (recap):**

1. Media Integration
2. Interactivity Support
3. Ergonomics / Learning Curve
4. Programming Flexibility
5. Performance Footprint
6. Portability / Platform Support
7. Cost / Licensing
8. Standards Compliance (SCORM, xAPI)
9. Documentation & Community

**Comparison of Adobe Animate vs. Microsoft PowerPoint** (five criteria):

| Criterion | Adobe Animate | Microsoft PowerPoint |
|---|---|---|
| **Media Integration** | Excellent — supports vector graphics, raster, video, audio, ActionScript objects | Adequate — supports images, audio, video, but limited vector / animation power |
| **Interactivity Support** | Strong — supports click events, branching, ActionScript/JavaScript logic | Limited — basic hyperlinks and trigger animations only |
| **Ergonomics / Learning Curve** | Moderate — requires understanding of timeline and basic scripting | Very low — almost anyone can use it within minutes |
| **Platform Support** | HTML5 export → Web, iOS/Android via AIR; multi-platform | Windows, macOS, iOS, Web (PowerPoint Online) |
| **Standards Compliance** | Limited SCORM support (requires external plugin) | Limited SCORM export (requires iSpring or similar) |

**Conclusion:** For a **richly interactive** e-learning module with branching scenarios and synchronized animations, **Adobe Animate** is the stronger choice. For **simple, content-driven** modules with linear navigation, **PowerPoint** is sufficient and more cost-effective.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **stages of the multimedia authoring process** with a neat flowchart. Explain the role of the **storyboarding** and **testing** stages in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **icon-based** and **timeline-based** authoring tools in terms of mental model, interactivity, animation handling, complexity handling, and a representative example.

#### Model Answer — (a)

**Six Stages of the Authoring Process:**

1. **Requirement Analysis** — Identify audience, learning objectives, platform constraints, deliverable format.
2. **Storyboarding / Scripting** — Create a textual and visual blueprint of every screen, scene, transition, and interaction. A storyboard lists every asset (text, image, audio, video) per slide/scene.
3. **Asset Acquisition** — Source or create media: photographs, illustrations, voice-overs, music, video clips.
4. **Assembly / Authoring** — Import assets into the tool, place them on canvas/timeline, define interactions, synchronize media.
5. **Testing & Debugging** — Play through every navigation path; verify timings, audio levels, broken links, accessibility (alt text, captions), and platform compatibility.
6. **Publishing / Distribution** — Compile to deliverable (HTML5 bundle, SCORM zip, EXE, mobile package); upload to LMS or hosting server.

**Role of Storyboarding:**
- Acts as a **blueprint** that aligns the developer, designer, and subject-matter expert.
- Identifies missing assets early, **reducing rework**.
- Defines navigation flow and decision points explicitly.

**Role of Testing:**
- Detects **broken hyperlinks, missing assets, audio-video desync, performance bottlenecks**.
- Ensures **accessibility compliance** (WCAG: captions, transcripts, alt text).
- Validates **cross-platform compatibility** (browser, OS, mobile device).

#### Model Answer — (b)

| Aspect | Icon-Based Authoring | Timeline-Based Authoring |
|---|---|---|
| **Mental Model** | Flowchart of icons representing events | Horizontal timeline of frames with stacked layers |
| **Interactivity** | Excellent — branching is native via flowchart connectors | Awkward — interactivity is bolted on via scripts/triggers |
| **Animation Handling** | Poor — animation must be pre-rendered as video | Native — frame-by-frame or tweened animation on timeline |
| **Complexity Handling** | Becomes cluttered beyond ~100 icons | Scales well for time-driven content but poor for branching |
| **Representative Example** | Adobe Authorware | Adobe Animate |

**Conclusion:** Icon-based is best for **decision-tree / CBT content**, while timeline-based excels at **animation-driven** content. Modern hybrid tools (e.g., Articulate Storyline, Adobe Captivate) attempt to combine both paradigms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Authoring & Tools Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Authoring Tool" with "Programming Language".** Tools like Unity or Director have scripting layers, but they are *authoring environments*, not raw languages. Examiners deduct marks when students treat them as equivalents.
> 2. **Naming a discontinued tool as the *current* best choice.** Director and Authorware are widely cited in textbooks but their runtimes have been **discontinued**; using them as a present-tense recommendation loses modernization marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to map the tool to the metaphor.** When asked "which tool is icon-based?", students sometimes just name a tool without explaining *why* it belongs to that metaphor.
> 4. **Skipping the deployment-target filter.** Recommending a desktop-only tool for a mobile project loses the *platform viability* marks.
> 5. **No mention of standards (SCORM/xAPI) for e-learning questions.** The KTU 2024 board explicitly tests awareness of LMS integration standards.
> 6. **Confusing "Authoring Tool" with "Multimedia Application".** The tool *creates* the application; it is not itself the deliverable. Many students conflate the two in their introductions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Authoring and Tools (Module 4):**

- ☐ **Definition:** An authoring tool is a software environment that integrates multiple media types into interactive applications.
- ☐ **Four Metaphors:** Icon-based, Timeline-based, Card/Slide-based, Scripting/Object-based.
- ☐ **Icon-Based Examples:** Adobe Authorware (discontinued), IconAuthor — best for CBT/branching.
- ☐ **Timeline-Based Examples:** Adobe Animate, Adobe Director — best for animation sync.
- ☐ **Card-Based Examples:** MS PowerPoint, HyperStudio — best for linear presentations.
- ☐ **Scripting Examples:** Unity (C#), Unreal (C++/Blueprint), Director (Lingo) — best for full procedural control.
- ☐ **Six-Stage Pipeline:** Requirements → Storyboarding → Asset Acquisition → Assembly → Testing → Publishing.
- ☐ **Nine Selection Criteria:** Media, Interactivity, Ergonomics, Programming, Performance, Portability, Cost, Standards, Community/Docs.
- ☐ **SCORM/xAPI:** Mandatory standards for e-learning LMS delivery.
- ☐ **Legacy Warning:** Director, Authorware, Shockwave runtimes are discontinued — mention only for historical/legacy contexts.
- ☐ **Modern Recommendations:** Adobe Animate (Web), Articulate Storyline (e-Learning), Unity (3D/VR), Captivate (Responsive e-Learning).
- ☐ **Always map tool → metaphor → application domain** in answers for full marks.
- ☐ **Decision Algorithm:** Compute IQ → map to metaphor → filter by deployment → filter by budget/performance → maximize ergonomics.
- ☐ **Multimedia Building Blocks:** Text, 2D Graphics, 3D Graphics, Audio, Video, Animation, Interaction, Streaming.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphics and Image Data Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graphics and Image Data Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Graphics and Image Data Representations** refers to the systematic, standardized methods used to encode, store, organize, and transmit visual information (2D images, drawings, photographs) within a digital computer. In KTU 2024 terminology, this encompasses the dual disciplines of **raster (bitmap) graphics** and **vector graphics**, the mathematical **color models** that define pixel color values, and the **file format standards** (BMP, GIF, JPEG, PNG, TIFF) that interleave these representations with practical metadata and compression schemes.

Every digital image is, at the lowest hardware level, a structured array of bits — the **bit depth** (or color depth) determines the number of distinct colors representable per pixel, while the **spatial resolution** (width × height) determines the geometric fidelity.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Core KTU Definition — Digital Image:** A 2D discrete function $f(x, y)$ where $x$ and $y$ are spatial (integer) coordinates and $f$ is the intensity (brightness/color) at that coordinate. For color images, $f$ is a vector with multiple channels (e.g., R, G, B).

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Paradigms

In the KTU 2024 scheme, image representations are broadly classified into two engineering paradigms:

| Paradigm | Synonyms | Storage Basis | Best For |
|----------|----------|---------------|----------|
| **Raster / Bitmap** | Pixel-based, paint representation | Fixed 2D array of pixels (color samples) | Photographs, scanned art, real-world textures |
| **Vector** | Geometric, draw representation | Mathematical primitives (lines, curves, polygons) | Logos, fonts, CAD drawings, technical illustrations |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Painter vs. The Architect):**  
> - A **raster image** is like a **mosaic painting** — millions of tiny colored tiles (pixels) are placed in a fixed grid. If you zoom in too much, you see the individual tiles (pixelation).  
> - A **vector image** is like an **architect's blueprint** — the document stores *instructions* ("draw a circle of radius 5 cm at point (10,10)"), not the actual marks. You can zoom in infinitely and the lines remain perfectly sharp.

### 1.3 Bit Depth and Color Capacity

The number of distinguishable colors in an image is governed by the bit depth $b$ per pixel:

$$N_{colors} = 2^{b}$$

Standard KTU-required bit depths:

| Bit Depth (b) | Colors ($2^b$) | Common Name | Typical Use |
|---------------|----------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | 2 | **Monochrome / Bi-level** | Fax, B&W line art, masks |
| 4 | 16 | Indexed (VGA palette) | Legacy displays |
| 8 | 256 | **Indexed / Grayscale** | GIF, simple icons |
| 16 | 65,536 | High Color | Older displays, RAW |
| 24 | 16,777,216 | **True Color** | JPEG, BMP — full RGB |
| 32 | 4,294,967,296 | True Color + Alpha | PNG, RGBA, transparency |

> [!NOTE]  
> **24-bit "True Color"** uses **8 bits per channel** (R, G, B), giving 256 shades per channel — a direct mapping to standard display hardware, which is why 24 bits is the "natural" color depth for PC graphics.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Categorical Snapshot of Image Types

1. **Binary (1-bit)** — each pixel is either black (0) or white (1).
2. **Grayscale (8-bit)** — each pixel is one of 256 luminance values.
3. **Indexed-color (8-bit)** — pixel stores an index into a 256-entry color lookup table (LUT / palette).
4. **True-color (24-bit RGB)** — pixel stores 3 channels of 8 bits each.
5. **True-color with Alpha (32-bit RGBA)** — adds 8 bits for transparency (0 = transparent, 255 = opaque).

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Raster (Bitmap) Image Architecture — Layered Logic

A raster image is a *structured container* of nested components. Decomposing the engineering pipeline:

**Layer 1 — Header / Metadata Block:**  
- Image width ($W$) and height ($H$) in pixels  
- Bit depth ($b$) and number of color planes  
- Compression type (none, RLE, LZW, DCT)  
- Palette / LUT data (for indexed images)  
- DPI / resolution fields

**Layer 2 — Pixel Data Array:**  
- A 1D byte stream (rows may be stored top-to-bottom or bottom-to-top, depending on format)  
- Total pixel count: $N_{px} = W \times H$  
- Total raw bit stream: $N_{bits} = W \times H \times b$

**Layer 3 — Optional Color Lookup Table (LUT):**  
- For indexed-color images, the LUT has $2^b$ entries, each $3 \times 8 = 24$ bits wide (RGB triplet).  
- LUT size in bytes: $S_{LUT} = 2^b \times 3$

**Layer 4 — Optional Alpha / Mask Channel:**  
- Encodes per-pixel transparency. Frequently packed as a 4th byte in 32-bit images.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why does the KTU 2024 scheme emphasize this layered view?** Because the *file size of a raster image* is the sum of header overhead + pixel data + palette + alpha. Students who forget any of these layers will derive the wrong file size in numerical problems.

### 2.2 Vector Graphics — Object Hierarchy

Vector images store a scene as a list of *geometric primitives*:

- **Primitives:** line, polyline, polygon, circle, ellipse, Bézier curve (quadratic & cubic), text glyphs.
- **Attributes:** stroke color, fill color, line width, line style (solid/dashed), opacity, transformation matrix.
- **Scene Graph / Grouping:** primitives are nested in a tree (e.g., SVG DOM, PDF object tree).

> **Key Engineering Property:** Vector files store **resolution-independent** descriptions. Render quality is bounded only by the *renderer's* precision — which is why logos and CAD data are always vector.

### 2.3 Color Models — The KTU Big Four

| Model | Channels | Domain | Primary Use |
|-------|----------|--------|-------------|
| **RGB** | R, G, B | 0–255 each (additive) | Displays, scanners, cameras |
| **CMY / CMYK** | C, M, Y (K=black) | 0–100% (subtractive) | Printing presses |
| **HSI / HSV** | Hue, Saturation, Intensity/Value | Polar color wheel | Image editing, color picking |
| **YIQ** | Y (luma), I, Q (chrominance) | Broadcast analog TV | NTSC television |

**CMY from RGB (subtractive complement):**
$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 1 - R \\
M &= 1 - G \\
Y &= 1 - B
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> Each value is normalized to the [0, 1] range. CMYK adds a separate black (K) channel because mixing C, M, Y inks produces a muddy brown, not a true black — this is a **practical printing reality**, not a mathematical necessity.

**YIQ from RGB (NTSC standard):**
$$\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B \\
I &= 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B \\
Q &= 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.311B
\end{aligned}$$

> **Why the strange coefficients?** They reflect the human eye's greater sensitivity to green than to red or blue — the Y channel (luminance) is essentially a perceptual brightness measure.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Memorize for ESE)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Number of distinct colors | $N = 2^{b}$ | dimensionless | $b$ = bit depth |
| 2 | Raw image data size (bytes) | $S = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8}$ | bytes | Excludes header & palette |
| 3 | Total file size (indexed) | $S_{total} = \dfrac{W H b}{8} + 2^{b} \cdot 3 + H_{header}$ | bytes | Adds palette + header |
| 4 | Aspect ratio | $AR = \dfrac{W}{H}$ | ratio | E.g., 4:3, 16:9 |
| 5 | Total pixels | $N_{px} = W \times H$ | pixels | — |
| 6 | PPI / DPI resolution | $R = \dfrac{W}{W_{inches}}$ | pixels/inch | Same as DPI informally |
| 7 | Physical print size | $W_{in} = \dfrac{W}{DPI}$ | inches | — |
| 8 | Compression ratio | $CR = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}$ | ratio | $\geq 1$ |
| 9 | Compression savings (%) | $Savings = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100\%$ | % | — |
| 10 | Color capacity (RGB) | $N = 2^{8} \cdot 2^{8} \cdot 2^{8} = 2^{24}$ | colors | True color |
| 11 | Storage (8-bit grayscale per pixel) | $1$ byte | byte/pixel | Common benchmark |
| 12 | RGB $\rightarrow$ CMY (per channel) | $C = 1 - R_{norm}$ | [0,1] | Normalize RGB first |
| 13 | Luminance (Y in YIQ) | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | [0,1] or 0–255 | NTSC weighted |
| 14 | Gamma correction (encoding) | $V_{out} = V_{in}^{\gamma}$ | [0,1] | $\gamma \approx 2.2$ for sRGB |
| 15 | Palette / LUT size | $S_{LUT} = 2^{b} \times 3$ | bytes | For 8-bit indexed only |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas drive real-world decisions — web designers use formula (1) to choose 8-bit PNGs for icons, formula (6) to set CSS pixel density, and formula (2) to estimate bandwidth on a streaming service. Mastering them is essential for both ESE marks and any multimedia engineering interview.

### 2.5 Standard Image File Format Comparison (KTU 2024 Required Knowledge)

| Format | Type | Color Depth | Compression | Transparency | Best Use |
|--------|------|-------------|-------------|--------------|----------|
| **BMP** | Raster | 1, 4, 8, 24, 32 | None / RLE | Optional (alpha) | Uncompressed Windows bitmap |
| **GIF** | Raster | 8-bit indexed | LZW (lossless) | 1-bit (binary) | Animations, simple web graphics |
| **JPEG** | Raster | 24-bit | DCT (lossy) | None | Photographs |
| **PNG** | Raster | 8/24/32 | DEFLATE (lossless) | Full 8-bit alpha | Web images needing transparency |
| **TIFF** | Raster | up to 48-bit | None / LZW / JPEG | Optional | Professional publishing, scanning |
| **SVG** | Vector | N/A | None (XML text) | Full | Logos, scalable web icons |
| **EPS / PDF** | Vector | N/A | None / LZW | Optional | Print publishing |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Lossy vs. Lossless — KTU Hot Topic:**  
> - **Lossless** (PNG, GIF, BMP, TIFF): the decompressed image is *bit-identical* to the original. Smaller savings.  
> - **Lossy** (JPEG): discards perceptually invisible information via DCT + quantization. Much smaller files, but generation loss accumulates on re-encoding.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Raw Image File Size (Full Worked Form)

We derive the closed-form expression for the on-disk size of a raster image, including all real overhead components.

**Given:** A 24-bit RGB image of width $W$ pixels and height $H$ pixels, with no palette and no alpha.

**Step 1 — Bits per pixel:**  
24 bits / pixel $= 3$ bytes / pixel (since 8 bits $= 1$ byte).

**Step 2 — Total pixel count:**  
$N_{px} = W \times H$

**Step 3 — Raw pixel data size in bytes:**  
$S_{pixels} = N_{px} \times \dfrac{24}{8} = W \times H \times 3$

**Step 4 — File header overhead:**  
Most BMP-style formats add a fixed header $H_{hdr} \approx 54$ bytes (BITMAPFILEHEADER + BITMAPINFOHEADER).

**Step 5 — Optional palette:**  
For 24-bit images, the palette is empty. For 8-bit indexed images, $S_{LUT} = 2^{8} \times 3 = 768$ bytes.

**Step 6 — Optional alpha mask:**  
If 32-bit RGBA, add 1 byte per pixel: $S_{\alpha} = W \times H$.

**Final consolidated formula:**

$$S_{total} = W \cdot H \cdot \left\lceil \dfrac{b}{8} \right\rceil + S_{LUT} + S_{\alpha} + H_{hdr}$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling (because partial bytes round up).

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example 1 — File Size of a True-Color Photograph

**Problem:** A digital camera captures a $1920 \times 1080$ RGB image. Calculate: (a) raw data size in MB, (b) compressed JPEG size if compression ratio is 12:1.

**Solution:**

**(a) Raw size:**  
$b = 24$ bits $= 3$ bytes per pixel.  
$N_{px} = 1920 \times 1080 = 2,073,600$ pixels.

$$S = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 3 = 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ bytes}$$

Converting to megabytes (1 MB = $1024 \times 1024 = 1,048,576$ bytes):

$$S_{MB} = \dfrac{6{,}220{,}800}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 5.93 \text{ MB}$$

**(b) Compressed JPEG size with $CR = 12:1$:**

$$S_{comp} = \dfrac{S}{CR} = \dfrac{6{,}220{,}800}{12} = 518{,}400 \text{ bytes} \approx 0.494 \text{ MB}$$

$$Savings = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{12}\right) \times 100\% \approx 91.67\%$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example 2 — Indexed Color with Palette

**Problem:** An 8-bit indexed image is $640 \times 480$ pixels. Calculate total file size including the 256-entry palette and 54-byte header.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pixel data:**
$S_{pixels} = 640 \times 480 \times 1 = 307{,}200$ bytes

**Step 2 — Palette size:**
$S_{LUT} = 2^{8} \times 3 = 768$ bytes

**Step 3 — Header:**
$H_{hdr} = 54$ bytes

**Step 4 — Total:**
$$S_{total} = 307{,}200 + 768 + 54 = 308{,}022 \text{ bytes} \approx 300.8 \text{ KB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Observation for the KTU examiner:** The 768-byte palette is a tiny overhead here ($< 0.25\%$), but for very small images (e.g., $32 \times 32$ icons) the palette becomes a *significant* fraction of the file — an important design trade-off.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example 3 — Color Capacity & Bit Depth

**Problem:** A scanner advertises 36-bit color. How many distinct colors can it represent? Express in scientific notation.

**Solution:**

$$N = 2^{36} = 68{,}719{,}476{,}736 \approx 6.87 \times 10^{10} \text{ colors}$$

(About 68.7 billion colors, often called "deep color.")

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example 4 — RGB to CMY Conversion

**Problem:** A pixel has $R = 200$, $G = 50$, $B = 100$ (each on 0–255). Convert to CMY as percentages.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Normalize to [0, 1]:**
$$R_{n} = \dfrac{200}{255} \approx 0.784, \quad G_{n} = \dfrac{50}{255} \approx 0.196, \quad B_{n} = \dfrac{100}{255} \approx 0.392$$

**Step 2 — Apply CMY equations:**
$$C = 1 - 0.784 = 0.216 = 21.6\%$$
$$M = 1 - 0.196 = 0.804 = 80.4\%$$
$$Y = 1 - 0.392 = 0.608 = 60.8\%$$

> The pixel is dominated by **magenta** ink (80.4%) — consistent with the original being a reddish-pink color.

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example 5 — YIQ Luminance Extraction

**Problem:** A grayscale printer needs the Y (luma) value of a pixel with $R=100, G=150, B=200$.

**Solution:**

$$Y = 0.299(100) + 0.587(150) + 0.114(200)$$
$$Y = 29.9 + 88.05 + 22.8 = 140.75$$

So the luma is approximately **141** (out of 255) — a relatively bright pixel, as expected for a light blue.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs Across Representations

| Trade-off Axis | Raster Pros | Raster Cons | Vector Pros | Vector Cons |
|----------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Photographic realism | ✅ Excellent | — | ❌ Poor for photos | — |
| Zoom scalability | — | ❌ Pixelation | ✅ Infinite | — |
| File size for complex scenes | — | ❌ Large | ✅ Compact | — |
| File size for simple shapes | ✅ Small | — | — | ❌ Overhead |
| Editability | — | ❌ Per-pixel edits | ✅ Object-level | — |
| Hardware compatibility | ✅ Universal | — | — | ❌ Needs renderer |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```


### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Compression Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IMG[Original Image] --> Q1{Need exact pixel match?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| LL[Lossless Compression]
    Q1 -->|No| LY[Lossy Compression]
    
    LL --> L1[Run Length Encoding RLE]
    LL --> L2[LZW GIF TIFF]
    LL --> L3[DEFLATE PNG ZIP]
    
    LY --> L4[Discrete Cosine Transform JPEG]
    LY --> L5[Wavelet JPEG2000]
    LY --> L6[Vector Quantization Older]
    
    L1 --> OUT1[Small Savings Identity Preserved]
    L2 --> OUT2[Moderate Savings Identity Preserved]
    L3 --> OUT3[High Savings Identity Preserved]
    L4 --> OUT4[High Savings Generation Loss]
    L5 --> OUT5[Very High Savings Better Quality]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why this block diagram matters in KTU ESE:** When asked to "compare lossless and lossy compression with examples," students who can sketch this decision tree automatically score the application-level marks. It demonstrates *engineering judgment*, not just memorization.

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gamma Correction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Linear Light Scene] -->|gamma encode| B[Non Linear sRGB 0 to 255]
    B --> C[Storage Transmission JPEG PNG]
    C --> D[Display Device]
    D -->|gamma decode| E[Perceived Linear Light]
    
    A1[gamma 1 over 2.2 approx 0.45] --> A
    E1[gamma 2.2] --> E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Gamma Correction — KTU high-yield point:** Cameras and displays apply a power-law $V_{out} = V_{in}^{\gamma}$ with $\gamma \approx 1/2.2$ (encoding) and $\gamma \approx 2.2$ (decoding) to match human visual perception, which is roughly logarithmic.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
Differentiate between **raster graphics** and **vector graphics**. State one practical example where each is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Raster Graphics | Vector Graphics |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Storage basis** | Grid of pixels (bit map) | Mathematical primitives (lines, curves) |
| **Resolution dependence** | Resolution-fixed; pixelates on zoom | Resolution-independent; infinitely scalable |
| **File size** | Proportional to $W \times H \times b$ | Proportional to scene complexity (object count) |
| **Best suited for** | Realistic photographs, scanned images | Logos, technical drawings, fonts |
| **Example file** | JPEG, BMP, PNG | SVG, EPS, PDF |
| **Preferred example** | Digital photograph (JPEG) | Company logo (SVG) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Explain the significance of **24-bit color depth**. How many distinct colors can be represented? Mention one disadvantage of using very high color depth.

**Model Answer:**

- A 24-bit color image allocates **8 bits per channel** (R, G, B), giving each channel 256 distinct intensity levels.
- Total distinct colors: $N = 2^{24} = 16,777,216 \approx 16.7$ million colors — known as **True Color**.
- This matches the native capability of standard PC monitors, making it the engineering baseline.
- **Disadvantage of higher bit depths (e.g., 36, 48 bit):** File size increases linearly without proportional perceptual benefit — human eyes cannot distinguish the additional shades, so storage and bandwidth are wasted.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **RGB** and **CMY** color models with their equations. Convert the RGB color $(150, 80, 200)$ to CMY percentages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **BMP file format** structure. A $1024 \times 768$ pixel image is stored as a 24-bit uncompressed BMP. Calculate the total file size in KB, including a 54-byte header and no palette. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RGB and CMY models:**

**RGB (Red, Green, Blue) — Additive Model:**  
Used in displays where colored light is summed. Each channel is independent, range 0–255 (8 bits). Pure black = (0,0,0), pure white = (255,255,255).

**CMY (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow) — Subtractive Model:**  
Used in printing where ink absorbs (subtracts) light from white paper. CMY is the **complement** of RGB:

$$C = 1 - R_{norm}, \quad M = 1 - G_{norm}, \quad Y = 1 - B_{norm}$$

**Conversion of RGB(150, 80, 200):**

Normalize:
$$R_n = \dfrac{150}{255} \approx 0.588, \quad G_n = \dfrac{80}{255} \approx 0.314, \quad B_n = \dfrac{200}{255} \approx 0.784$$

Apply:
$$C = 1 - 0.588 = 0.412 = 41.2\%$$
$$M = 1 - 0.314 = 0.686 = 68.6\%$$
$$Y = 1 - 0.784 = 0.216 = 21.6\%$$

---

**(b) BMP file structure & size calculation:**

**BMP Structure (top to bottom in the file):**

1. **BITMAPFILEHEADER (14 bytes):** Signature "BM", file size, reserved fields, offset to pixel data.
2. **BITMAPINFOHEADER (40 bytes):** Width, height, planes, bit count, compression type, image size, etc.
3. **Color Palette (optional):** For 8-bit and lower — absent in 24-bit.
4. **Pixel Data:** Stored **bottom-up, left-to-right**, with each row padded to a 4-byte boundary.

**File Size Calculation:**

Pixel data:
$$S_{px} = 1024 \times 768 \times 3 = 2,359,296 \text{ bytes}$$

Header:
$$H_{hdr} = 54 \text{ bytes}$$

**Row padding check:** $1024 \times 3 = 3072$ bytes per row, which is already a multiple of 4 — no padding needed.

Total:
$$S_{total} = 2{,}359{,}296 + 54 = 2{,}359{,}350 \text{ bytes} \approx 2304 \text{ KB} \approx 2.25 \text{ MB}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **lossy** and **lossless** compression techniques with two examples each. A photograph of $4000 \times 3000$ pixels is stored as a 24-bit JPEG with a compression ratio of 15:1. Calculate the original raw size in MB and the compressed JPEG size in MB. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **gamma correction** in image display systems. Why is it necessary, and what is the typical value of gamma used in sRGB systems? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lossy vs. Lossless Compression:**

| Feature | Lossless | Lossy |
|---------|----------|-------|
| Decompressed = Original? | ✅ Yes (bit-exact) | ❌ No (approximate) |
| Compression ratio | Low to moderate (2:1 to 3:1 typical) | High (10:1 to 100:1) |
| Suitable for | Text, line art, medical scans, masters | Photographs, streaming video |
| Examples | PNG, GIF, BMP (RLE), TIFF (LZW) | JPEG, JPEG 2000, WebP, MP3, MPEG |

**File Size Calculation:**

Original raw size:
$$S = 4000 \times 3000 \times 3 = 36{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} = \dfrac{36{,}000{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 34.33 \text{ MB}$$

Compressed JPEG size:
$$S_{comp} = \dfrac{36{,}000{,}000}{15} = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 2.29 \text{ MB}$$

Savings:
$$Savings = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{15}\right) \times 100\% \approx 93.3\%$$

---

**(b) Gamma Correction:**

**Definition:** Gamma correction is a nonlinear encoding/decoding of luminance values that compensates for the nonlinear response of human vision and cathode-ray-tube (CRT) displays.

**Why necessary:**  
- The human eye perceives brightness logarithmically, not linearly.  
- If brightness were stored linearly, dark regions would exhibit visible **banding** (posterization).  
- Gamma encoding uses more code values for darker shades, where the eye is more sensitive to differences.

**Equation (encoding):**
$$V_{out} = V_{in}^{\gamma}$$

**Typical gamma value:** $\gamma \approx 1/2.2 \approx 0.45$ for sRGB encoding; the display re-applies $\gamma \approx 2.2$ on decoding. The composite effect is approximately linear light perception.

**Practical effect:** Without gamma correction, images would appear too dark on screen. Every JPEG, PNG, and display pipeline assumes gamma-corrected values.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting row padding in BMP size calculation.** If $W \times b/8$ is not a multiple of 4, the *unpadded* formula is wrong. Always check and add padding bytes.
> 2. **Confusing PPI and DPI.** PPI = pixels per inch (digital image); DPI = dots per inch (print output). The numerical values can be the same, but the engineering context differs.
> 3. **Mixing up color models.** A common error is using RGB coefficients in a YIQ formula. Memorize the **0.299, 0.587, 0.114** NTSC weights explicitly.
> 4. **Compression ratio direction.** $CR = S_{original} / S_{compressed}$, not the inverse. A 15:1 ratio means the compressed file is 1/15 the original.
> 5. **24-bit ≠ 16 million bits.** It is 24 bits per pixel = 3 bytes per pixel = **16.7 million distinct colors**. This is the most-frequently-mis-stated number in the KTU answer scripts.
> 6. **Palette present only for ≤ 8-bit images.** For 24-bit true color, the palette field in BMP is empty — many students incorrectly add 768 bytes.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Raster vs. Vector:** Raster = pixel grid (photographs); Vector = mathematical primitives (logos, CAD).
- **Bit depth & colors:** $N = 2^b$. Standard depths: 1 (binary), 8 (grayscale/indexed), 24 (true color), 32 (RGBA).
- **File size formula:** $S = (W \times H \times b)/8$ bytes (raw). Add header + palette (if any) for total.
- **Aspect ratio:** $W/H$ — e.g., 4:3 (legacy TV), 16:9 (HDTV), 1:1 (square).
- **Resolution:** PPI/DPI = pixels per inch. Physical width (inches) = pixels / PPI.
- **Color models:** RGB (additive, displays), CMY/CMYK (subtractive, print), HSI (editing), YIQ (NTSC TV).
- **CMY from RGB:** $C = 1-R, \ M = 1-G, \ Y = 1-B$ (normalized).
- **Y from RGB (NTSC):** $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$.
- **File formats:** BMP (uncompressed), GIF (8-bit, LZW, animation), JPEG (24-bit, lossy DCT), PNG (lossless, alpha), TIFF (professional), SVG (vector).
- **Lossy vs. Lossless:** Lossy (JPEG) gives small files, introduces generation loss; Lossless (PNG, GIF) preserves exact pixels, smaller savings.
- **Compression ratio:** $CR = S_{orig} / S_{comp}$; savings = $(1 - 1/CR) \times 100\%$.
- **Gamma correction:** $V_{out} = V_{in}^{\gamma}$, with $\gamma \approx 1/2.2$ for sRGB encoding; necessary because human vision is nonlinear.
- **Palette/LUT:** Used only for ≤ 8-bit indexed images; size = $2^b \times 3$ bytes.
- **True Color (24-bit) = 16.7 million colors**, with 8 bits per R, G, B channel.
- **Alpha channel:** Extra 8 bits per pixel for transparency (0=transparent, 255=opaque).
- **BMP row padding:** Each scanline is padded to a 4-byte boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Final KTU ESE Tip:** Always state the *units* in numerical answers (bytes, KB, MB, inches, etc.). Examiners explicitly reward unit-aware answers and deduct marks for ambiguous "size = 200" without a unit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Popular File Formats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Popular File Formats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **File Format** is a standardized way of encoding information for storage in a digital file. It defines the **bitstream structure**, **encoding rules**, and **metadata conventions** used to represent multimedia data (images, audio, video, text, 3D models) on a digital storage medium. In the context of Computer Graphics & Multimedia, file formats are classified into:

- **Image File Formats** (e.g., BMP, JPEG, PNG, GIF, TIFF, SVG)
- **Audio File Formats** (e.g., WAV, MP3, AAC, FLAC, OGG)
- **Video File Formats / Containers** (e.g., AVI, MP4, MKV, MOV)
- **Animation / 3D File Formats** (e.g., GIF, FLV, OBJ, FBX, GLTF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *multimedia file format* is a standardized specification that defines how data (pixels, samples, frames, metadata) is structured, compressed, and stored in a file, ensuring interoperability between different software and hardware platforms.

### 1.2 The "Container vs Codec" Distinction (Critical Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Container** = The box that holds everything (video stream + audio stream + subtitles + metadata).
> **Codec** = The algorithm used to encode/decode the actual data inside the container.

| Term | What it Refers To | Example |
|------|------------------|---------|
| Container | File wrapper (extension) | `.mp4`, `.avi`, `.mkv` |
| Codec | Compression algorithm | H.264 (AVC), H.265 (HEVC), VP9, MPEG-2 |
| Audio Codec | Audio compression algorithm | AAC, MP3, FLAC, Opus |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Parcel"

Imagine you are sending a **parcel overseas**:

- The **parcel box** = the **Container** (`.mp4`, `.mov`)
- The **language the letter is written in** = the **Codec** (H.264, AAC)
- The **shrink-wrap machine that compressed the contents** = the **Compression Algorithm** (Lossy / Lossless)
- The **shipping label with sender/receiver info** = the **Metadata** (author, date, dimensions, bitrate)

Just as a postal system requires a standard way of packaging (boxes, labels, weights), a digital system requires a **standard file format** so any computer can "open the parcel" and read its contents correctly.

### 1.4 Two Major Classification Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — Compression Type:**
> - **Lossless Compression**: Original data is perfectly reconstructed. Used when fidelity is critical (medical imaging, archival, RAW photography). Examples: PNG, FLAC, BMP (uncompressed), TIFF.
> - **Lossy Compression**: Sacrifices some original data for much smaller file size. Used when minor quality loss is acceptable. Examples: JPEG, MP3, MP4, AAC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 2 — Raster vs Vector (for images):**
> - **Raster** = grid of pixels (BMP, JPEG, PNG). Resolution-dependent.
> - **Vector** = mathematical equations (SVG). Resolution-independent, infinitely scalable.
> - **Bitmap depth** is typically **8 bits per channel** (24-bit RGB) as the standard for color display.
> - Standard color depth for TrueColor images: **16,777,216 colors = 2^24**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Lossless vs Lossy Compression — Decision Logic

**When to use Lossless:**
- Medical imaging (X-ray, MRI, CT scans)
- Master archival copies
- Text/document storage
- Audio mastering (FLAC studio recordings)
- Line art, logos, screenshots with text

**When to use Lossy:**
- Streaming video/audio (YouTube, Netflix, Spotify)
- Web images (JPEG)
- Consumer-grade photography
- Voice calls (Opus codec)
- Anywhere bandwidth/storage is constrained

### 2.2 Popular File Formats — Comprehensive Classification

#### A. Image File Formats

| Format | Extension | Type | Compression | Best Use Case |
|--------|-----------|------|-------------|---------------|
| **BMP** (Bitmap) | `.bmp` | Raster | Uncompressed / RLE | Windows native, simple storage |
| **JPEG** | `.jpg / .jpeg` | Raster | Lossy (DCT-based) | Photos, web images |
| **JPEG 2000** | `.jp2` | Raster | Lossy/Lossless (Wavelet) | Medical imaging, cinema |
| **PNG** | `.png` | Raster | Lossless (DEFLATE) | Logos, transparency, web |
| **GIF** | `.gif` | Raster | Lossless (LZW) | Animations, simple graphics |
| **TIFF** | `.tif / .tiff` | Raster | Lossless / Lossy | Print publishing, scanning |
| **SVG** | `.svg` | Vector | Text-based XML | Logos, icons, scalable graphics |
| **WebP** | `.webp` | Raster | Lossy/Lossless | Modern web (Google) |
| **HEIC** | `.heic` | Raster | Lossy (HEVC) | iPhone photos |
| **EXR** | `.exr` | Raster (HDR) | Lossless/Lossy | VFX, film production |

#### B. Audio File Formats

| Format | Extension | Compression | Bitrate Range | Use Case |
|--------|-----------|-------------|---------------|----------|
| **WAV** | `.wav` | Uncompressed (PCM) | 1411 kbps (CD) | Studio master |
| **AIFF** | `.aiff` | Uncompressed (PCM) | 1411 kbps | Apple equivalent of WAV |
| **MP3** | `.mp3` | Lossy | 32–320 kbps | Music distribution |
| **AAC** | `.aac / .m4a` | Lossy | 32–256 kbps | iTunes, YouTube, streaming |
| **FLAC** | `.flac` | Lossless | ~50–70% of WAV | Audiophile archival |
| **OGG Vorbis** | `.ogg` | Lossy | 32–500 kbps | Open-source alternative to MP3 |
| **Opus** | `.opus` | Lossy | 6–510 kbps | VoIP, WebRTC, real-time |
| **WMA** | `.wma` | Lossy/Lossless | 64–768 kbps | Windows Media |

#### C. Video File Formats / Containers

| Container | Extension | Common Video Codecs | Common Audio Codecs | Use Case |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **AVI** | `.avi` | DivX, Xvid, H.264 | MP3, AC-3 | Legacy Windows |
| **MP4** | `.mp4` | H.264, H.265, MPEG-4 | AAC | Universal, streaming |
| **MKV** | `.mkv` | H.264, VP9, AV1 | AAC, FLAC, Opus | Open-source container |
| **MOV** | `.mov` | H.264, ProRes | AAC, PCM | Apple, professional video |
| **FLV** | `.flv` | VP6, H.264 | MP3, AAC | Flash video (legacy) |
| **WebM** | `.webm` | VP8, VP9, AV1 | Vorbis, Opus | HTML5 web video |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **routinely tested** in KTU 2024 ESE numerical questions.

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Application |
|---|-----------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Uncompressed Image Size** | $S = W \times H \times B$ (bytes) | Where W=width, H=height, B=bits per pixel |
| 2 | **Color Depth Resolution** | $\text{Colors} = 2^{b}$ | b = bits per pixel |
| 3 | **File Size in KB** | $S_{KB} = \dfrac{W \times H \times B}{8 \times 1024}$ | Convert bytes → KB |
| 4 | **File Size in MB** | $S_{MB} = \dfrac{W \times H \times B}{8 \times 1024^2}$ | Convert bytes → MB |
| 5 | **Compression Ratio** | $C_R = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Always $\geq 1$ |
| 6 | **Space Saving %** | $S_\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{C_R}\right) \times 100$ | Efficiency of compression |
| 7 | **Compressed Size** | $S_{comp} = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{C_R}$ | After applying compression ratio |
| 8 | **Bits per Pixel (BPP)** | $B = \dfrac{S \times 8}{W \times H}$ | Reverse calculation |
| 9 | **Uncompressed Audio Size** | $S = R \times B_{ps} \times C \times \dfrac{t}{8}$ (bytes) | R=sample rate (Hz), B_ps=bits/sample, C=channels, t=duration (s) |
| 10 | **Video Bitrate** | $\text{Bitrate} = W \times H \times fps \times \text{Motion Factor}$ | Approximate bandwidth |
| 11 | **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** | $PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{MAX^2}{MSE}\right)$ dB | Image quality metric |
| 12 | **MSE (Mean Squared Error)** | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{mn}\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\left[I(i,j) - K(i,j)\right]^2$ | Lossy compression fidelity |
| 13 | **CD Audio Size (74 min)** | $S = 44100 \times 16 \times 2 \times (74 \times 60) / 8$ | ~783 MB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values** (memorize for KTU):
> - CD Audio Sample Rate: **44,100 Hz** (44.1 kHz)
> - DVD Sample Rate: **48,000 Hz** (48 kHz)
> - CD Audio Bit Depth: **16 bits/sample, stereo (2 channels)**
> - 1 byte = **8 bits**, 1 KB = **1024 bytes**, 1 MB = **1024 KB**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Uncompressed Image File Size

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the storage size of an uncompressed RGB image with width W = 1920 pixels, height H = 1080 pixels, and color depth of 24 bits per pixel.

**Step 1 — Identify the data per pixel:**
For RGB TrueColor, each pixel is encoded with 24 bits:
$$\text{bits per pixel} = 8(\text{R}) + 8(\text{G}) + 8(\text{B}) = 24 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate total number of pixels:**
$$\text{Total Pixels} = W \times H = 1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total bits:**
$$\text{Total Bits} = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 = 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Convert bits to bytes:**
$$\text{Total Bytes} = \dfrac{49{,}766{,}400}{8} = 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Convert bytes to Megabytes (binary):**
$$S_{MB} = \dfrac{6{,}220{,}800}{1024^2} = \dfrac{6{,}220{,}800}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 5.93 \text{ MB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A 1080p Full HD uncompressed image ≈ **5.93 MB**. A 24-bit, 1920×1080 image is exactly **6,220,800 bytes** (without file header).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Compression Ratio & Space Saving

**Problem Setup:** Original BMP file size = 5.93 MB. After JPEG compression, the file becomes 850 KB. Calculate the compression ratio and percentage space saved.

**Step 1 — Normalize units to common base (KB):**
$$S_{orig} = 5.93 \text{ MB} = 5.93 \times 1024 = 6072.32 \text{ KB}$$
$$S_{comp} = 850 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply compression ratio formula:**
$$C_R = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}} = \dfrac{6072.32}{850} \approx 7.14$$

**Step 3 — Calculate space saving percentage:**
$$S_\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{C_R}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{7.14}\right) \times 100$$
$$S_\% = (1 - 0.140) \times 100 = 0.860 \times 100 = 86.0\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The JPEG compression achieved a **7.14:1 compression ratio**, saving **86% of storage space**. This is the typical range for "high-quality" JPEG.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Uncompressed Audio File Size (CD Quality)

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the size of a 5-minute stereo audio track at CD quality (44.1 kHz, 16 bits per sample).

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- Sample rate: $R = 44{,}100$ Hz
- Bit depth: $B_{ps} = 16$ bits/sample
- Channels: $C = 2$ (stereo)
- Duration: $t = 5 \times 60 = 300$ seconds

**Step 2 — Apply the uncompressed audio formula:**
$$S = R \times B_{ps} \times C \times \dfrac{t}{8} \text{ (bytes)}$$
$$S = 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2 \times \dfrac{300}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate intermediate values:**
$$S = 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2 \times 37.5$$
$$S = 1{,}411{,}200 \times 37.5 = 52{,}920{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MB:**
$$S_{MB} = \dfrac{52{,}920{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 50.47 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 5 — MP3 equivalent at 128 kbps (CBR):**
$$S_{MP3} = 128{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \times 300 \text{ s} = 38{,}400{,}000 \text{ bits} = 4.8 \text{ MB}$$
$$C_R = \dfrac{50.47}{4.8} \approx 10.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** 5 minutes of CD-quality audio = **~50 MB uncompressed** vs. **~4.8 MB MP3 @ 128 kbps** → a **~10.5:1 compression ratio** with imperceptible loss to most listeners.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio) for Lossy Quality

**Problem Setup:** Two 8-bit grayscale images of size 4×4 have an MSE of 12.5. Calculate PSNR.

**Step 1 — Identify MAX value (for 8-bit):**
$$MAX = 2^8 - 1 = 255$$

**Step 2 — Apply PSNR formula:**
$$PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{MAX^2}{MSE}\right)$$
$$PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{255^2}{12.5}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{65{,}025}{12.5}\right)$$
$$PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(5202) \approx 10 \times 3.716 = 37.16 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Interpretation (KTU-expected answer):**
> - **PSNR > 40 dB** → Excellent quality, indistinguishable from original
