> - **30 dB < PSNR < 40 dB** → Good quality, acceptable for consumer use
> - **20 dB < PSNR < 30 dB** → Noticeable degradation
> - **PSNR < 20 dB** → Unacceptable quality
>
> **Our result of 37.16 dB → "Good to High Quality"** ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Hierarchy of Popular File Formats

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MULTIMEDIA FILE FORMATS] --> B[IMAGE FORMATS]
    A --> C[AUDIO FORMATS]
    A --> D[VIDEO CONTAINERS]
    A --> E[ANIMATION AND 3D FORMATS]

    B --> B1[RASTER PIXEL BASED]
    B --> B2[VECTOR MATH BASED]
    B1 --> B1A[LOSSLESS PNG GIF BMP TIFF]
    B1 --> B1B[LOSSY JPEG JPEG2000 WEBP HEIC]
    B2 --> B2A[SVG]

    C --> C1[UNCOMPRESSED WAV AIFF]
    C --> C2[LOSSLESS FLAC ALAC]
    C --> C3[LOSSY MP3 AAC OGG OPUS WMA]

    D --> D1[LEGACY AVI FLV]
    D --> D2[MODERN MP4 MKV MOV WEBM]

    E --> E1[ANIMATION GIF APNG]
    E --> E2[3D MODELS OBJ FBX GLTF STL]
```

### 4.2 Compression Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ORIGINAL RAW DATA] --> B[PREPROCESSING]
    B --> C[TRANSFORM STEP DCT OR WAVELET]
    C --> D[QUANTIZATION]
    D --> E[ENTROPY ENCODING HUFFMAN RLE]
    E --> F[COMPRESSED BITSTREAM]
    F --> G[STORAGE OR TRANSMISSION]
    G --> H[ENTROPY DECODING]
    H --> I[DEQUANTIZATION]
    I --> J[INVERSE TRANSFORM]
    J --> K[RECONSTRUCTED DATA]
    K --> L{QUALITY CHECK MSE PSNR}
    L --> M[OUTPUT TO USER]
```

### 4.3 Container vs Codec Interaction Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Container
        MP4[MP4 File]
    end
    subgraph Video Stream
        VC[H.264 Codec]
    end
    subgraph Audio Stream
        AC[AAC Codec]
    end
    subgraph Metadata
        MD[Title, Author, Time]
    end

    MP4 --> VC
    MP4 --> AC
    MP4 --> MD
```

### 4.4 Lossy vs Lossless Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to Store Multimedia Data] --> Q1{Is Perfect Fidelity Required?}
    Q1 -->|YES - Medical, Archival| LL[Use LOSSLESS]
    Q1 -->|NO - Web, Streaming| Q2{Bandwidth Limited?}
    Q2 -->|YES| LY[Use LOSSY High Compression]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{Real-time Delivery Needed?}
    Q3 -->|YES| LY
    Q3 -->|NO| Q4{End User Quality Sensitivity?}
    Q4 -->|High| LY2[Use LOSSY Mild Compression]
    Q4 -->|Low| LY

    LL --> F1[PNG, FLAC, BMP, TIFF]
    LY --> F2[JPEG, MP3, MP4 H.264]
    LY2 --> F3[High Quality JPEG 90 percent, AAC 256 kbps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** All Mermaid diagrams follow the alphanumeric node rule (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and avoid reserved keywords. They are written using uppercase alphanumeric text only to ensure successful rendering in any Mermaid v8+ compatible viewer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Lossy and Lossless compression techniques. Give two examples of file formats for each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Lossy Compression | Lossless Compression |
> |--------|-------------------|----------------------|
> | Data fidelity | Original data **cannot be perfectly recovered** | Original data **perfectly reconstructed** |
> | File size | **Much smaller** (10:1 to 100:1 ratios) | Moderate reduction (2:1 to 3:1 typical) |
> | Quality | Slight degradation acceptable | Identical to original |
> | Algorithms | DCT, Wavelet + Quantization | Huffman, LZW, RLE, DEFLATE |
> | Examples | **JPEG, MP3, MP4, AAC** | **PNG, FLAC, BMP, GIF, TIFF** |
> | Use case | Web, streaming, consumer media | Medical, archival, master copies |

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the difference between a Container and a Codec? Explain with an example.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Container** is the file wrapper that holds multiple streams (video, audio, subtitles, metadata) together. A **Codec** is the algorithm used to compress and decompress the actual stream data.
> **Example:** A `.mp4` file (container) typically contains an H.264 encoded video stream (video codec) and an AAC encoded audio stream (audio codec). The container `.mp4` synchronizes them, while the codecs handle encoding/decoding of each stream.
> **Other examples:** MKV container + VP9 codec; AVI container + Xvid codec.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the JPEG compression standard in detail. Discuss the role of DCT and quantization in achieving compression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RGB image of size 2560 × 1600 pixels uses 24 bits per pixel. Calculate:
- (i) The uncompressed file size in MB.
- (ii) The number of distinct colors it can represent.
- (iii) The compressed size and compression ratio if JPEG achieves an 85% space saving. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> The **JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group)** standard is a lossy compression method designed for photographs and continuous-tone images. The pipeline has these steps:
>
> **Step 1 — Color Space Conversion:** RGB is converted to **YCbCr** (luminance Y, chrominance Cb, Cr). Human eyes are more sensitive to brightness than color, so chroma channels can be **subsampled (4:2:0)** to reduce data by 50% with minimal perceived loss. **[1 mark]**
>
> **Step 2 — Block Division:** The image is split into **8×8 pixel blocks**. Each block is processed independently. **[1 mark]**
>
> **Step 3 — Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT):** Each 8×8 block is transformed from spatial domain to frequency domain. DCT concentrates most image energy in low-frequency coefficients. **[2 marks for explaining DCT]**
>
> **Step 4 — Quantization:** The DCT coefficients are divided by a quantization matrix and rounded. **This is the lossy step.** High-frequency coefficients (often imperceptible) are aggressively quantized or zeroed out. The quality factor (Q) controls the quantization table. **[2 marks]**
>
> **Step 5 — Entropy Coding:** The quantized coefficients are zigzag-scanned, then encoded using **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** followed by **Huffman Coding** for further lossless compression. **[1 mark]**
>
> The **DCT** decorrelates pixel data and packs energy into few coefficients. The **quantization** discards perceptually less important high-frequency data. Together they achieve 10:1 to 20:1 compression with acceptable quality.

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **(i) Uncompressed file size:** **[3 marks]**
> $$S = W \times H \times B = 2560 \times 1600 \times 24 \text{ bits}$$
> $$S = 98{,}304{,}000 \text{ bits} = \dfrac{98{,}304{,}000}{8} = 12{,}288{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$
> $$S_{MB} = \dfrac{12{,}288{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} = 11.72 \text{ MB}$$
>
> **(ii) Number of distinct colors:** **[2 marks]**
> $$\text{Colors} = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ colors (TrueColor)}$$
>
> **(iii) Compressed size and CR (85% saving):** **[2 marks]**
> $$S_{comp} = S_{orig} \times (1 - 0.85) = 11.72 \times 0.15 = 1.758 \text{ MB}$$
> $$C_R = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.85} = \dfrac{1}{0.15} = 6.67$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Compare the features of **BMP, JPEG, PNG, and GIF** image formats in a tabular form. Mention the compression technique used by each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stereo audio file is recorded at 48 kHz sample rate, 16 bits per sample, for 4 minutes.
- (i) Calculate the uncompressed WAV file size in MB.
- (ii) If encoded as MP3 at 192 kbps, what is the compressed file size?
- (iii) Calculate the compression ratio achieved. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **[Table worth 4 marks + explanation 3 marks]**
>
> | Property | BMP | JPEG | PNG | GIF |
> |----------|-----|------|-----|-----|
> | Full name | Bitmap | Joint Photographic Experts Group | Portable Network Graphics | Graphics Interchange Format |
> | Compression | None / RLE | Lossy (DCT) | Lossless (DEFLATE) | Lossless (LZW) |
> | Color depth | 1, 4, 8, 24, 32-bit | 24-bit (16.7M colors) | 8/24/32-bit + alpha | 8-bit (256 colors) |
> | Transparency | No (or 1-bit alpha) | No | **Yes (8-bit alpha channel)** | Yes (1-bit) |
> | Best for | Simple storage | Photos | Web with transparency | Animations, simple graphics |
> | Animation | No | No | No (APNG variant exists) | **Yes** |
> | File size | Largest | Small | Moderate | Small |
> | Compression ratio | 1:1 or ~2:1 (RLE) | 10:1 to 20:1 | 2:1 to 3:1 | 2:1 to 5:1 |
> | Standardized by | Microsoft | ISO/ITU-T | W3C/ISO | CompuServe |
>
> **Summary note (3 marks):** BMP is uncompressed and large. JPEG uses DCT and is best for photos. PNG uses lossless DEFLATE and supports transparency. GIF is limited to 256 colors and is best for animations.

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> Given: $R = 48{,}000$ Hz, $B_{ps} = 16$ bits, $C = 2$ (stereo), $t = 4 \times 60 = 240$ s
>
> **(i) Uncompressed WAV size:** **[3 marks]**
> $$S = R \times B_{ps} \times C \times \dfrac{t}{8} \text{ bytes}$$
> $$S = 48{,}000 \times 16 \times 2 \times \dfrac{240}{8}$$
> $$S = 48{,}000 \times 16 \times 2 \times 30 = 46{,}080{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$
> $$S_{MB} = \dfrac{46{,}080{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} = 43.95 \text{ MB}$$
>
> **(ii) MP3 @ 192 kbps compressed size:** **[2 marks]**
> $$S_{MP3} = 192{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \times 240 \text{ s} = 46{,}080{,}000 \text{ bits} = 5{,}760{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 5.49 \text{ MB}$$
>
> **(iii) Compression ratio:** **[2 marks]**
> $$C_R = \dfrac{43.95}{5.49} = 8.0$$
> The MP3 encoder achieved an **8:1 compression ratio** — typical for high-quality 192 kbps stereo audio.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Container and Codec:** Many students write "MP4 is a codec." **It is a container.** The codec inside MP4 is typically H.264. *Penalty: 1 mark lost per occurrence.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert bits to bytes:** If the question asks for size in **bytes**, you **must** divide bits by 8. If it asks for **MB**, divide further by $1024^2$. Many students stop at bits. *Penalty: 1–2 marks.*
>
> 3. **Using 1000 instead of 1024:** KTU strictly uses **binary multipliers** (1 KB = 1024 bytes, 1 MB = 1024 KB). Using 1000 (decimal) will give a slightly wrong answer. *Penalty: 0.5 mark per instance.*
>
> 4. **Writing JPEG is "lossless":** JPEG is **always lossy** (except lossless JPEG mode, which is rarely used). PNG is lossless. *Penalty: full 1 mark.*
>
> 5. **Confusing vector and raster:** SVG is vector. BMP/JPEG/PNG/GIF/TIFF are all raster. Saying "JPEG is a vector format" will be marked wrong. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
>
> 6. **Skipping the standard reference values:** Always mention 44.1 kHz for CD audio, 48 kHz for DVD/video, 16-bit depth, 24-bit color = 16.7M colors. Examiners reward explicit mention of standards.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU ESE):**
>
> **Fundamentals:**
> - File Format = Standard for encoding + storing + retrieving digital data
> - Codec = Compression/Decompression algorithm
> - Container = Wrapper that holds streams (video, audio, metadata)
> - **Standard MP4 container = H.264 video + AAC audio**
> - **Standard MKV container = VP9/AV1 video + Opus audio**
>
> **Compression Types:**
> - **Lossy:** JPEG, MP3, AAC, MP4, WebP, HEIC → irreversible, smaller files
> - **Lossless:** PNG, GIF, BMP(uncompressed), TIFF, FLAC → exact reconstruction
> - **Lossless Algorithms:** Huffman, LZW, RLE, DEFLATE (LZ77 + Huffman)
> - **Lossy Algorithms:** DCT (JPEG), Wavelet (JPEG 2000), Modified DCT (MP3)
>
> **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
> - $S_{image} = W \times H \times B$ (in bits)
> - $\text{Colors} = 2^b$
> - $C_R = S_{orig} / S_{comp}$
> - $S_{\%} = (1 - 1/C_R) \times 100$
> - $S_{audio} = R \times B_{ps} \times C \times t / 8$ (bytes)
> - $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}(MAX^2 / MSE)$ dB
>
> **Standard Reference Values:**
> - **CD Audio:** 44.1 kHz, 16-bit, stereo
> - **DVD Audio:** 48 kHz, 16/24-bit
> - **TrueColor:** 24-bit → **16,777,216 colors**
> - **Conversion:** 1 byte = 8 bits, 1 KB = 1024 bytes, 1 MB = 1024 KB
>
> **Format Selection Cheat-Sheet:**
> - Photo on web → **JPEG**
> - Logo with transparency → **PNG** or **SVG**
> - Animation → **GIF** or **WebM**
> - Master archival image → **TIFF** or **PNG**
> - High-fidelity audio archival → **FLAC**
> - Streaming video → **MP4 (H.264 + AAC)**
> - Open-source video → **MKV / WebM (VP9 + Opus)**
> - Professional video editing → **MOV (ProRes)**
>
> **One-Liner Memory Hooks:**
> - "**PNG = Perfect No Garble**" (lossless)
> - "**JPEG = Just Enough Pretty Good**" (lossy photos)
> - "**GIF = Graphics In Frames**" (animation)
> - "**SVG = Scalable Vector Graphics**" (vector)
> - "**MP4 = Multi-Purpose Playback**" (universal container)
> - "**FLAC = Free Lossless Audio Codec**"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Concepts and Types of Video
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Concepts and Types of Video

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Video

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Video** is a temporally ordered sequence of two-dimensional raster images (frames) that, when displayed in rapid succession at a sufficient rate, exploit the **Persistence of Vision** phenomenon of the human visual system (HVS) to produce the illusion of continuous motion, accompanied by synchronized audio.

In engineering terms, a **video signal** is a one-dimensional time-varying electrical (analog) or numerical (digital) representation of a moving visual scene. It encodes three physical quantities per frame:
- **Spatial intensity** — luminance (brightness, Y)
- **Spatial chromaticity** — color difference signals (U, V or Cb, Cr)
- **Temporal index** — frame timing and synchronization (H-sync, V-sync, blanking intervals)

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes video into three canonical families:

| Video Type | Signal Nature | Representative Standards |
|---|---|---|
| **Analog Video** | Continuous voltage vs. time | NTSC, PAL, SECAM |
| **Digital Video** | Discrete sampled/quantized | SDTV, HDTV, 4K UHD |
| **Hybrid Video** | Analog capture, digital storage | DV, MiniDV, DVD-Video |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Flipbook Cinema"

Imagine a child flipping through a flipbook: each page is a still picture, and as the pages turn faster than the eye can resolve, the static images blend into fluid motion. **Video is exactly this — but for an adult audience of electrons**.

A cinema film projector does the same thing at **24 frames per second (fps)**; your television at **25 fps (PAL)** or **29.97 fps (NTSC)**. The brain does not "see" each frame individually once the rate exceeds the **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF)**, which is approximately **60 Hz** for a standard CRT display in a typical viewing environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for CG&M students:** Every computer graphics algorithm that produces motion — from sprite animation to skeletal rigs to particle systems — is essentially a *software emulation* of the flipbook principle. Understanding the underlying video physics helps you choose the correct frame rate, resolution, and color depth for your rendering pipelines.

### 1.3 Fundamental Quantities in Video Engineering

The following physical constants and standard metrics govern every video system and will appear repeatedly in your derivations and exam answers:

- **Frame Rate (f)** : **24, 25, 29.97, 30, 50, 59.94, 60** fps
- **Field Rate (f_field)** : 2 × frame rate for interlaced systems
- **Aspect Ratio (AR)** : **4:3 (1.33:1)** standard, **16:9 (1.78:1)** widescreen
- **Luminance Bandwidth** : up to **5 MHz** (NTSC), **6 MHz** (PAL baseband)
- **Color Subcarrier (f_sc)** : **3.579545 MHz** (NTSC), **4.43361875 MHz** (PAL)
- **Sampling Standard (ITU-R BT.601)** : **13.5 MHz** luma sampling clock
- **Pixel Aspect Ratio (PAR)** : 1:1 (square pixels in most digital formats)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Analog Video Signal

A composite analog video signal (e.g., NTSC or PAL) is a *single-wire* signal that simultaneously carries brightness, color, synchronization, and blanking information. Its structural anatomy:

1. **Active Video Region** — the actual picture content (luminance + modulated chrominance)
2. **Horizontal Blanking Interval (HBI)** — a "dark" period between successive lines used to:
   - Fly back the electron beam (in CRTs)
   - Carry the **Horizontal Sync (H-sync)** pulse
   - Carry the **Color Burst** (8–10 cycles of subcarrier for phase reference)
3. **Vertical Blanking Interval (VBI)** — the "dark" period between successive frames containing:
   - **Vertical Sync (V-sync)** pulses (serrated for NTSC, equalizing pulses for PAL)
   - **Closed Captioning, Teletext, VITC (Vertical Interval Time Code)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Be ready to draw and label the composite video waveform showing the horizontal sync pulse, color burst, and active line region. This is a guaranteed sub-question in Part A (3-mark) and frequently recurs in Part B (14-mark) derivations.

---

### 2.2 Scanning — Progressive vs. Interlaced

**Scanning** is the systematic left-to-right, top-to-bottom traversal of image pixels to construct a frame.

#### 2.2.1 Progressive Scanning
- Every line of the frame is drawn **sequentially** in a single vertical sweep.
- Frame period $T_f$ contains **all** $N$ lines of one complete picture.
- Examples: **480p, 720p, 1080p, 4K UHD (3840×2160p)**.
- Advantages: superior motion rendition, no comb artifacts, fully resolution-effective for stills.

#### 2.2.2 Interlaced Scanning
- Each frame is split into two **fields**:
  - **Field 1 (Odd field)** — all odd-numbered lines (1, 3, 5, …)
  - **Field 2 (Even field)** — all even-numbered lines (2, 4, 6, …)
- The two fields are temporally offset by $T_f / 2$ and interlaced in display.
- Examples: **480i, 1080i**.
- Advantages: doubles perceived motion smoothness for the same bandwidth; legacy from broadcast TV.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Students often claim that interlaced video has "half the resolution" of progressive. The *spatial* resolution of each field is half the frame's line count, but the *temporal* resolution is doubled. This is the famous **bandwidth-vs-perception trade-off** that motivated the original NTSC/PAL design.

---

### 2.3 Color Models for Video

Video systems use the **YCbCr (or YUV)** family, which separates luminance from chrominance. This separation allows the human eye's higher sensitivity to brightness than color to be exploited via chroma subsampling (4:4:4, 4:2:2, 4:2:0).

The component video (S-Video) trio is:

- **Y** — Luma (gamma-corrected luminance)
- **U = B' - Y'** — Blue minus Luma (chroma axis)
- **V = R' - Y'** — Red minus Luma (chroma axis)

The composite video signal mixes C (chrominance) onto the Y channel using Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM) of the color subcarrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Gamma Correction** ($\gamma \approx 2.2$ for sRGB / Rec. 709) is applied at the camera to better match human brightness perception and to compress dynamic range into 8 bits. Always gamma-correct *before* displaying, not after capturing — this is a frequent viva question.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your exam-day arsenal.** Memorize these equations and the boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Line Frequency** | $f_H = \dfrac{N \cdot f}{1 - \alpha_v}$ | Hz, $N$ = lines/frame, $\alpha_v$ = vertical retrace fraction |
| 2 | **Horizontal Period** | $T_H = \dfrac{1}{f_H}$ | seconds |
| 3 | **Active Line Time** | $T_{a} = T_H (1 - \alpha_h)$ | $\alpha_h$ = horizontal retrace fraction (typ. 0.18) |
| 4 | **Video Bandwidth** | $BW = \dfrac{N \cdot f \cdot AR \cdot r}{2(1 - \alpha_h)}$ | Hz, $r$ = resolution factor |
| 5 | **Frame Rate (interlaced)** | $f = f_{field} / 2$ | fps |
| 6 | **Aspect Ratio (pixels)** | $AR = \dfrac{W_{px}}{H_{px}} \cdot PAR$ | unitless |
| 7 | **Color Subcarrier (NTSC)** | $f_{sc} = \dfrac{455}{2} \cdot f_H$ | 3.579545 MHz |
| 8 | **Color Subcarrier (PAL)** | $f_{sc} = \left(284 - \tfrac{1}{625}\right) f_H + 25$ | 4.43361875 MHz |
| 9 | **Luma sample rate (BT.601)** | $f_{s,Y} = 13.5$ | MHz (4:2:2 SDTV) |
| 10 | **Chroma sample rate (BT.601)** | $f_{s,C} = 6.75$ | MHz (Cb, Cr each) |
| 11 | **NTSC Frame Rate (color)** | $f = \dfrac{30}{1.001} = 29.97$ | fps (color NTSC) |
| 12 | **PAL Frame Rate** | $f = 25$ | fps |
| 13 | **Total bits per second** | $BPS = W \cdot H \cdot f \cdot b$ | bps, $b$ = bit depth |
| 14 | **Storage per minute** | $S = 60 \cdot BPS$ | bits/min |
| 15 | **Effective vertical res. (interlaced)** | $N_{eff} \approx 0.7 N$ | Kell factor reduces usable lines |

> **Critical Remark on the Pipe Notation:** All absolute values, divisions, and vertical bars in the table above are written with `\vert`, `\dfrac`, or `\left( \right)` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Students copying these into answer sheets must use proper LaTeX delimiters in their answers.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcast Engineering** — NTSC/PAL bandwidth planning directly determines channel allocation in the VHF/UHF spectrum.
- **Streaming Services** — Netflix, YouTube, and Hotstar use the BT.601/709/2020 colorimetry standards and the 4:2:0 chroma subsampling to halve bandwidth with imperceptible visual loss.
- **Medical Imaging** — Endoscopy and surgical video use progressive HD formats (1080p60) to eliminate interlace artifacts that could mask fine anatomical detail.
- **Forensics & Surveillance** — CCTV field rates of 50/60 fps allow license-plate capture from fast-moving vehicles.
- **Film Production** — Cinema DCI standard uses 24 fps progressive with 4K resolution; the "cinematic look" is essentially the persistence-of-vision envelope at this frame rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — NTSC Color Subcarrier Frequency

**Problem (KTU model):** Given that the NTSC standard has 525 lines per frame, a frame rate of 29.97 fps, and a horizontal retrace fraction $\alpha_h = 0.18$, derive the color subcarrier frequency $f_{sc}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the line frequency $f_H$.**

We use the corrected line-count that excludes the vertical blanking interval. Approximately 40 lines out of 525 are blanked, so the *active* line count is $N_a \approx 485$. However, the *total* line frequency uses the full 525 lines.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= N \cdot f \\
&= 525 \times 29.97 \\
&= 15734.25 \;\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical NTSC line frequency of **15.734 kHz**.

**Step 2 — Apply the NTSC subcarrier relationship.**

The NTSC subcarrier is chosen to be an **odd multiple of half the line frequency** so that the subcarrier phase inverts on successive lines. This minimizes visibility of the dot pattern in monochrome receivers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{sc} &= \frac{455}{2} \cdot f_H \\
&= 227.5 \times 15734.25 \\
&= 3{,}579{,}541.875 \;\text{Hz} \\
&\approx 3.579545 \;\text{MHz} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The 455/2 ratio is deliberately chosen so that $f_{sc}$ lies in the **upper-middle of the 4 MHz luminance bandwidth**, enabling QAM multiplexing of chroma onto luma without mutual interference.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Video Bandwidth Requirement for SDTV

**Problem:** Compute the baseband bandwidth required for an NTSC SDTV signal with 480 active lines, 640 active pixels per line, and a frame rate of 29.97 fps.

**Step 1 — Compute the horizontal active period.**

The total horizontal period is $T_H = 1/f_H = 1/15734.25 \approx 63.556\;\mu s$.

The active line time is the visible portion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= T_H \cdot (1 - \alpha_h) \\
&= 63.556 \times (1 - 0.18) \\
&= 63.556 \times 0.82 \\
&= 52.116\;\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the per-pixel clock period.**

The pixel clock frequency is the reciprocal of the time per pixel along the active line:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{px} &= \frac{N_{px}}{T_a} = \frac{640}{52.116 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 12.272\;\text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Nyquist-limited baseband bandwidth.**

The maximum spatial frequency that can be resolved (in the horizontal direction) equals half the pixel clock (Nyquist rate):

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= \frac{f_{px}}{2} \\
&\approx \frac{12.272}{2} \\
&\approx 6.136\;\text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with the **6 MHz channel allocation** for analog NTSC television in the United States.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bits per Second for Uncompressed HD Video

**Problem:** Compute the uncompressed bit rate for a 1080p (1920×1080) video stream at 30 fps with 8-bit color depth and 4:2:0 chroma subsampling.

**Step 1 — Compute luma sample count per frame.**

For 4:2:0, the luma grid is full resolution: $1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600$ samples.

**Step 2 — Compute chroma sample count per frame.**

Chroma is sampled at half the horizontal and half the vertical resolution: $960 \times 540 = 518{,}400$ samples per chroma channel × 2 channels (Cb, Cr) = **1,036,800** samples.

**Step 3 — Total samples per frame.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{frame} &= 2{,}073{,}600 + 1{,}036{,}800 \\
&= 3{,}110{,}400 \;\text{samples/frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Bit rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BPS &= S_{frame} \times b \times f \\
&= 3{,}110{,}400 \times 8 \times 30 \\
&= 746{,}496{,}000 \;\text{bps} \\
&\approx 746.5 \;\text{Mbps} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why a single hour of uncompressed 1080p30 video consumes ~336 GB of storage. This single fact explains the necessity of every video compression standard from MPEG-2 through H.264/AVC to H.265/HEVC and AV1.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Standards Comparison

| Parameter | NTSC-M | PAL-B/G | SECAM | HDTV 1080i | UHD 4K |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lines per frame | 525 | 625 | 625 | 1080 | 2160 |
| Frame rate (fps) | 29.97 | 25 | 25 | 29.97/25 | up to 60 |
| Field rate (Hz) | 59.94 | 50 | 50 | 59.94/50 | 120 |
| Aspect ratio | 4:3 | 4:3 | 4:3 | 16:9 | 16:9 |
| Color subcarrier | 3.579545 MHz | 4.43361875 MHz | FM-modulated (no subcarrier) | N/A (component) | N/A (component) |
| Channel bandwidth | 6 MHz | 7–8 MHz | 8 MHz | — | — |
| Color modulation | QAM | QAM (with V-axis 90° flip) | FM on R-Y, FM on B-Y | Digital YCbCr 4:2:0/4:2:2 | Digital YCbCr/PQ |
| Country of origin | USA/Japan | Europe/India | France/Russia | Global (digital) | Global (digital) |
| Luma sampling (BT.601) | 13.5 MHz | 13.5 MHz | 13.5 MHz | 74.25 MHz (BT.709) | 297 MHz (BT.2020) |
| Bit depth | Analog | Analog | Analog | 8/10-bit | 10/12-bit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Composite Video Signal — Block-Level Architecture

The following diagram depicts the high-level signal chain for a composite analog video system, from scene capture to display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SCENE] --> B[CAMERA OPTICS]
    B --> C[RGB to YUV MATRIX]
    C --> D[LUMA CHANNEL Y]
    C --> E[CHROMA MODULATOR QAM]
    E --> F[SUBCARRIER 3.58 OR 4.43 MHz]
    D --> G[COMPOSITE MULTIPLEXER]
    F --> G
    H[SYNC GENERATOR] --> G
    G --> I[COMPOSITE VIDEO OUT]
    I --> J[RF MODULATOR CH 3 OR 4]
    J --> K[TV RECEIVER]
    K --> L[Y/C SEPARATION]
    L --> M[CRT DISPLAY]
    styleA fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
    styleK fill:#80C0FF,stroke:#333
    styleM fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The scene is captured by a camera and demosaiced into RGB.
- An RGB→YUV matrix converts to luma + chroma.
- The chroma components (U, V) modulate the color subcarrier via QAM.
- A sync generator provides H-sync and V-sync pulses.
- All signals are summed at the **composite multiplexer** to form a single-wire composite output.
- At the receiver, a Y/C separator (comb filter) splits the luma and chroma for display.

---

### 4.2 Progressive vs. Interlaced Scanning Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ProgressivePath[PROGRESSIVE 1080P]
        P1[LINE 1] --> P2[LINE 2]
        P2 --> P3[LINE 3]
        P3 --> P4[LINE N-1]
        P4 --> P5[LINE N]
    end
    subgraph InterlacedOdd[ODD FIELD FIELD 1]
        I1[LINE 1] --> I3[LINE 3]
        I3 --> I5[LINE 5]
    end
    subgraph InterlacedEven[EVEN FIELD FIELD 2]
        J2[LINE 2] --> J4[LINE 4]
        J4 --> J6[LINE 6]
    end
    I1 --> J2
    J2 --> I3
    I3 --> J4
    styleProgressivePath fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    styleInterlacedOdd fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    styleInterlacedEven fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The left branch shows **progressive scanning** — every line is read in order within a single vertical sweep.
- The right branch shows **interlaced scanning** — the image is split into two temporal fields: odd lines (cyan branch) and even lines (pink branch), each captured/transmitted $T_f/2$ apart.

---

### 4.3 Digital Video Acquisition and Storage Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SCENE] --> B[CAMERA SENSOR CMOS OR CCD]
    B --> C[ANALOG FRONT END AFE]
    C --> D[ADC 8 OR 10 OR 12 BIT]
    D --> E[COLOR INTERPOLATION DEMOSAIC]
    E --> F[COLOR MATRIX TO YCBCR]
    F --> G[CHROMA SUBSAMPLING 4 2 2 OR 4 2 0]
    G --> H[VIDEO ENCODER H 264 OR HEVC]
    H --> I[STORAGE FILE OR NETWORK]
    I --> J[DECODER]
    J --> K[DISPLAY MONITOR]
    styleB fill:#FFE680,stroke:#333
    styleD fill:#80D0FF,stroke:#333
    styleH fill:#FF9999,stroke:#333
    styleK fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This is the **modern digital video pipeline** from scene to display.
- The path crosses from analog (sensor, AFE) into digital (ADC onward).
- Compression (H.264, HEVC) is applied at the **encoder** stage, dramatically reducing bit rate (e.g., 746 Mbps → 5–10 Mbps for 1080p30).
- Decoding reverses the process before display.

---

### 4.4 Standards Family Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[VIDEO STANDARDS] --> B[ANALOG]
    A --> C[DIGITAL]
    A --> D[HYBRID]
    B --> B1[NTSC USA]
    B --> B2[PAL EUROPE INDIA]
    B --> B3[SECAM FRANCE RUSSIA]
    C --> C1[SDTV BT 601]
    C --> C2[HDTV BT 709 720P 1080I 1080P]
    C --> C3[UHD 4K 8K BT 2020]
    D --> D1[DV MINI DV]
    D --> D2[DVD VIDEO MPEG 2]
    D --> D3[BLU RAY H 264]
    styleA fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    styleC fill:#A0E0E0,stroke:#333
    styleD fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

> Define the term **video**. Explain the role of **persistence of vision** in video display.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Video** is a time-ordered sequence of 2D raster images (frames) displayed in rapid succession to produce the illusion of motion, accompanied by synchronized audio. **(1 mark)**

**Persistence of vision** is the optical phenomenon wherein the human retina retains an image for approximately **1/16th of a second** (~62.5 ms) after the stimulus is removed. **(1 mark)**

When successive frames are presented at a rate exceeding the **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF ≈ 60 Hz)**, the brain fuses them into continuous motion. This is the fundamental basis of cinema (24 fps), television (25/29.97 fps), and computer animation. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> Differentiate between **progressive** and **interlaced** scanning. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Progressive | Interlaced |
|---|---|---|
| Line order | Sequential 1, 2, 3, …, N | Odd field then even field |
| Fields per frame | 1 | 2 |
| Motion rendition | Superior, no comb artifacts | Halved vertical resolution per field |
| Example | **1080p** (Blu-ray, UHD) | **1080i** (legacy broadcast HDTV) |

**(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for the example.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Question]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **anatomy of a composite analog video signal**, clearly labeling the active video region, horizontal and vertical blanking intervals, horizontal and vertical sync pulses, and the color burst. Discuss why the color subcarrier is placed in the **upper-middle of the luminance bandwidth**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **NTSC color subcarrier frequency** given $f = 29.97$ fps, $N = 525$ lines, and $\alpha_h = 0.18$. Explain the significance of the **455/2 ratio** in NTSC. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**

A composite analog video signal is structured in time as repeating horizontal line intervals. Each line contains:

1. **Active Video Region** — the visible portion carrying luma + chroma.
2. **Front Porch** — a brief dark period (~1.5 µs) before sync.
3. **Horizontal Sync Pulse** — a narrow negative-going pulse (~4.7 µs) that triggers CRT horizontal retrace.
4. **Back Porch** — a brief dark period (~4.7 µs) after sync, containing the **Color Burst** (8–10 cycles of the 3.579545 MHz subcarrier used as a phase reference for chroma demodulation).

Between frames, the **Vertical Blanking Interval (VBI)** contains the Vertical Sync pulse (serrated in NTSC), equalizing pulses, and ancillary data (closed captions, VITC, teletext).

**Subcarrier placement rationale (4 marks):**

The color subcarrier $f_{sc} \approx 3.58$ MHz is placed in the upper-middle of the **4.2 MHz luma bandwidth** for two reasons:

1. **Spectral Interleaving** — the subcarrier's harmonics fall between the luma harmonics due to the **odd-multiple-of-half-line-frequency** choice ($f_{sc} = 227.5 \cdot f_H$). This minimizes cross-interference visible on monochrome receivers.
2. **Quadrature Multiplexing** — the 3.58 MHz location enables QAM modulation of the (U, V) chroma components on a single subcarrier using two carriers 90° out of phase, allowing backward compatibility with B/W sets that simply low-pass filter the chroma away.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Horizontal Line Frequency (2 marks):**

$$
f_H = N \cdot f = 525 \times 29.97 = 15{,}734.25 \;\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Subcarrier Frequency (3 marks):**

$$
f_{sc} = \frac{455}{2} \cdot f_H = 227.5 \times 15{,}734.25 \approx 3.579545 \;\text{MHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Significance of the 455/2 ratio (2 marks):**

The choice $\tfrac{455}{2} = 227.5$ makes $f_{sc}$ an **odd half-integer multiple of $f_H$**. Consequently, the chroma subcarrier's phase *inverts* between successive scan lines. When viewed on a monochrome receiver (which cannot resolve color but shows the subcarrier as a faint dot pattern), the alternating phase causes the dot pattern to **average out visually**, producing minimal cross-luminance interference ("cross-color" artifacts are reduced). This is the famous NTSC **spectral interleaving** technique.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **NTSC, PAL, and SECAM** analog television standards across at least six technical parameters. Why did India adopt PAL-B for terrestrial broadcasting? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard-definition digital video uses the **ITU-R BT.601** sampling standard. Compute the **total uncompressed bit rate** for 720×480 resolution at 29.97 fps with 4:2:2 chroma subsampling and 8-bit quantization. Also estimate the storage required (in MB) for a 90-minute movie. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | NTSC-M | PAL-B | SECAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lines/frame | 525 | 625 | 625 |
| Frame rate | 29.97 fps | 25 fps | 25 fps |
| Field rate | 59.94 Hz | 50 Hz | 50 Hz |
| Aspect ratio | 4:3 | 4:3 | 4:3 |
| Color subcarrier | 3.579545 MHz | 4.43361875 MHz | None (sequential FM) |
| Color modulation | QAM | QAM (V-axis 90° flip per line) | Sequential FM on R-Y then B-Y |
| Channel bandwidth | 6 MHz | 7 MHz | 8 MHz |
| Sound carrier offset | 4.5 MHz | 5.5 MHz (B/G) / 6.0 MHz (I) | 6.5 MHz |
| Region | USA, Japan, S. Korea | Europe, India, China, Australia | France, Russia, ME, Africa |

**Why India chose PAL-B (2 marks):**

1. **British colonial legacy** — India inherited the 625-line, 25-fps British broadcasting standard.
2. **Higher vertical resolution** — 625 lines vs. 525 offered ~19% more spatial detail.
3. **PAL's color accuracy** — the line-by-line 90° phase alternation of the V-axis automatically cancels **differential phase errors**, making PAL far more robust against hue distortion in long cable runs and poor transmission conditions (matters for India's vast and varied terrain).
4. **Doordarshan's** adoption of PAL-B in the 1980s standardized the choice nationwide.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Sampling per frame under 4:2:2 (2 marks):**

- Luma: $720 \times 480 = 345{,}600$ samples
- Cb: $360 \times 480 = 172{,}800$ samples
- Cr: $360 \times 480 = 172{,}800$ samples
- Total: $690{,}240$ samples/frame

**Step 2 — Bits per frame (1 mark):**

$$
B_{frame} = 690{,}240 \times 8 = 5{,}521{,}920 \;\text{bits/frame}
$$

**Step 3 — Bit rate (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BPS &= B_{frame} \times f \\
&= 5{,}521{,}920 \times 29.97 \\
&= 165{,}490{,}542.4 \;\text{bps} \\
&\approx 165.5 \;\text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Storage for 90 minutes (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= BPS \times t = 165.5 \times 10^6 \times (90 \times 60) \\
&= 165.5 \times 10^6 \times 5400 \\
&= 8.937 \times 10^{11} \;\text{bits} \\
&= \frac{8.937 \times 10^{11}}{8 \times 10^6} \;\text{MB} \\
&\approx 111{,}712 \;\text{MB} \\
&\approx 109 \;\text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** A 90-minute uncompressed SD video at 4:2:2 requires approximately **109 GB** of storage. This is why professional video workflows use **uncompressed only at the editing stage** and rely on **intra-frame codecs** (e.g., ProRes, DNxHR) or **long-GOP codecs** (H.264, HEVC) for delivery.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Video Questions:**
>
> 1. **NTSC is NOT exactly 30 fps** — students write 30 and lose 0.5 marks. The correct color NTSC frame rate is **30/1.001 = 29.97003 fps** because of the historical choice to keep the audio subcarrier at 4.5 MHz while using 3.579545 MHz for color.
> 2. **Confusing field rate with frame rate** — for **interlaced** systems, the *field* rate is 50/59.94 Hz but the *frame* rate is 25/29.97 fps. Always state which one.
> 3. **Aspect ratio in pixels vs. display** — a 720×480 NTSC frame has a **non-square pixel aspect ratio (PAR ≈ 10:11)** to display correctly as 4:3. Students who claim square pixels for SDTV lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Chroma subsampling direction** — in **4:2:0**, both the horizontal and vertical chroma resolutions are halved. In **4:2:2**, only horizontal is halved. Confusing these two loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting blanking intervals** — bandwidth derivations that omit $T_H (1 - \alpha_h)$ produce an overestimated bandwidth and lose 1 mark.
> 6. **PAL vs. PAL-B** — the **PAL** family contains variants B, B/G, D, G, H, I, M, N. Stating "PAL = 7 MHz" without specifying the variant loses 0.5 marks.
> 7. **HDTV sampling clock** — the **74.25 MHz** BT.709 clock is for 1080i/p; for 720p it is **74.25 / 2 = 37.125 MHz**. Memorize the 74.25 root.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fundamental Concepts and Types of Video**

- ☐ **Video = sequence of frames + synchronized audio**, displayed fast enough to exploit persistence of vision (CFF ≈ 60 Hz).
- ☐ **Three video families:** Analog (NTSC/PAL/SECAM), Digital (SDTV/HDTV/UHD), Hybrid (DV, DVD, Blu-ray).
- ☐ **Two scanning methods:** Progressive (1 field/frame) and Interlaced (2 fields/frame, offset by $T_f/2$).
- ☐ **Color subcarriers are NOT optional knowledge:** NTSC = 3.579545 MHz, PAL = 4.43361875 MHz, SECAM uses FM not subcarrier.
- ☐ **Three must-know analog standards:** NTSC-M (525/29.97), PAL-B (625/25), SECAM (625/25, sequential FM).
- ☐ **YCbCr color model** separates luma (Y) from chroma (Cb, Cr), enabling 4:4:4, 4:2:2, 4:2:0 subsampling.
- ☐ **Color Burst** = 8–10 cycles of subcarrier in the back porch used as a phase reference.
- ☐ **Gamma correction** ($\gamma \approx 2.2$) is applied *at capture* for perceptual linearization.
- ☐ **ITU-R BT.601** = SDTV digital sampling at 13.5 MHz luma.
- ☐ **ITU-R BT.709** = HDTV digital sampling at 74.25 MHz for 1080-line formats.
- ☐ **ITU-R BT.2020** = UHD (4K/8K) with 10/12-bit depth and wide color gamut.
- ☐ **Blank the blanks:** Always subtract blanking fractions ($\alpha_h \approx 0.18$, $\alpha_v \approx 0.08$) in bandwidth derivations.
- ☐ **Kell factor** ≈ 0.7 limits effective interlaced resolution — use it when asked for "perceived" lines.
- ☐ **Uncompressed 1080p30 ≈ 746 Mbps** — single most important back-of-envelope number for compression motivation.
- ☐ **Color NTSC frame rate = 30 / 1.001**, not 30. Audio subcarrier offset = **4.5 MHz** (NTSC), **5.5 / 6.0 / 6.5 MHz** (PAL variants).
- ☐ **Interlaced vs. progressive trade-off:** bandwidth vs. temporal smoothness — remember it for *both* questions and viva.
- ☐ **SDTV PAR** is **10:11** (not square); **HDTV and UHD** use **square pixels (1:1)**.
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** bandwidth = (samples/s)/2 (Nyquist), not (samples/s).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Digital Audio and Its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Basics of Digital Audio and Its Types # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Sound? (The Physical Foundation) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)** > **Sound** is a mechanical longitudinal pressure wave that propagates through an elastic medium (air, water, or solids) by causing alternate compressions (high-pressure zones) and rarefactions (low-pressure zones) of particles along the direction of wave travel. The human ear can typically perceive frequencies from **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz)** — this range is called the **audible spectrum** or **audio band**. ### Key Physical Properties of a Sound Wave | Property | Symbol | Standard Unit | Real-World Meaning | |----------|--------|---------------|---------------------| | Frequency | $f$ | Hertz (Hz) | How fast the wave oscillates (pitch) | | Amplitude | $A$ | Pascal (Pa) / Decibel (dB) | Loudness / volume | | Wavelength | $\lambda$ | Metre (m) | Distance of one full cycle | | Period | $T$ | Second (s) | Time for one complete cycle | | Phase | $\phi$ | Radians (rad) | Position in the cycle | | Velocity | $v$ | m/s | $v \approx 343$ m/s in air at 20°C | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Relation between Frequency and Period:** > > $$f = \frac{1}{T} \quad \text{and} \quad v = f \cdot \lambda$$ --- ## 1.2 What is Digital Audio? > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)** > **Digital Audio** is the numerical representation of an analog audio signal, obtained by capturing instantaneous amplitude values of the continuous sound wave at discrete time intervals and converting each value into a binary number (a sequence of 0s and 1s) suitable for storage, processing, and playback by digital systems. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at the edge of a flowing river with a **bucket** in your hand. Instead of trying to scoop the *entire* river at once, you simply **scoop water at fixed regular intervals** (say, every 1 second) and **measure exactly how much water** you collected each time using markings on the bucket (e.g., 1 litre, 2 litres, 3.5 litres). - **The river** = your continuous (analog) sound wave - **The fixed interval of scooping** = **sampling rate** (how often you record) - **The bucket markings** = **bit depth** (how precise your measurement is) - **The list of recorded measurements** = your **digital audio file** If you scoop very often and your bucket has fine markings, you get a near-perfect copy of the river. If you scoop rarely or have a coarse bucket, you lose detail. **That is the entire philosophy of digital audio!** --- ## 1.3 Analog vs. Digital Audio — A Comparison > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Comparison Table (Frequently asked in 2-mark questions)** | Parameter | Analog Audio | Digital Audio | |-----------|--------------|---------------| | Representation | Continuous waveform | Discrete numeric samples (binary) | | Storage medium | Vinyl, magnetic tape | Hard disk, SSD, memory card | | Signal degradation | Yes (every copy loses quality) | No (perfect copies forever) | | Noise immunity | Low | High | | Editing flexibility | Difficult (cut/splice tape) | Easy (cut/copy/paste) | | Dynamic range | Limited (~50–60 dB) | Wide (up to 96 dB for 16-bit) | | Required bandwidth | Low | High | | Example devices | Turntable, cassette player | Smartphone, MP3 player, DAW | --- ## 1.4 Types / Categories of Digital Audio > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Classification of Digital Audio** > Digital audio is broadly classified into **three major categories** based on the source content and application domain: 1. **Speech Audio (Voice)** — Narrowband content, primarily **300 Hz to 3,400 Hz** for telephony. Highly compressible due to predictable vocal-tract models. Used in VoIP, podcasts, voice assistants. 2. **Music Audio** — Wideband content covering the full audible range **20 Hz to 20 kHz**. Requires higher fidelity, larger storage, and better compression algorithms. Used in streaming, CD production, broadcasting. 3. **General / Ambient Sound Effects (Foley)** — Environmental sounds (rain, footsteps, traffic). Often multi-layered and used in films, games, virtual reality. Can be highly variable in spectrum and dynamics. ### Further Sub-Classification: Synthesis-Based vs. Recorded-Based | Type | Description | Example Format | |------|-------------|----------------| | **Recorded Digital Audio** | Captured directly from a microphone or line-in | WAV, AIFF, FLAC | | **Synthesized Digital Audio** | Generated mathematically by a computer or hardware | MIDI, MOD, DLS | | **Hybrid Audio** | Combines recorded samples with synthesis instructions | DLS, SoundFont (.sf2) | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Digital Audio Every digital audio system is built on **three fundamental operations** that convert the continuous world into discrete numbers. ### Pillar 1 — Sampling (Time Discretization) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Sampling)** > **Sampling** is the process of measuring the instantaneous amplitude of a continuous-time analog signal at **uniformly spaced time intervals** to obtain a sequence of discrete values called **samples**. The number of samples captured per second is called the **Sampling Rate** (or **Sampling Frequency**), denoted $f_s$, and measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **samples per second**. ### Pillar 2 — Quantization (Amplitude Discretization) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Quantization)** > **Quantization** is the process of mapping each continuous amplitude sample onto a **finite set of discrete amplitude levels**, then representing each level using a fixed number of binary bits. The number of bits used per sample is called the **Bit Depth** (or **Sample Size**), denoted $N$. If the bit depth is $N$ bits, the number of possible discrete levels is: $$L = 2^{N}$$ For example: $N = 16 \Rightarrow L = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ levels. ### Pillar 3 — Encoding (Binary Representation) The quantized integer values are then **encoded as binary numbers** (a sequence of 0s and 1s) and packed into a bitstream for storage or transmission. Common encoding schemes include **Linear Pulse Code Modulation (LPCM)**, **Differential PCM (DPCM)**, and **Adaptive DPCM (ADPCM)**. --- ## 2.2 The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem > [!NOTE] > **Theorem Statement** > A band-limited analog signal containing **no frequency components higher than $f_{max}$** Hz can be reconstructed **perfectly without any loss of information** from its samples, provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ is **strictly greater than twice** the highest frequency present in the signal. Mathematically: $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ The minimum allowed sampling rate ($2 \cdot f_{max}$) is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and half of this rate ($f_{max}$) is called the **Nyquist Frequency**. ### Why "Strictly Greater"? If we sample *exactly* at $2 \cdot f_{max}$, we hit a critical boundary condition. In practice, engineers always choose a sampling rate **slightly above** the Nyquist rate, and an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** is applied before sampling to remove any frequency content above $f_{max}$. ### Aliasing — The Sampling Disaster > [!WARNING] > **Aliasing** is the phenomenon where frequencies above $f_s/2$ (the Nyquist frequency) "fold back" and appear as **spurious lower-frequency artefacts** in the reconstructed signal. This is **irreversible** — the original information is permanently lost. This is why a proper anti-aliasing filter is mandatory *before* sampling. --- ## 2.3 The Quantization Error & Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SQNR) When a continuous amplitude is rounded to the nearest discrete level, a small error called **Quantization Noise** is introduced. For a uniform quantizer with bit depth $N$, the theoretical **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** in decibels is: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot N + 1.76 \quad (\text{dB})$$ This is the famous **"6 dB per bit" rule**: every additional bit of precision **doubles the number of quantization levels** and **adds approximately 6 dB of dynamic range**. | Bit Depth $N$ | Levels $2^N$ | Theoretical SQNR (dB) | Common Application | |:-------------:|:------------:|:---------------------:|--------------------| | 8 | 256 | ~50 dB | Telephone-quality speech | | 16 | 65,536 | ~98 dB | CD audio standard | | 24 | 16,777,216 | ~146 dB | Studio master recording | | 32 (float) | $\approx 4.29 \times 10^9$ | ~193 dB | Internal DAW processing | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR ESE** | # | Concept | Formula | Standard Units | |---|---------|---------|----------------| | 1 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | | 2 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | Hz | | 3 | Number of Quantization Levels | $L = 2^N$ | dimensionless | | 4 | SQNR (Theoretical) | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ | dB | | 5 | Quantization Step Size (Linear) | $\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^N}$ | Volts | | 6 | Audio File Size (uncompressed) | $\text{Size} = \dfrac{f_s \cdot N \cdot C \cdot D}{8}$ | Bytes | | 7 | Period of a Wave | $T = 1/f$ | seconds | | 8 | Wave Velocity | $v = f \cdot \lambda$ | m/s | | 9 | Dynamic Range (approx) | $\text{DR} \approx 6N$ | dB | > **Notation used:** $f_s$ = sampling rate, $f_{max}$ = highest input frequency, $N$ = bit depth, $C$ = number of channels (1=mono, 2=stereo), $D$ = duration in seconds, $V_{max}/V_{min}$ = peak voltage range of the signal. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The SQNR formula is the foundation of every digital audio specification on the planet. The **CD standard (Red Book, 1980)** chose **16-bit / 44.1 kHz** because: - 16 bits gives $\approx 96$ dB dynamic range (exceeds human hearing $\sim 120$ dB pain threshold by a safe margin). - 44.1 kHz is slightly above the Nyquist rate for 20 kHz audio (human hearing limit), and was convenient for video-rate storage on early equipment. The **DVD-Audio** and **Blu-ray Audio** standards use **24-bit / 96 kHz or 192 kHz** for high-resolution studio masters. **VoIP systems** (Skype, WhatsApp calls) use **8 kHz / 16-bit** because human speech is intelligible in the **300–3,400 Hz** band, and saving bandwidth is critical for real-time transmission. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Uncompressed Audio File Size Formula **Goal:** Derive the formula for the storage size (in bytes) required to record a piece of uncompressed digital audio. ### Given Data (from the problem) Let: - $f_s$ = sampling rate in samples/second (Hz) - $N$ = bit depth in bits/sample - $C$ = number of channels (1 for mono, 2 for stereo) - $D$ = total duration in seconds ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Total number of samples generated per channel per second** By definition, the sampling rate is the number of samples recorded per second. So for **one** channel: $$\text{Samples per second (1 channel)} = f_s$$ **Step 2 — Total bits produced per second (per channel)** Each sample uses $N$ bits. Hence the **bit rate** (data rate) for one channel is: $$R_{1\text{ch}} = f_s \cdot N \quad (\text{bits/second})$$ **Step 3 — Extend to multiple channels** For $C$ channels, the data rate is multiplied by $C$: $$R_{total} = C \cdot f_s \cdot N \quad (\text{bits/second})$$ **Step 4 — Multiply by the total duration** The total number of bits produced over a recording of duration $D$ seconds is: $$\text{Total Bits} = R_{total} \cdot D = C \cdot f_s \cdot N \cdot D$$ **Step 5 — Convert bits to bytes** Since $1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$, we divide by 8: $$\text{Size (bytes)} = \frac{C \cdot f_s \cdot N \cdot D}{8}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{\;\text{Size (Bytes)} = \frac{C \cdot f_s \cdot N \cdot D}{8}\;}$$ To convert to kilobytes (KB), divide by $1024$. To convert to megabytes (MB), divide by $1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576$. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — CD-Quality Stereo Audio **Problem:** Calculate the size of a 4-minute stereo song recorded at CD quality (16-bit, 44.1 kHz). ### Given Values $$f_s = 44{,}100 \text{ Hz}, \quad N = 16 \text{ bits}, \quad C = 2, \quad D = 4 \text{ min} = 240 \text{ s}$$ ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Substitute into the formula** $$\text{Size} = \frac{C \cdot f_s \cdot N \cdot D}{8}$$ $$\text{Size} = \frac{2 \times 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 240}{8}$$ **Step 2 — Group the constants** $$\text{Size} = \frac{2 \times 16}{8} \times 44{,}100 \times 240$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the fraction** $$\frac{2 \times 16}{8} = \frac{32}{8} = 4$$ **Step 4 — Compute the result** $$\text{Size} = 4 \times 44{,}100 \times 240$$ $$\text{Size} = 4 \times 10{,}584{,}000 = 42{,}336{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to megabytes** $$\text{Size in MB} = \frac{42{,}336{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 40.37 \text{ MB}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check:** A standard 700 MB CD can hold about **80 minutes** of audio. Our 4-minute song is $\sim 40$ MB, which is about $1/17.5$ of the disc — consistent with real-world CD capacity of ~17 to 18 songs per disc. ✔ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: SQNR for Uniform Linear Quantization **Goal:** Show why SQNR $\approx 6.02N + 1.76$ dB. ### Step 1 — Quantization Step Size Assume the signal swing is from $-V$ to $+V$ (peak-to-peak range $= 2V$). With $L = 2^N$ levels, the step size is: $$\Delta = \frac{2V}{L} = \frac{2V}{2^N}$$ ### Step 2 — RMS Quantization Noise Power For a uniform quantizer driven by a signal much larger than $\Delta$, the quantization error is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$. The mean-square (RMS) noise power is: $$N_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$ ### Step 3 — RMS Signal Power (Full-Scale Sine Wave) For a full-scale sine wave of amplitude $V$: $$S = \frac{V^2}{2}$$ ### Step 4 — Form the SQNR Ratio $$\text{SQNR} = \frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{V^2 / 2}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{6 V^2}{\Delta^2}$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute $\Delta = 2V / 2^N$ $$\text{SQNR} = \frac{6 V^2}{\left(\dfrac{2V}{2^N}\right)^2} = \frac{6 V^2 \cdot 2^{2N}}{4 V^2} = \frac{6 \cdot 2^{2N}}{4} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}$$ ### Step 6 — Convert to Decibels $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}\right)$$ $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20N \log_{10}(2)$$ Using $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02N$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{\;\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 N + 1.76 \;\text{dB}\;}$$ This is the celebrated **6-dB-per-bit rule** of digital audio quantization. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Audio Parameters | Use Case | Recommended $f_s$ | Recommended $N$ | Channels | Approx. Bitrate (uncompressed) | Reasoning | |----------|:-----------------:|:---------------:|:--------:|:------------------------------:|-----------| | Telephone (PSTN/VoIP) | 8 kHz | 8 bit | 1 (mono) | 64 kbps | Speech only, 300–3.4 kHz | | AM Radio (digital) | 16 kHz | 16 bit | 1 | 256 kbps | Voice + light music | | FM Radio (digital) | 32 kHz | 16 bit | 2 | 1024 kbps | Full music, mono/stereo | | CD Audio (Red Book) | 44.1 kHz | 16 bit | 2 (stereo) | 1411 kbps | Consumer hi-fi standard | | Studio Master | 96 kHz | 24 bit | 2 | 4608 kbps | Headroom for mixing | | DVD-Audio / Blu-ray | 192 kHz | 24 bit | up to 6 | 13824 kbps | Audiophile / surround | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Digital Audio Processing Pipeline The following block diagram shows the complete lifecycle of a digital audio signal — from physical sound waves to playback through a speaker. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SOURCE SOUND WAVE
Continuous Analog Signal
20 Hz to 20 kHz] --> B[ANTIALIASING
LOW PASS FILTER
Cutoff at fN equals fs slash 2] B --> C[SAMPLER
Capture amplitude at rate fs
Holds value steady] C --> D[QUANTIZER
Round to nearest of 2 to the N levels] D --> E[ENCODER
Convert to binary bitstream
LPCM or ADPCM] E --> F[DIGITAL STORAGE
OR TRANSMISSION
WAV file or network packets] F --> G[DECODER
Reconstruct levels from bits] G --> H[DIGITAL TO ANALOG CONVERTER
DAC staircase output] H --> I[RECONSTRUCTION
LOW PASS FILTER
Smooth staircase to waveform] I --> J[AMPLIFIER AND SPEAKER
Convert electrical to acoustic] J --> K[PERCEIVED SOUND
at listeners ears] ``` ### Mermaid Compilation Notes * All node identifiers (`A`, `B`, `C`...) are purely alphanumeric single letters — no reserved-keyword conflicts. * All quoted labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text and standard ASCII symbols — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tags. * The diagram flows strictly left-to-right, matching the natural KTU pedagogy of *capture → process → store → reproduce*. --- ## 4.2 Sampling & Quantization Visual Block Architecture This second diagram explains **how the two operations differ conceptually** — a common KTU viva question. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph T1[Time Domain Operation] direction TB S1[Continuous Wave] --> S2[Sampler Switch
closes at rate fs] S2 --> S3[Discrete Time Samples
still continuous in amplitude] end subgraph T2[Amplitude Domain Operation] direction TB Q1[Continuous Amplitude Values] --> Q2[Quantizer Rounding
to nearest of L levels] Q2 --> Q3[Discrete Amplitude Codes
binary integers 0 to L minus 1] end S3 --> Q1 Q3 --> R[FINAL DIGITAL AUDIO
Pair of time n and amplitude x of n] ``` ### Conceptual Walkthrough - **Time domain block (T1):** The horizontal axis (time) is sliced into discrete moments. The vertical axis (amplitude) is still smooth/continuous at this stage. - **Amplitude domain block (T2):** The horizontal axis is preserved (still discrete in time), but now the vertical amplitude is rounded onto a finite ladder of values. - **Final block (R):** A true 2-D discrete grid of points $(t_n, x[n])$ — the digital audio waveform. --- ## 4.3 Audio File Format Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DIGITAL AUDIO FILE FORMATS] --> B[UNCOMPRESSED] A --> C[LOSSLESS COMPRESSED] A --> D[LOSSY COMPRESSED] A --> E[SYNTHESIS BASED] B --> B1[WAV
Microsoft PCM container] B --> B2[AIFF
Apple PCM container] B --> B3[AU
Unix PCM container] C --> C1[FLAC
Free Lossless Audio Codec] C --> C2[ALAC
Apple Lossless] C --> C3[WV
WavPack] D --> D1[MP3
MPEG1 Layer 3] D --> D2[AAC
Advanced Audio Coding] D --> D3[OGG Vorbis
Open source] D --> D4[WMA
Windows Media Audio] E --> E1[MIDI
Instrument instructions only] E --> E2[MOD
Tracker module samples] E --> E3[SoundFont
Sample plus synthesis hybrid] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Assessment Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Part A = 3 marks each. Part B = 14 marks each with internal choice (a)/(b) sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define sampling rate and quantization. Why is the Nyquist theorem important in digital audio?** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Adapted)]` **Model Answer:** > **Sampling Rate ($f_s$):** The number of amplitude samples captured per second from a continuous audio signal, measured in Hertz (Hz) or samples per second. Standard values include 8 kHz (telephony), 44.1 kHz (CD), and 48 kHz (DVD/video). > > **Quantization:** The process of rounding each sampled amplitude value to the nearest discrete level from a set of $2^N$ possible levels, where $N$ is the bit depth. The difference between the actual value and the rounded value is called **quantization error** or **quantization noise**. > > **Importance of Nyquist Theorem:** According to the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem, to perfectly reconstruct a band-limited signal whose maximum frequency is $f_{max}$, the sampling rate must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$. If this condition is violated, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency components fold back into the audible range as false, unwanted signals, permanently corrupting the recording. Thus the theorem sets the *minimum* sampling rate and prevents aliasing, which is the foundation of all digital audio fidelity. --- ### **Q2. Distinguish between lossy and lossless audio compression. Give one example of each.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Adapted)]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Lossless Compression | Lossy Compression | |-----------|----------------------|-------------------| | **Principle** | Encodes audio with no information loss; original bit-perfect reconstruction possible | Discards perceptually irrelevant audio data; original cannot be exactly recovered | | **Compression Ratio** | Low (typically 2:1 to 3:1) | High (typically 10:1 to 12:1) | | **Quality** | Identical to original | Near-transparent at high bitrates, audible artefacts at low bitrates | | **Use Case** | Archival, studio mastering | Streaming, portable players, online music | | **Example** | **FLAC** (Free Lossless Audio Codec) | **MP3** (MPEG-1 Layer 3) | | **Another Example** | ALAC (Apple Lossless) | AAC (Advanced Audio Coding) | Lossy codecs use **psychoacoustic models** (e.g., perceptual masking, threshold of hearing) to remove data that the human ear cannot perceive anyway, achieving much smaller file sizes at the cost of permanent data loss. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)** [Choose either A or B] `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]` #### Part (a) — (7 Marks) **With suitable diagrams, explain the complete process of converting an analog audio signal into digital form. Discuss the role of the anti-aliasing filter.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — The Analog Input** An analog audio signal is a continuous function of time $x(t)$ containing frequency components in the audible band of **20 Hz to 20 kHz**. It can be represented graphically as a smooth oscillating waveform. **Step 2 — Anti-Aliasing Filter (Pre-Filtering)** A **low-pass filter** is applied with cutoff frequency equal to the Nyquist frequency $f_N = f_s/2$. This removes any frequency content above $f_N$ that would otherwise cause **aliasing** — an irreversible distortion where high frequencies reflect back into the audible spectrum. *This step is mandatory and must occur BEFORE sampling.* **Step 3 — Sampling** The filtered signal is fed into a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** which closes a switch at regular intervals $T = 1/f_s$, capturing the instantaneous amplitude at each moment. The output is a sequence of pulses $x[n] = x(nT)$ — discrete in time but still continuous in amplitude. **Step 4 — Quantization** Each sample $x[n]$ is rounded to the nearest of $L = 2^N$ discrete amplitude levels. This introduces a small **quantization error** $e[n] = x[n] - x_q[n]$, which manifests as quantization noise. **Step 5 — Encoding** The quantized integer level is converted into an $N$-bit binary code (typically in **LPCM** format). The resulting bitstream is packaged into a file format like **WAV** or **AIFF**. **Diagram (refer Section 4.1 of these notes):** The complete pipeline is **Analog → Anti-alias Filter → Sampler → Quantizer → Encoder → Bitstream**. **Conclusion:** The reverse process at playback is **Decoder → DAC → Reconstruction Filter → Amplifier → Speaker**, recovering an approximation of the original waveform. --- #### Part (b) — (7 Marks) **A stereo music track is sampled at 44.1 kHz with 16-bit resolution and lasts 5 minutes. Calculate the file size in MB. If the same track is stored in MP3 format at 192 kbps, what is the new file size, and what is the compression ratio?** **Model Solution:** **Given Data (uncompressed):** $f_s = 44{,}100$ Hz, $N = 16$ bits, $C = 2$ (stereo), $D = 5 \text{ min} = 300$ s. **Step 1 — Compute uncompressed size in bytes** $$\text{Size}_{WAV} = \frac{C \cdot f_s \cdot N \cdot D}{8}$$ $$\text{Size}_{WAV} = \frac{2 \times 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 300}{8}$$ $$\text{Size}_{WAV} = \frac{2 \times 16 \times 44{,}100 \times 300}{8} = 4 \times 44{,}100 \times 300$$ $$\text{Size}_{WAV} = 4 \times 13{,}230{,}000 = 52{,}920{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to megabytes** $$\text{Size}_{WAV} = \frac{52{,}920{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 50.47 \text{ MB}$$ **Step 3 — Compute MP3 file size at 192 kbps** MP3 bitrate is 192 kilobits per second. For duration 300 s: $$\text{Size}_{MP3} = \frac{192{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \times 300 \text{ s}}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576}$$ $$\text{Size}_{MP3} = \frac{57{,}600{,}000}{8{,}388{,}608} \approx 6.87 \text{ MB}$$ **Step 4 — Compute compression ratio** $$\text{CR} = \frac{\text{Size}_{WAV}}{\text{Size}_{MP3}} = \frac{50.47}{6.87} \approx 7.35 : 1$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** WAV file $\approx 50.47$ MB, MP3 file $\approx 6.87$ MB, Compression ratio $\approx 7.35:1$. --- ### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice] `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]` #### Part (a) — (7 Marks) **Explain the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem. A musician wants to digitally record a violin whose highest harmonic reaches 18 kHz. What is the minimum sampling rate? If the recording uses 16-bit quantization, calculate the theoretical SQNR in dB.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Statement of the Theorem** A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be reconstructed *exactly* from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ The minimum allowed value, $2 \cdot f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**. Half of this is the **Nyquist Frequency**, $f_N = f_s/2$. **Step 2 — Apply to the violin's highest harmonic** $f_{max} = 18$ kHz. Therefore: $$f_s \geq 2 \times 18 \text{ kHz} = 36 \text{ kHz}$$ **Minimum Sampling Rate = 36,000 Hz (36 kHz).** In practice, the engineer would choose a *standard* rate slightly above this, e.g., **44.1 kHz** (CD standard), to accommodate practical anti-aliasing filter roll-off. **Step 3 — Theoretical SQNR for 16-bit quantization** Using the derived formula: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 N + 1.76$$ $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76$$ $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation** A dynamic range of ~98 dB is more than sufficient for music recording, since the threshold of human pain is ~120 dB and the threshold of hearing is 0 dB. The CD standard was deliberately engineered around this value of $N = 16$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** Minimum $f_s = 36$ kHz, Theoretical SQNR $\approx 98.08$ dB. --- #### Part (b) — (7 Marks) **Describe the MIDI standard. How is it fundamentally different from a sampled audio file like WAV? List at least four advantages of MIDI over digital audio for music composition.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition of MIDI** **MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface)** is a *technical standard* (introduced in 1983) that describes a protocol, digital interface, and file format (.mid) for communicating musical performance information between electronic instruments, computers, and related devices. A MIDI file does **not** contain any actual audio waveform data. Instead, it contains a **list of instructions** such as *"Note On: pitch 60 (Middle C), velocity 80, on channel 1, at time 0.5 seconds"* and *"Note Off, channel 1, at time 1.2 seconds"*. **Step 2 — Fundamental Difference from WAV** | Aspect | WAV (Sampled Audio) | MIDI (Instructional) | |--------|---------------------|----------------------| | **Content** | Digitized audio waveform (bits) | Symbolic musical events (notes, controllers) | | **File Size** | Large (e.g., 50 MB for 5-min song) | Tiny (e.g., 50–100 KB for same song) | | **Editable** | Hard — must re-record or DSP-edit | Trivially — change note/pitch/tempo | | **Synthesizer Required** | No (plays back directly) | Yes (must render to audio) | | **Quality depends on** | Bit depth and sample rate | Sound library/instrument patches used | | **Sound Source** | Real recorded performance | Synthesized electronic tones | **Step 3 — Four Advantages of MIDI over Sampled Audio for Composition** 1. **Extremely Compact File Size:** A 5-minute orchestral piece may be just 100 KB in MIDI vs. 50 MB in WAV — a **500:1** size advantage. 2. **Easy Editing:** The composer can change a wrong note by simply editing a number in the file, without re-recording any audio. Tempo, key, and instrumentation can be altered non-destructively. 3. **Instrument Flexibility:** The same MIDI file can be played back on a piano, guitar, strings, or even a full orchestra, simply by changing the playback device or sound bank. 4. **Perfect Quality on Replay:** MIDI audio is re-synthesized each time, so it is **not subject to generation loss**. A WAV file copied/recoded multiple times loses quality, but a MIDI file does not. **Conclusion:** MIDI is a *performance description language*, whereas WAV is a *captured acoustic result*. For composition, education, and prototyping, MIDI is vastly more efficient; for distribution and archival fidelity, sampled audio (WAV/FLAC) is preferred. --- ## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE Valuation** > > 1. **Confusing the formula direction.** Many students write $f_{max} \geq 2 f_s$. The correct relationship is **$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$**. The sampling rate must be *at least* double the highest frequency — not the other way around. This single error costs the entire 7 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting to multiply by the number of channels.** In file-size problems, students often calculate for mono (1 channel) but the question states stereo (2 channels). Always re-read the problem statement for the channel count. > > 3. **Unit conversion pitfalls.** Forgetting to convert **minutes to seconds** (multiply by 60) or **bits to bytes** (divide by 8) or **bytes to MB** (divide by $1024^2$, not $10^6$). Each conversion is a potential mark deduction. > > 4. **Confusing SQNR formula.** Writing $6N$ (without the $+ 1.76$ and the $0.02$ precision factor) loses the 1.76 dB mark. Use the full form: $\text{SQNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB. > > 5. **Aliasing definition.** Many students write *"aliasing is noise"*. The correct definition is **"aliasing is the folding back of frequencies above the Nyquist frequency into the audible band, causing irreversible distortion."** Examiners mark strictly on terminology. > > 6. **Confusing MIDI and audio.** Stating that *"MIDI is a compressed audio format"* is a **factual error** that costs full marks on that sub-question. MIDI is *not* audio — it is a set of performance instructions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 4, BASICS OF DIGITAL AUDIO** - **Sound** is a mechanical pressure wave; the audible band is **20 Hz – 20 kHz**. - **Digital audio** = discrete-time + discrete-amplitude numeric representation of sound. - **Three pillars:** Sampling → Quantization → Encoding. - **Sampling rate $f_s$** = samples per second (Hz). Higher $f_s$ = higher frequency capture. - **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Violating this causes **aliasing** (irreversible). - **Anti-aliasing filter** is a mandatory low-pass filter *before* sampling. - **Bit depth $N$** determines the number of amplitude levels: $L = 2^N$. - **SQNR formula (memorize verbatim):** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB. - **"6-dB-per-bit rule"** — every extra bit adds ~6 dB of dynamic range. - **Uncompressed file size:** $\text{Size (Bytes)} = \dfrac{C \cdot f_s \cdot N \cdot D}{8}$. - **CD standard:** 16-bit, 44.1 kHz, stereo → 1411 kbps uncompressed bitrate. - **Voice/Studio:** 8 kHz/16-bit (VoIP) vs. 96 kHz/24-bit (studio master). - **Types of digital audio:** Speech, Music, Ambient/Foley. - **Audio categories by content:** Recorded (WAV, AIFF), Synthesized (MIDI), Hybrid (SoundFont). - **Lossless formats** (FLAC, ALAC, WAV): no data loss, low compression ratio. - **Lossy formats** (MP3, AAC, OGG Vorbis): use psychoacoustic models, high compression. - **MIDI** is *not* audio — it is a symbolic instruction protocol for musical performance. - **Aliasing** is the most-tested 2-mark concept — be ready to define, state the prevention, and give an example. - **Standard sample rates to remember:** 8 kHz, 11.025 kHz, 22.05 kHz, 32 kHz, 44.1 kHz, 48 kHz, 96 kHz, 192 kHz. - **Standard bit depths:** 8-bit (telephony), 16-bit (CD), 24-bit (studio), 32-bit float (internal DAW).

topics.title: Lossless Compression – Run-Length Coding and Arithmetic Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lossless Compression – Run-Length Coding and Arithmetic Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lossless Compression — The Big Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Lossless compression* is a class of data encoding algorithms that compress data in such a way that the **original information can be perfectly reconstructed from the compressed data, bit-for-bit, with zero information loss*. It is mathematically governed by **Kraft's Inequality** and approaches the theoretical **Shannon Entropy** $\left( H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i \right)$ as a lower bound on average code length.

**Why do we need it?** Many file types — text documents, executable programs, medical X-rays, satellite imagery, database records — *cannot tolerate* a single flipped bit. Lossy compression (like JPEG) is great for photos, but for these, we need **mathematically exact, reversible** encoding.

**Real-World Analogy (Intuition):**
> Think of lossless compression as *packing a suitcase so that every single sock, shirt, and trouser can be re-folded back into the *exact* original arrangement* — no wrinkles added, no items lost, no items changed. You save space (bits), but the original is bit-perfectly preserved.

## 1.2 Run-Length Coding (RLC) — The "Counting Game"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Run-Length Encoding (RLE)* is a lossless data compression technique in which **consecutive identical data elements (called "runs") are stored as a single data value and a count**, rather than as the original run. Formally, a run of $n$ identical symbols $s$ is represented as the pair $\langle n, s \rangle$.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine you are counting tally marks on a piece of paper. Instead of drawing `||||` (four strokes) for the number four, you just write **"4 |"**. Same information, but you used fewer symbols. RLE does *exactly* this for any data stream.

**Where is RLE used in real life?**
- **Bilevel (black & white) fax transmission (T.4/T.6 standards)** — long runs of black or white pixels.
- **Simple bitmap images (.BMP, .PCX)** — areas of uniform color.
- **Run-length limited (RLL) encoding** in hard-disk storage.
- **Pieces in board games, snake game trails, simple animations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Run Length (n):** Number of consecutive identical symbols. *Must be $\geq 1$*.
> - **Standard ASCII storage:** Run length stored in **1 byte (0–255)** and symbol in **1 byte**, so a 4-byte tuple represents up to 255 repetitions.

## 1.3 Arithmetic Coding — The "Magic Interval" Trick

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Arithmetic Coding* is an **entropy-based lossless compression technique** that encodes an *entire message as a single real number (or a short binary fraction) in the half-open interval $[0, 1)$*. The output interval's length is proportional to the **probability of the message**, achieving compression that can approach the **Shannon entropy limit** with an efficiency of nearly **100%**.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine you have a **number line from 0 to 1**. You give every symbol its own "territory" on this line, sized according to how often it appears (probabilities). Then for each symbol in your message, you **zoom into that symbol's territory** and repeat the subdivision. After processing the whole message, you are left looking at a tiny slice. You just announce *any number* inside that slice — that single number encodes the **entire message**. The decoder reverses the journey, looking up which territory your number falls in, symbol by symbol.

**Why is this revolutionary?**
Unlike Huffman coding (which assigns *whole bits* per symbol, wasting up to 1 bit per symbol), arithmetic coding encodes **the whole message holistically** — squeezing out the last possible bit of redundancy.

**Where is it used?**
- **JPEG 2000** image compression standard
- **H.264 / H.265 / AV1** video codecs
- **DEFLATE** (basis of ZIP, PNG, gzip)
- **Brotli** web compression
- **PDF** document compression

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Boundaries:**
> - The encoder interval is **always the half-open interval $[0, 1)$**.
> - The minimum number of bits required to encode a message of probability $P$ is $\lceil -\log_2 P \rceil$ — this is the theoretical **entropy bound**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Run-Length Coding — Operational Logic

The RLE algorithm is built on a simple state machine. Here is the structured logic:

### **Encoding Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)**

1. **Initialize** a counter `count = 1` and read the first symbol `s`.
2. **Iterate** through the source sequence from left to right.
3. For each new symbol `c`:
   - If `c == s` **and** `count < 255` → increment `count`.
   - Else → **emit** the pair $\langle count, s \rangle$ to the output, reset `count = 1`, and update `s = c`.
