## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** What is *transform coding*? Why is the **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** preferred over the **Fourier Transform** for image compression?

> **Model Answer:**
> Transform coding is a compression technique that converts spatial-domain data into a frequency-domain representation using an orthogonal transform, after which the coefficients are quantized and entropy-coded. The DCT is preferred over the DFT because:
> 1. The DCT produces **only real coefficients**, halving computational and storage cost compared to the complex-valued DFT.
> 2. The DCT avoids the **Gibbs phenomenon** (boundary discontinuities) because it implicitly assumes even periodic extension of the signal — the standard JPEG 8×8 DCT does not introduce visible seam artefacts at block boundaries.
> 3. The DCT has excellent **energy compaction** properties that closely approach the optimal Karhunen-Loève Transform (KLT) for natural images, but is **data-independent** (a fixed basis), unlike KLT which requires computing eigenvectors from the image statistics.
> 4. There exist fast algorithms (similar to the FFT) reducing the per-block cost to $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]** List any **three** major differences between **Baseline JPEG** and **JPEG-LS** standards.

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Compression Type** — Baseline JPEG is *lossy* (using DCT + quantization); JPEG-LS is primarily *lossless* (with optional near-lossless mode).
> 2. **Core Technique** — Baseline JPEG uses *8×8 DCT + scalar quantization + Huffman coding*; JPEG-LS uses *predictive coding (MED predictor) + adaptive Golomb-Rice entropy coding*.
> 3. **Application Domain** — Baseline JPEG is used for *photographs and the World Wide Web* where small losses are acceptable; JPEG-LS is used in *medical imaging (DICOM), satellite imagery, and archival* where bit-exact reconstruction is mandatory.
> 4. **Compression Ratio** — Baseline JPEG achieves *10:1 – 20:1*; JPEG-LS achieves only *~2:1 – 3:1* but with zero loss.
> 5. **Computational Complexity** — JPEG-LS is significantly *simpler* and faster than baseline JPEG (no DCT/IDCT).

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Note to students:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 questions typically have an internal choice. We provide TWO complete alternatives below.

---

#### **Question A — [14 Marks]** *(Lossy Transform Coding & JPEG Pipeline Focus)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **JPEG baseline encoder**. Describe each stage with the equations used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the following 4×4 block, compute the forward DCT. Use the standard 1-D DCT formula and explain the role of the DC coefficient.

$$f = \begin{bmatrix} 200 & 200 & 200 & 200 \\ 200 & 200 & 200 & 200 \\ 200 & 200 & 200 & 200 \\ 200 & 200 & 200 & 200 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Model Solution (a):**
> The JPEG baseline encoder consists of the following stages (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):
>
> 1. **Color Space Conversion** — RGB image converted to YCbCr: $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$, $Cb = 128 + 0.564(B-Y)$, $Cr = 128 + 0.713(R-Y)$. This separates luminance (Y) from chrominance (Cb, Cr) so chroma can be sub-sampled.
> 2. **Chroma Sub-sampling** — Typically 4:2:0; the Cb and Cr planes are halved in both dimensions because the eye is less sensitive to colour detail.
> 3. **Level Shift** — Each pixel $p$ becomes $p - 128$ so the DC range is centred on zero (improves DCT efficiency for unsigned 8-bit images).
> 4. **Block Partition** — Image split into 8×8 blocks.
> 5. **Forward DCT** — Apply $F(u,v) = C(u)C(v)\sum_{x,y} f(x,y)\cos[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}]\cos[\frac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}]$.
> 6. **Quantization** — $\tilde{F}(u,v) = \text{round}[F(u,v)/Q(u,v)]$.
> 7. **Differential DC Coding** — Encode $\text{DC}_i - \text{DC}_{i-1}$.
> 8. **Zigzag Scan** — Reorder AC coefficients for run-length efficiency.
> 9. **Run-Length + Huffman** — Variable-length coding.

> **Model Solution (b):**
> For a constant 4×4 block $f(x,y) = 200$, applying the 1-D DCT row-wise first:
>
> $$F(u) = C(u)\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{4}}\sum_{x=0}^{3} 200 \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{8}\right]$$
>
> For $u = 0$: $F(0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot 200 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot [1+1+1+1] = 200 \cdot 4 = 800$.
> For $u = 1, 2, 3$: the cosines form symmetric pairs that cancel to 0. So $F(1) = F(2) = F(3) = 0$.
>
> Then the 2-D DCT becomes $F(u,v) = F_{\text{row}}(u) \cdot F_{\text{col}}(v)$ (separable transform).
> Hence the only non-zero coefficient is the DC term:
>
> $$F(0,0) = 800 \times 800 = 640\,000 \quad \text{(or } = 4096 \text{ if we use normalised form with N=8)}$$
>
> All other coefficients $F(u,v) = 0$ for $(u,v) \neq (0,0)$.
>
> **Role of DC coefficient:** It represents the **average intensity** of the block. For uniform blocks, all detail (AC) coefficients vanish, and only the DC term remains — the perfect illustration of energy compaction. For natural blocks, the DC term contains the most energy, which is why it is differentially coded and given special Huffman category treatment.

---

#### **Question B — [14 Marks]** *(JPEG-LS Focus & Predictive Coding)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **JPEG-LS** compression standard with its encoder block diagram. Describe the **LOCO-I** algorithm and the **MED predictor** in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the 8-bit pixel values in the 2-D neighbourhood below (X is the pixel to predict):

$$\begin{matrix} & c & b \\ a & X & \\ \end{matrix} \quad \text{with} \quad a = 100,\; b = 130,\; c = 105,\; X_{\text{actual}} = 125$$

Compute the **predicted value**, the **prediction error**, and demonstrate the **Golomb-Rice coding** for the residual using parameter $k = 2$.

> **Model Solution (a):**
> **JPEG-LS** (ISO 14495-1) is the lossless/near-lossless JPEG standard based on the **LOCO-I (LOw COmplexity LOssless COmpression for Images)** algorithm by Weinberger, Seroussi and Sapiro (1996).
>
> **Pipeline (refer to Section 4.3 diagram):**
> 1. Predict the current pixel $\hat{x}$ from its causal neighbours using the MED predictor.
> 2. Compute the residual $e = x - \hat{x}$.
> 3. Estimate the local gradient (e.g., $D_1 = d - b$, $D_2 = b - c$, $D_3 = c - a$) and quantize each gradient into one of 9 zones $\{−4,−3,−2,−1,0,1,2,3,4\}$. This gives a *context* index out of $9^3 = 729$ possible contexts.
> 4. Maintain running sums $A$ (sum of absolute residuals) and $N$ (count of residuals) per context.
> 5. Compute optimal Golomb-Rice parameter $k$ from $A$ and $N$ using $k = \lfloor \log_2(A/N) \rfloor$.
> 6. Apply Golomb-Rice encoding to the mapped residual.
>
> **MED Predictor (full derivation in Section 3.3):**
>
> $$\hat{x} = \begin{cases} \min(a,b) & \text{if } c \geq \max(a,b) \quad (\text{horizontal edge}) \\ \max(a,b) & \text{if } c \leq \min(a,b) \quad (\text{vertical edge}) \\ a + b - c & \text{otherwise (smooth region)} \end{cases}$$
>
> The predictor is computationally trivial (three comparisons and at most two additions), making JPEG-LS ideal for hardware and low-power devices.

> **Model Solution (b):**
> Given: $a = 100$ (West), $b = 130$ (North), $c = 105$ (NW), $X_{\text{actual}} = 125$.
>
> **Step 1 — Check MED conditions:**
> - $\max(a,b) = \max(100, 130) = 130$
> - $\min(a,b) = \min(100, 130) = 100$
> - Condition 1: $c \geq \max(a,b)$? Is $105 \geq 130$? **No.**
> - Condition 2: $c \leq \min(a,b)$? Is $105 \leq 100$? **No.**
> - Therefore we are in the **smooth-region** case.
>
> **Step 2 — Prediction:**
>
> $$\hat{x} = a + b - c = 100 + 130 - 105 = 125$$
>
> **Step 3 — Prediction Error:**
>
> $$e = X_{\text{actual}} - \hat{x} = 125 - 125 = 0$$
>
> **Step 4 — Golomb-Rice Coding of $e = 0$ with $k = 2$:**
> - Quotient $q = \lfloor 0 / 2^2 \rfloor = \lfloor 0/4 \rfloor = 0$.
> - Remainder $r = 0 \bmod 4 = 0$, encoded in $k = 2$ binary bits: `00`.
> - Unary prefix: $q$ zeros followed by a single `1` — i.e., `1` (the unary code for zero).
> - Full Golomb-Rice codeword: `1 00` = **3 bits**.
>
> Original would have been 8 bits (one full pixel); we saved 5 bits per pixel on this one. Over an image of $N$ pixels, the savings compound to roughly **2:1 – 3:1** even on natural images.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (where KTU students lose marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $C(u), C(v)$ normalisation constants** in the DCT formula — examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you write only $\sum\sum f \cos\cos$ without the $C(u)C(v)$ factor.
> 2. **Confusing "lossless" with "JPEG-LS"** — JPEG-LS is the *lossless* standard, NOT to be confused with the lossless mode of baseline JPEG (which uses *predictive coding without the DCT*, totally different from JPEG-LS).
> 3. **Saying "JPEG is lossless" or "JPEG-LS is lossy"** — always clarify *baseline JPEG* vs *JPEG-LS*.
> 4. **Wrong MED branch** — when asked to compute the predicted value, students frequently skip the condition check and directly write $a + b - c$. This is wrong: the MED predictor has *three branches*, and the choice depends on the local gradient.
> 5. **Confusing quantization tables with the DCT output** — these are *separate* matrices; the table is fixed in the standard, while the DCT output varies per image.
> 6. **Skipping zigzag scan in JPEG pipeline** — many answers jump from quantization to Huffman, ignoring the crucial intermediate step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet Bank**

- [x] **Transform coding** = orthogonal transform + quantization + entropy coding. Three redundancies exploited: coding (statistical), inter-pixel (spatial), and psycho-visual (perceptual).
- [x] **DCT** is the transform of choice in JPEG/MPEG because: real-valued, energy-compacting, avoids Gibbs phenomenon, fast algorithms exist.
- [x] **JPEG baseline pipeline (in order):** RGB → YCbCr → 4:2:0 sub-sample → level shift ($-128$) → 8×8 blocks → DCT → quantize → DC differencing → zigzag → RLE → Huffman.
- [x] **DC coefficient** = average brightness of the block. **AC coefficients** = spatial frequencies.
- [x] **Quantization is the ONLY lossy step** in baseline JPEG.
- [x] **Zigzag scan** orders coefficients by frequency so low-frequency (high-magnitude) coefficients are visited first, producing long zero-runs later.
- [x] **JPEG modes:** Baseline (sequential, lossy), Progressive, Lossless (predictive, *not* DCT-based), Hierarchical.
- [x] **JPEG-LS** = ISO 14495-1, LOCO-I algorithm. Lossless + optional near-lossless. No DCT used.
- [x] **MED predictor** has three branches based on whether $c \geq \max(a,b)$, $c \leq \min(a,b)$, or otherwise.
- [x] **Golomb-Rice** is asymptotically optimal for geometrically-distributed residuals; $k$ is chosen adaptively per context.
- [x] **JPEG** ≈ 10:1–20:1 lossy. **JPEG-LS** ≈ 2:1–3:1 lossless.
- [x] **JPEG-LS** is the *de facto* choice for medical imaging (DICOM) and archival; **Baseline JPEG** dominates Web/photography.
- [x] Key standards: **ISO 10918-1 (JPEG, 1992)** and **ISO 14495-1 (JPEG-LS, 1999)**.
- [x] **PSNR** $= 10 \log_{10}(255^2/\text{MSE})$ dB; higher is better quality.
- [x] **MSE** $= \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{x,y}[f(x,y) - \hat{f}(x,y)]^2$.
- [x] JPEG-LS handles *run mode* (when $N$ for a context is small) using simple run-length encoding, and *regular mode* otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: H.261 Video Compression Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST527 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS & MULTIMEDIA
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** H.261 Video Compression Technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **H.261** is the first practical **ITU-T (formerly CCITT) hybrid video compression standard**, finalized in **1993**, originally known as the **"p × 64 kbit/s"** codec. It was designed primarily for **ISDN-based videotelephony and video conferencing** services, where the available channel bandwidth is a multiple of the basic ISDN B-channel rate of **64 kbit/s**.

H.261 is classified as a **hybrid (intra + inter) codec** because it jointly uses:
- **Intra-frame redundancy removal** via 8×8 Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) + quantization + Variable Length Coding (VLC).
- **Inter-frame temporal redundancy removal** via block-based **Motion Estimation (ME)** and **Motion Compensation (MC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Design Specification of H.261:**
> - **Target Bit Rate:** $p \times 64$ kbit/s, where $p \in [1, 30]$.
> - **Spatial Resolutions:** Common Intermediate Format (**CIF**, $352 \times 288$) and Quarter CIF (**QCIF**, $176 \times 144$).
> - **Frame Types:** I-frames (Intra-coded) and P-frames (Predictive-coded).
> - **Color Subsampling:** 4:2:0 (Y : Cb : Cr).
> - **Basic Processing Unit:** 8×8 pixel block.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magic Notebook"

> [!TIP]
> **Think of H.261 as a clever detective filling a notebook while watching a movie:**

1. The detective draws the **first frame** carefully (this is the **I-frame** — fully described, like a fresh painting).
2. For every subsequent frame, instead of redrawing everything, the detective notes: *"Same as the previous frame, except this small piece moved 3 squares left and 2 squares up."* (This is **motion estimation** producing a **motion vector**.)
3. Only the **tiny differences** (residuals) between the predicted block and the actual block are scribbled down (this is the **DCT + quantization** stage).
4. Common scribbles (e.g., the word "and") are written as short symbols (**Variable Length Coding**) to save space.

The notebook is the **bitstream**; the detective's previous drawings are the **frame store**; the moving-piece note is the **motion vector**. H.261 essentially does this for every macroblock, every frame.

---

### 1.3 Standardized Metrics Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Standard Metrics (verbatim from ITU-T Recommendation H.261):**
> - **Source formats:** **CIF (352×288)** and **QCIF (176×144)** at approximately **29.97 fps (NTSC)** or **25 fps (PAL)** frame rates.
> - **Mandatory decoder requirement:** every H.261 decoder must support **QCIF**; CIF is optional.
> - **Symbol alphabet size:** **4 bits** for general picture coding symbols (MQUANT, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of the H.261 Encoder

H.261 is a **closed-loop predictive hybrid DPCM/DCT codec**. The encoder contains a replica of the decoder in its feedback path so that predicted frames are reconstructed from already-decoded pixels (preventing drift between encoder and decoder).

**Step-by-step Encoding Logic:**

1. **Frame Partitioning** — Each incoming picture is divided into:
   - **Picture → Group of Blocks (GOB) → Macroblock (MB) → Block (8×8)**.
2. **Macroblock Decision** — For each MB:
   - If scene change / I-frame → use **Intra mode (no motion compensation)**.
   - Otherwise → use **Inter mode (motion estimation + compensation)**.
3. **Motion Estimation (ME)** — Performed only at the encoder. Searches the previous reconstructed frame for the best-matching 16×16 MB, producing a **motion vector (MV)**.
4. **Prediction Subtraction** — The predicted block is subtracted from the current block to form the **residual (prediction error)**.
5. **DCT (8×8)** — Applied to the residual (inter) or to the original block (intra).
6. **Quantization** — DCT coefficients are quantized to remove perceptually insignificant detail.
7. **VLC Encoding** — Quantized coefficients are zig-zag scanned and entropy-coded using **H.261's 2-D VLC table (RUN, LEVEL)**.
8. **Feedback Reconstruction** — Inverse quantize + Inverse DCT + add prediction → stored in **Frame Store** for future prediction.
9. **Bit-Rate Control** — Adjusts the **MQUANT** (quantization parameter) per macroblock to keep the output buffer from overflowing.

---

### 2.2 H.261 Hierarchical Block Structure

| Layer | Size | Quantity per QCIF | Quantity per CIF |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Picture (Frame)** | $176 \times 144$ / $352 \times 288$ | 1 | 1 |
| **Group of Blocks (GOB)** | $176 \times 48$ / $352 \times 48$ | 3 | 6 |
| **Macroblock (MB)** | $16 \times 16$ (4 luma + 2 chroma blocks) | $\mathbf{11 \times 9 = 99}$ | $22 \times 18 = 396$ |
| **Block** | $8 \times 8$ | 4 luma + 2 chroma = **6 per MB** | Same |

> [!NOTE]
> A **GOB** corresponds to **11 MBs (QCIF)** or **22 MBs (CIF)** arranged horizontally. A **Macroblock (MB)** contains **4 luma (Y) blocks + 1 Cb + 1 Cr block**, all of size $8 \times 8$, i.e., 6 DCT blocks per MB.

---

### 2.3 Frame Types in H.261

| Frame Type | Abbreviation | Reference? | MB Type Allowed | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Intra-coded** | **I-frame** | None | Only Intra MBs | Forced refresh (every 132 frames typical) |
| **Predictive-coded** | **P-frame** | Previous frame | Intra or Inter MBs | Normal inter-frame coding |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are recurring high-yield items for KTU Board Examination questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Symbol | Units / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Channel Bit Rate** | $R = p \times 64$ | kbit/s, $p \in [1, 30]$ |
| 2 | **Quantization (Inter / AC Intra)** | $\vert \text{level} \vert = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{\vert c \vert}{2 \cdot \text{MQUANT}}\right)$ | MQUANT $\in [1, 31]$ |
| 3 | **DC Intra Quantization** | $\text{level}_{DC} = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{DC}}{8}\right)$ | Uniform step = 8 |
| 4 | **Inverse Quantization (Reconstruction)** | $c' = 2 \cdot \text{MQUANT} \cdot \text{level} + \text{sign}(\text{level})\cdot \text{MQUANT}$ | If $\vert \text{level} \vert > 0$ |
| 5 | **SAD (Block Matching Cost)** | $\text{SAD}(dx,dy) = \sum_{x=0}^{15}\sum_{y=0}^{15} \vert I_{cur}(x,y) - I_{ref}(x+dx, y+dy) \vert$ | Lower is better |
| 6 | **MAD (Mean Absolute Difference)** | $\text{MAD} = \dfrac{1}{256}\,\text{SAD}$ | Same range as SAD |
| 7 | **Compression Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Uncompressed Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **PSNR (Reconstruction Quality)** | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^{2}}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ dB | dB, typical 30–40 dB |
| 9 | **MSE (Mean Squared Error)** | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum (I - I')^{2}$ | Per-pixel variance |
| 10 | **DCT 1-D Kernel** | $F(u) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{N}} C(u) \sum_{x=0}^{N-1} f(x) \cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2x+1)u\pi}{2N}\right]$ | $C(0) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\ C(u) = 1$ |
| 11 | **QCIF Macroblock Count** | $N_{MB} = 11 \times 9 = 99$ | MBs per QCIF frame |
| 12 | **CIF Macroblock Count** | $N_{MB} = 22 \times 18 = 396$ | MBs per CIF frame |

> [!NOTE]
> In Formula #4, the term $\text{sign}(\text{level})\cdot \text{MQUANT}$ is the **dead-zone offset** that H.261 adds to push reconstruction toward the middle of the quantization bin, reducing quantization noise.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation for video communications**: H.261 was the **first codec** deployed in commercial ISDN video phones and **3G video calling**.
- **Direct ancestor** of **H.263, H.264/AVC, MPEG-1, MPEG-2, and MPEG-4 Part 2** — all use the **same hybrid DPCM/DCT** skeleton.
- **Industrial utility**: Video conferencing (legacy Polycom, Tandberg systems), early 3G-324M mobile video calls, and surveillance DVRs.
- **Pedagogical utility**: H.261 is **the canonical teaching codec** because its toolset (ME/MC, DCT, Q, VLC, BRC) reappears in every later standard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — H.261 Bit Rate for a Given ISDN Configuration

> **Problem:** A KTU board-question scenario requires the bit rate for $p = 6$ B-channels of ISDN.

**Step 1 — Recall the ISDN hierarchy:**
A single ISDN **B-channel** has a capacity of **64 kbit/s**.

**Step 2 — Apply the H.261 channel formula:**
The H.261 standard allows aggregation of $p$ such B-channels:

$$
R \;=\; p \times 64 \;\; \text{kbit/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $p = 6$:**

$$
R \;=\; 6 \times 64 \;=\; 384 \;\; \text{kbit/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in Mbit/s for context:**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{384}{1024} \;\approx\; 0.375 \;\; \text{Mbit/s}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 6-channel ISDN configuration delivers **384 kbit/s** of compressed video — sufficient for acceptable CIF video conferencing at ~15 fps in 1990s hardware.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantization of a DCT Coefficient (Worked Numerical Example)

> **Problem:** During H.261 encoding of an inter-frame residual, an 8×8 DCT block yields a particular AC coefficient $c = -146$. The current macroblock has $\text{MQUANT} = 5$. Compute the quantized level and the reconstructed value.

**Step 1 — Apply the quantization formula (Formula #2 in the cheat sheet):**

$$
\vert \text{level} \vert \;=\; \text{round}\!\left(\frac{\vert c \vert}{2 \cdot \text{MQUANT}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
\vert \text{level} \vert \;=\; \text{round}\!\left(\frac{146}{2 \times 5}\right) \;=\; \text{round}\!\left(\frac{146}{10}\right) \;=\; \text{round}(14.6) \;=\; 15
$$

**Step 3 — Reapply the sign of the original coefficient:**

$$
\text{level} \;=\; \text{sign}(c) \cdot 15 \;=\; -1 \times 15 \;=\; -15
$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruct the coefficient using Inverse Quantization (Formula #4):**

$$
c' \;=\; 2 \cdot \text{MQUANT} \cdot \text{level} + \text{sign}(\text{level}) \cdot \text{MQUANT}
$$

$$
c' \;=\; 2 \times 5 \times (-15) + (-1)(5) \;=\; -150 - 5 \;=\; -155
$$

**Step 5 — Compute quantization error:**

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; c - c' \;=\; -146 - (-155) \;=\; 9
$$

**Step 6 — Percent error analysis:**

$$
\text{Error \%} \;=\; \frac{\vert \varepsilon \vert}{\vert c \vert} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{9}{146} \times 100 \;\approx\; 6.16 \%
$$

> **Conclusion:** The coefficient $-146$ is represented using just **6 bits** (signed magnitude, range $\pm 63$) instead of the original 9 bits needed for $\pm 255$ pixel values — a significant saving that the VLC layer will compress further.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Block-Matching Motion Estimation (Full Search)

> **Problem:** A 16×16 macroblock is to be motion-compensated with a **search range of $\pm 2$ pixels** horizontally and vertically. Compute the candidate positions and the SAD expression.

**Step 1 — Define the search grid:**
The H.261 motion vector range for each axis is $dx, dy \in [-2, +2]$ (a typical small range).

$$
N_{candidates} \;=\; (2 \cdot d_x + 1) \times (2 \cdot d_y + 1) \;=\; 5 \times 5 \;=\; 25 \;\;\text{candidates}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the matching cost (Formula #5):**

$$
\text{SAD}(dx, dy) \;=\; \sum_{x=0}^{15}\sum_{y=0}^{15} \;\vert\; I_{\text{cur}}(x, y) \;-\; I_{\text{ref}}(x + dx, \; y + dy)\;\vert
$$

**Step 3 — Optimize the search (full-search algorithm):**

$$
\text{MV}_{\text{opt}} \;=\; \arg\min_{(dx,dy)} \; \text{SAD}(dx, dy)
$$

**Step 4 — Hypothetical numerical example:**
Suppose the SAD values at the five central candidates are:

| $(dx, dy)$ | $(-1, -1)$ | $(0, -1)$ | $(0, 0)$ | $(1, 0)$ | $(1, 1)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| SAD | 1820 | 1450 | **1180** | 1390 | 1740 |

The minimum SAD is **1180** at $(0, 0)$, so the chosen motion vector is:

$$
\text{MV} \;=\; (0, 0)
$$

This means **no motion** is detected — the encoder may choose to skip this MB (further saving bits).

> **Conclusion:** Even a small search window produces 25 SAD computations per macroblock — for a CIF frame (396 MBs) at 15 fps, that is $\approx 148{,}500$ SAD computations per second, justifying the need for **fast ME algorithms** (3SS, 4SS, diamond search, etc.) in real H.261 hardware.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Macroblock Count Verification for QCIF

> **Problem:** Verify that a QCIF frame ($176 \times 144$ luma) contains exactly **99 macroblocks**.

**Step 1 — Number of MBs horizontally:**

$$
N_h \;=\; \frac{176}{16} \;=\; 11
$$

**Step 2 — Number of MBs vertically:**

$$
N_v \;=\; \frac{144}{16} \;=\; 9
$$

**Step 3 — Total macroblocks:**

$$
N_{MB} \;=\; N_h \times N_v \;=\; 11 \times 9 \;=\; 99
$$

**Step 4 — Total DCT blocks per frame (6 blocks per MB):**

$$
N_{blocks} \;=\; 6 \times 99 \;=\; 594
$$

> **Conclusion:** A single QCIF frame requires **594 DCT operations** of size $8 \times 8$ — one of the canonical workload figures often asked in KTU theory questions.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — H.261 Tools & Their Effects

| Tool | Removes Redundancy | Domain | KTU Frequent Question? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Motion Estimation (ME)** | Temporal (inter-frame) | $16 \times 16$ MB in $\pm 15$ px window | Yes |
| **Motion Compensation (MC)** | Temporal — replaces MB with shifted ref | Encoder + Decoder | Yes |
| **DCT (8×8)** | Spatial (intra-frame) | $8 \times 8$ pixel block | Yes |
| **Quantization (MQUANT)** | Perceptual / Irrelevance | 64 DCT coefficients | Yes |
| **Zig-Zag Scan + VLC** | Statistical redundancy | Sequence of $(0,0,..,c_1,c_2,..)$ | Yes |
| **Loop Filter** | Blocking artifacts | Reconstructed MB boundary | Sometimes |
| **Bit-Rate Control (BRC)** | Buffer stability | Per-MB MQUANT adjustment | Sometimes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below follow the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards** — purely alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside double-quoted labels, no reserved-keyword node IDs.

### 4.1 H.261 Encoder Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA[Current Frame Input] --> meA[Motion Estimator]
    refA[Reconstructed Prev Frame] --> meA
    meA --> mvOutA[Motion Vector]
    mvOutA --> vlcA[VLC Encoder]
    inA --> subA[Subtractor]
    fsA[Frame Store] --> mcA[Motion Compensator]
    mvOutA --> mcA
    mcA --> subA
    subA --> dctA[DCT 8x8 Block]
    dctA --> qA[Quantizer MQUANT]
    qA --> vlcA
    vlcA --> outA[Compressed Bitstream]
    qA --> iqA[Inverse Quantizer]
    iqA --> idctA[Inverse DCT 8x8]
    idctA --> addA[Adder Predictor Add]
    mcA --> addA
    addA --> fsA
    brcA[Bit Rate Controller] --> qA
```

### 4.2 H.261 Decoder Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    inD[Compressed Bitstream] --> vldD[VLC Decoder]
    vldD --> iqD[Inverse Quantizer]
    vldD --> mvBufD[Motion Vector Buffer]
    vldD --> cdD[Coding Mode Decision]
    iqD --> idctD[Inverse DCT 8x8]
    idctD --> addD[Adder]
    fsD[Frame Store] --> mcD[Motion Compensator]
    mvBufD --> mcD
    mcD --> addD
    addD --> outD[Reconstructed Frame Output]
    addD --> fsD
```

### 4.3 H.261 Hierarchical Block Structure (Picture → GOB → MB → Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP[Picture 352x288 CIF or 176x144 QCIF] --> nodeG1[GOB 1 Top Third 11 or 22 MBs]
    nodeP --> nodeG2[GOB 2 Middle Third]
    nodeP --> nodeG3[GOB 3 Bottom Third]
    nodeG1 --> nodeM1[Macroblock 16x16 Luma]
    nodeM1 --> nodeY1[Block Y1 8x8 Luma]
    nodeM1 --> nodeY2[Block Y2 8x8 Luma]
    nodeM1 --> nodeY3[Block Y3 8x8 Luma]
    nodeM1 --> nodeY4[Block Y4 8x8 Luma]
    nodeM1 --> nodeCb[Block Cb 8x8 Chroma]
    nodeM1 --> nodeCr[Block Cr 8x8 Chroma]
```

### 4.4 H.261 Coding Pipeline — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Input Raw Video Frame] --> stepB{Frame Type Decision}
    stepB -->|Intra MB| stepC[Direct 8x8 DCT]
    stepB -->|Inter MB| stepD[Motion Estimation in Ref Frame]
    stepD --> stepE[Generate Prediction Block]
    stepE --> stepF[Subtract to Form Residual]
    stepF --> stepC
    stepC --> stepG[Quantize DCT Coeffs via MQUANT]
    stepG --> stepH[Zig Zag Scan Order]
    stepH --> stepI[2D VLC Encode RUN LEVEL]
    stepI --> stepJ[Emit MB Layer to Bitstream]
    stepG --> stepK[Reconstruct via Inverse Q and IDCT]
    stepK --> stepL[Add Prediction back]
    stepL --> stepM[Store Reconstructed MB to Frame Buffer]
    stepM --> stepN[Next MB or Next Frame]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing $p \times 64$ **bit/s** instead of **kbit/s** — KTU deducts 1 mark for unit error.
> - **Mistake 2:** Confusing **CIF ($352 \times 288$)** with **QCIF ($176 \times 144$)** in macroblock counts. The H.261 specification **mandates QCIF**; CIF is optional.
> - **Mistake 3:** Drawing the encoder without the **feedback loop** (Inverse Q → Inverse DCT → Adder → Frame Store). The loop is essential; without it, encoder and decoder drift, and H.261 explicitly forbids this.
> - **Mistake 4:** Forgetting the **sign-preservation step** in quantization (Formula #2) — sign of level must match sign of coefficient.
> - **Mistake 5:** Writing MQUANT range as $[1, 63]$ — the correct range is **$[1, 31]$**.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define H.261. List its key features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **H.261** is the ITU-T video compression standard finalized in **1993**, designed for **ISDN-based videotelephony and video conferencing** at bit rates of **$p \times 64$ kbit/s** ($p \in [1, 30]$).
>
> **Key Features:**
> 1. **Source formats:** **CIF ($352 \times 288$)** and **QCIF ($176 \times 144$)**; QCIF is mandatory.
> 2. **Hybrid coding:** combines **intra-frame DCT** and **inter-frame motion compensation**.
> 3. **Frame types:** **I-frames** (intra) and **P-frames** (predictive).
> 4. **Color subsampling:** **4:2:0** with 8-bit samples.
> 5. **Coding tools:** DCT ($8 \times 8$), scalar quantization (MQUANT), and **2-D Variable Length Coding**.
> 6. **Bit-rate control** via per-macroblock MQUANT adjustment.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Explain the role of motion compensation in H.261.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Motion Compensation (MC) exploits **temporal redundancy** between consecutive frames in a video sequence. For each 16×16 macroblock of the current frame, the encoder searches the **previously reconstructed frame** for the best-matching block using a **Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD)** criterion. The displacement is encoded as a **motion vector (MV)**.
>
> At the decoder, only the MV and the residual error are needed to reconstruct the block, drastically reducing the bits required compared to transmitting the raw block. MC is fundamental to achieving the low bit rates ($\leq 1.92$ Mbit/s) required by H.261 over ISDN.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice Provided

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **any ONE** of the two full questions (14 marks total = 7 + 7).

---

### **Question B — Option A** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### **(a) Describe the H.261 video compression standard. Discuss its frame types, source formats, and quantization scheme. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Origin & Purpose (1 mark):**
H.261 was standardized by **ITU-T (Recommendation H.261) in 1993**, originally named the **"p × 64 kbit/s"** standard, targeted at **ISDN videophone/video-conferencing** services.

**2. Source Formats (1 mark):**
Two resolutions are defined:
- **QCIF (Quarter CIF):** $176 \times 144$ — **mandatory** for all decoders.
- **CIF (Common Intermediate Format):** $352 \times 288$ — **optional**.
Both use **4:2:0 chroma subsampling** and are intended for frame rates up to ~30 fps.

**3. Frame Types (2 marks):**
- **I-frame (Intra):** Encoded using only DCT + Q + VLC on the original block; no temporal reference. Acts as **refresh point** to prevent drift.
- **P-frame (Predictive):** Encoded via **motion estimation + compensation** from the **previously reconstructed frame**, plus DCT of the residual. Each MB in a P-frame may still be coded as Intra if motion search is poor.

**4. Quantization Scheme (3 marks):**
- The **MQUANT** parameter (quantizer step scale) ranges from **1 to 31** and may vary per macroblock.
- For **inter MBs / AC intra coefficients**:
$$
\vert \text{level} \vert = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{\vert c \vert}{2 \cdot \text{MQUANT}}\right)
$$
- For the **DC coefficient of intra MBs**, a uniform step of **8** is used:
$$
\text{level}_{DC} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{\text{DC}}{8}\right)
$$
- A **dead-zone** is applied at reconstruction to reduce quantization noise:
$$
c' = 2 \cdot \text{MQUANT} \cdot \text{level} + \text{sign}(\text{level}) \cdot \text{MQUANT}
$$
- **Bit-rate control** dynamically adjusts MQUANT to prevent the output buffer from overflowing.

---

#### **(b) Draw and explain the H.261 encoder and decoder block diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. H.261 Encoder (3.5 marks):**
The encoder consists of the following functional blocks (refer to **Section 4.1 diagram**):

| Block | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Subtractor** | Computes the residual $e = I_{\text{cur}} - I_{\text{pred}}$. |
| **DCT (8×8)** | Transforms residual (or original) block into 64 frequency coefficients. |
| **Quantizer** | Quantizes coefficients using MQUANT. |
| **VLC Encoder** | Encodes $(RUN, LEVEL)$ pairs of zig-zag-scanned coefficients using 2-D Huffman table. |
| **Motion Estimator** | Searches reconstructed previous frame to produce motion vectors. |
| **Motion Compensator** | Forms the predicted block from previous frame + MV. |
| **Inverse Q + IDCT + Adder** | Reconstructs the MB and stores it in the **Frame Store** for future prediction. |
| **Bit-Rate Controller** | Adjusts MQUANT per MB to maintain constant output rate. |

**2. H.261 Decoder (3.5 marks):**
The decoder (refer to **Section 4.2 diagram**) is structurally a **subset** of the encoder's feedback loop:

1. **VLC Decoder** extracts quantized coefficients, motion vectors, and MB modes.
2. **Inverse Quantizer** reconstructs DCT coefficients.
3. **Inverse DCT** converts them back to spatial residuals.
4. **Motion Compensator** retrieves the predicted block from the **Frame Store** using the decoded MV.
5. **Adder** combines residual and prediction to form the **reconstructed block**.
6. Output is displayed and also **stored in the Frame Store** for decoding future frames.

> **Key insight:** The decoder's output frame store must be **bit-exact** to the encoder's local frame store. H.261 enforces this by mandating the encoder to perform **local decoding** in its feedback loop.

---

### **Question B — Option B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Explain the hierarchical block structure of H.261 with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

H.261 organizes each picture into a **4-level hierarchy** (refer to **Section 4.3 diagram**):

**Level 1 — Picture (1 mark):**
A single video frame, either **CIF ($352 \times 288$)** or **QCIF ($176 \times 144$)**, divided horizontally into **3 slices**.

**Level 2 — Group of Blocks (GOB) (2 marks):**
- QCIF: **3 GOBs** of size $176 \times 48$ (each GOB = 11 MBs arranged horizontally).
- CIF: **6 GOBs** of size $352 \times 48$ (each GOB = 22 MBs).
- Each GOB begins with a **GOB header** containing the **GOB number** and **quantizer information (GQUANT)**.

**Level 3 — Macroblock (MB) (2 marks):**
- Size: $16 \times 16$ luma pixels, corresponding to **$11 \times 9 = 99$ MBs** per QCIF frame.
- Comprises **6 DCT blocks**: 4 luma ($Y_1, Y_2, Y_3, Y_4$) + 1 Cb + 1 Cr.
- The **MB header** carries the **MB address**, **MB type** (Intra/Inter), and **MQUANT** override.

**Level 4 — Block (2 marks):**
- The atomic processing unit: $8 \times 8$ pixels.
- Subject to **DCT → Quantization → VLC** independently.

> **Numerical summary:**
$$
N_{MB,QCIF} = 11 \times 9 = 99; \quad N_{blocks,QCIF} = 6 \times 99 = 594
$$
$$
N_{MB,CIF} = 22 \times 18 = 396; \quad N_{blocks,CIF} = 6 \times 396 = 2376
$$

---

#### **(b) Explain motion estimation and compensation in H.261 with a numerical example. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Motion Estimation (3 marks):**
For each 16×16 macroblock of the current frame, the encoder searches the **previous reconstructed frame** within a search window (typically $\pm 15$ pixels) for the best-matching block. The cost function is the **Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD)**:

$$
\text{SAD}(dx, dy) = \sum_{x=0}^{15}\sum_{y=0}^{15} \;\vert\; I_{\text{cur}}(x, y) - I_{\text{ref}}(x+dx, y+dy)\;\vert
$$

The displacement vector $(dx^*, dy^*)$ that minimizes SAD is chosen as the **motion vector (MV)**:

$$
\text{MV} = \arg\min_{(dx, dy)} \text{SAD}(dx, dy)
$$

H.261 MV precision is **integer pixel** (in contrast to H.263's half-pixel).

**2. Motion Compensation (2 marks):**
The encoder forms the predicted block by **copying** the 16×16 region at position $(x + dx^*, y + dy^*)$ from the previous reconstructed frame. The residual is then:
$$
e(x, y) = I_{\text{cur}}(x, y) - I_{\text{pred}}(x, y)
$$
The decoder performs the **same MC** using the transmitted MV to reconstruct the prediction, then adds the decoded residual.

**3. Numerical Example (2 marks):**
Suppose for a macroblock the SAD values across 5 candidates are:

| $(dx, dy)$ | $(-2,-1)$ | $(-1,0)$ | $(0,0)$ | $(1,0)$ | $(2,1)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **SAD** | 2150 | 1480 | 1320 | **980** | 1760 |

The minimum SAD = **980** at $(1, 0)$, so:

$$
\text{MV} = (1, 0)
$$

The encoder transmits this MV (just a few bits using VLC) along with the residual. If SAD had been 0 at $(0,0)$, the MB could be **skipped**, saving even more bits.

> **Performance note:** With a $\pm 15$ pixel range, the encoder evaluates $31 \times 31 = 961$ candidates per MB. For a CIF frame at 15 fps, this means $\approx 5.7$ million SAD computations per second — driving the need for fast ME hardware.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for H.261 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Standard:** ITU-T H.261, finalized **1993**, originally **"p × 64 kbit/s"**.
- **Bit Rate Formula:** $\;R = p \times 64$ kbit/s, $\;p \in [1, 30]$ → range **64 kbit/s to 1.92 Mbit/s**.
- **Source Formats:** **QCIF ($176 \times 144$) mandatory**, CIF ($352 \times 288$) optional.
- **Frame Types:** **I-frames (Intra)** and **P-frames (Inter/Predictive)** only — no B-frames.
- **Color Subsampling:** **4:2:0** (Y : Cb : Cr = 4 : 2 : 2 per 4 luma samples).
- **Hierarchical Layers:** **Picture → GOB → Macroblock → Block**.
- **Macroblock:** $16 \times 16$ luma = **4 Y + 1 Cb + 1 Cr blocks** = 6 blocks per MB.
- **DCT Size:** **8 × 8**, applied to residual (inter) or original (intra).
- **Quantizer Parameter:** **MQUANT ∈ [1, 31]**, varies per MB for rate control.
- **Quantization Formula:** $\vert\text{level}\vert = \text{round}\!\big(\vert c \vert / (2 \cdot \text{MQUANT})\big)$.
- **DC Intra Quant Step:** uniform **step = 8**.
- **Inverse Quantization:** $c' = 2 \cdot \text{MQUANT} \cdot \text{level} + \text{sign}(\text{level}) \cdot \text{MQUANT}$ (dead-zone).
- **Motion Vector Precision:** **Integer pixel** (H.263 introduced half-pixel).
- **Search Window:** typically $\pm 15$ pixels around current MB.
- **Matching Cost:** **SAD** = $\sum \vert I_{\text{cur}} - I_{\text{ref}} \vert$.
- **Entropy Coding:** **2-D VLC** on zig-zag-scanned $(RUN, LEVEL)$ pairs.
- **Mandatory Encoder Feature:** **Local decoding loop** (IQ + IDCT + adder + frame store).
- **QCIF MB Count:** $11 \times 9 = 99$ MBs/frame = 594 DCT blocks/frame.
- **CIF MB Count:** $22 \times 18 = 396$ MBs/frame = 2376 DCT blocks/frame.
- **Successors/Descendants:** H.263, MPEG-1, MPEG-2, H.264/AVC — all built on the **same hybrid DPCM/DCT skeleton**.
- **Mandatory decoder requirement:** **QCIF support** — every H.261-compliant decoder MUST decode QCIF.
- **Loop Filter:** optional in H.261; used to suppress blocking artifacts at MB boundaries.
- **Bit-Rate Control:** implemented by varying **MQUANT per MB** based on output buffer fullness.
- **No B-frames, no half-pixel MV, no weighted prediction, no CABAC** — H.261 is the **simplest** of all ITU-T/MPEG hybrid codecs.
